title: RINKO aroft_o2/aroft_t glitch filter omitted — spurious DO/DO_T spikes survive into output (active on ARCTERX data)
evidence: ODAS odas_aroft_o2_internal (convert_odas.m:657-661) and odas_aroft_t_internal (convert_odas.m:675-679) apply a glitch filter for the 'buggy RS232 receiver': `for i=2:length(input), if input(i)<=50 || input(i)>=2^16-50, input(i)=input(i-1); end`. The Python ports convert_aroft_o2 (channels.py:262-265) and convert_aroft_t (channels.py:268-271) do `d=_unsigned_16bit(data); return d/100.0` / `d/1000.0-5.0` with NO glitch replacement. The RINKO sensor IS present in ARCTERX (channels DO=aroft_o2, DO_T=aroft_t in SN479_0002.p, 0003.p, ...). Demonstrated divergence: input [25000,10,25100,65500,25200] -> Python aroft_o2 = [250.0, 0.1, 251.0, 655.0, 252.0] while ODAS yields [250,250,251,251,252] (glitch samples 10 and 65500 replaced by the previous sample). test_channels.py never exercises any value <=50 or >=65486, so the gap is hidden by a test blind spot.
suggested_fix: Before scaling, apply the ODAS glitch filter to a copy: iterate i from 1..len-1 and set d[i]=d[i-1] where d[i]<=50 or d[i]>=2**16-50 (index 0 is never filtered, matching MATLAB i=2 start). A vectorized forward-fill equivalent: build a mask = (d<=50)|(d>=2**16-50) with mask[0]=False, then forward-fill the masked positions from the last good sample. Add a regression test feeding glitch samples through both converters.
verification:
VERIFIED — the finding holds and matters in practice. I tried to refute it on three fronts and each held up.

1) Code-level omission is real. ODAS `odas_aroft_o2_internal` (convert_odas.m:657-661) and `odas_aroft_t_internal` (convert_odas.m:675-679) both apply the documented "Temporary fix of a buggy RS232 receiver" glitch filter: `for i=2:length(input), if input(i)<=50 || input(i)>=2^16-50, input(i)=input(i-1); end`. The Python ports `convert_aroft_o2` (channels.py:262-265) and `convert_aroft_t` (channels.py:268-271) do only `d=_unsigned_16bit(data); return d/100.0` / `d/1000.0-5.0` with no glitch replacement. The omission is unambiguous.

2) The path is live on ARCTERX data. The converters are registered in CONVERTERS (channels.py:330-331), and RINKO is present: `grep aroft` matches every ARCTERX VMP/*.p file; PFile('...0002.p') exposes DO (aroft_o2) and DO_T (aroft_t) channels. Conversion flows through CONVERTERS (p_file.py:530) and the result is written straight to NetCDF (convert.py:81-82, 391-399) with no downstream glitch filter, despike, or masking — so the divergence reaches output.

3) Real glitch samples occur mid-record, where ODAS forward-fills and Python does not. Scanning all ARCTERX DO channels: files 0004/0006/0014 have 127 mid-record glitch samples each (no leading warm-up), and 0007/0010 have 127 mid-record glitches after a short warm-up run. Concrete case (file 0007, idx ~5065): DO reads a valid ~242.91 µmol/L, drops to raw count 0 for ~120 consecutive samples surrounded by valid ~243 readings, then recovers. Python writes 0.0 µmol/L (a physically impossible zero-DO spike); ODAS holds 242.91 µmol/L (last good value). This is exactly the spurious-spike divergence claimed.

Caveat on scope (slight overstatement in the finding's universality): in most files (0002, 0003, 0005, 0008, 0009, 0011, etc.) the glitch samples are confined to a leading sensor warm-up run at the very start of the record. There, ODAS leaves index 0 unfiltered and only forward-propagates the leading zero, so the warm-up region remains ~0 in both ODAS and Python — divergence is negligible for those files. The genuinely impactful, clearly-divergent cases are the mid-record dropouts in roughly a third of the files. The finding's claim of demonstrated divergence and "active on ARCTERX data" is nonetheless correct.

Test blind spot confirmed: test_channels.py (lines 468-533) only feeds values like 25000/10000/0/-1/200/-100. None land in the glitch range mid-array. Note `-1` wraps to 65535 (which is ≥65486, a glitch value) yet the test asserts a valid 655.35 output — directly confirming the Python code does NOT apply the filter.

Severity assessment: This is silent data corruption / ODAS port-infidelity affecting an auxiliary sensor (dissolved oxygen / DO temperature), not the core epsilon/chi shear and FP07 microstructure products that are the package's primary scientific outputs. The corrupted samples are localized spikes that any downstream consumer could re-filter, and DO is not used in the ε/χ pipeline. That argues against "major" (which I reserve for primary-product or widespread corruption) and for "minor": a genuine, demonstrable correctness/port-faithfulness defect on real data, but bounded in blast radius to a secondary channel.

title: Unsigned-wrap step runs AFTER deconvolution, reversing ODAS order (latent divergence for an unsigned/32-bit deconvolved channel)
evidence: PFile._read calls self._apply_deconvolution(...) at p_file.py:498, then performs the signed->unsigned wrap at p_file.py:503-523. ODAS does the opposite: read_odas.m wraps unsigned channels at lines 370-398 BEFORE odas_p2mat.m performs deconvolution (odas_p2mat.m:516-570 operates on the already-sign-corrected d.(channels{i})). For ARCTERX this is inert — the only deconvolved channels are T1/T1_dT1, T2/T2_dT2, P/P_dP, all signed 16-bit (therm/poly, no sign=unsigned), confirmed from the config. But if a future config marks a deconvolved channel sign=unsigned (or it is a 2-id 32-bit channel), the Python path would deconvolve raw signed int16 values whereas ODAS deconvolves the unsigned-corrected values, producing different hres.
suggested_fix: Move the unsigned-wrap loop (p_file.py:503-523) to run BEFORE _apply_deconvolution so the deconvolution sees the same sign-corrected data ODAS does, matching read_odas-then-odas_p2mat ordering. (The 32-bit 2-id join at lines 450-473 already produces unsigned float64 before deconvolution, so only the int16 sign=unsigned case needs reordering.)
verification: VERIFIED REAL but latent. The ordering claim is factually correct against both codebases. (1) ODAS order: odas_p2mat.m:372 calls read_odas(), which performs the unsigned/sign correction at read_odas.m:370-398 (`ch.(name)(ch.(name)<0)+=2^16` for sign=unsigned and jac_t); deconvolution happens LATER in odas_p2mat.m:516-570, where deconvolve() at line 566 receives `d.(channels{i})` — the already-sign-corrected array. (2) Python order is reversed: p_file.py:498 runs `self._apply_deconvolution(...)` FIRST, then the unsigned-wrap loop at p_file.py:503-523 runs on `channels_raw` afterward. So Python deconvolves raw signed int16 while ODAS deconvolves the +2^16-wrapped values. I confirmed numerically that this matters: a synthetic deconvolve() run on signed vs. pre-wrapped int16 inputs gave a max-abs hres difference of ~34731 counts, so the divergence is real for any channel that is BOTH deconvolved (has diff_gain, non-shear) AND sign=unsigned (16-bit). The finding correctly scopes out the 32-bit 2-id case: p_file.py:463-473 wraps both words to unsigned float64 BEFORE deconvolution, matching ODAS for that path. Impact on ARCTERX is genuinely inert and this is verified, not merely asserted: the SN479 config's deconvolved channels are T1_dT1/T2_dT2 (therm) and P_dP (poly), all sign=None (signed); the unsigned channels (JAC_T jac_t, Chlorophyll/Turbidity poly sign=unsigned) carry no diff_gain, so the deconvolved-and-unsigned intersection is empty. The MATLAB validation suite (tests/test_matlab_all_files.py) exact-matches P, T1, T2, T1_dT1, T2_dT2 against the ODAS reference at rtol=1e-10 and passes, confirming no current divergence. Also note convert_poly (the converter for the unsigned Chlorophyll/Turbidity) does NOT call _unsigned_16bit, so the upstream wrap loop is the sole sign-correction site for unsigned poly channels — meaning the converter would NOT mask the bug for the hypothetical deconvolved-unsigned-poly case; the divergence would propagate. The proposed fix (move the wrap loop before _apply_deconvolution) is correct and restores ODAS ordering. The defect is genuine and well-evidenced, but it has no reachable trigger in the current code/data (purely a future-config / port-faithfulness latent issue with zero present numerical impact), so 'minor' overstates it; 'nit' is the accurate severity.

title: _load_from_nc leaks NetCDF _FillValue (9.97e36) into pressure and channels via masked-array .data
evidence: Every read in _load_from_nc uses `ds.variables[...][:].data`. netCDF4 returns a *masked* array when a variable has a _FillValue or has unwritten elements (default fill ~9.96921e36). `.data` exposes the raw fill buffer, NOT NaN. Verified end-to-end: an NC with one unwritten slow-P element makes `_load_from_nc(...)['P']` return `[10.0, 9.96920997e+36]` instead of `[10.0, nan]`. That 9.97e36 pressure value then drives `_smooth_fall_rate`/`get_profiles`, producing a colossal spurious fall-rate spike and silently corrupting profile detection (and every channel read at line 427). The package's own *fully-written* NCs are not masked, so the happy path is unaffected — but any partially-written/interrupted NC, or any NC carrying an explicit _FillValue, triggers corruption with no error. No test in tests/test_rsi_profile.py covers masked/fill input.
suggested_fix: Replace `[:].data` with fill-aware extraction, e.g. `np.ma.filled(ds.variables[name][:].astype('f8'), np.nan)` (or call `ds.set_auto_mask(True)` then `.filled(np.nan)`), so masked/fill entries become NaN rather than 9.97e36. Apply to t_fast/t_slow (362-368), P (393/397/399), and the channel scan (427). Add a regression test that writes an NC with an unwritten/fill element and asserts the result is NaN, not >1e30.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL after attempting to refute. All claims hold under direct testing.

MECHANISM (profile.py:362-368, 393, 397, 399, 427): Every read uses `ds.variables[...][:].data`. netCDF4 returns a MaskedArray, and `.data` exposes the raw fill buffer. Confirmed directly: a variable with a masked/unwritten element yields `.data` = `[10.0, 9.96921e+36]` while `np.ma.filled(..., np.nan)` correctly yields `[10.0, nan]`.

END-TO-END LEAK: Running the actual `_load_from_nc('/tmp/masked_test.nc')` returns `P = [10.0, 9.96920997e+36]`, exactly as the finding claims. The ATOMIX `L1_converted` group path (lines 397/399) is equally affected — a CF-style declared `_FillValue` coinciding with a real element leaks `9.97e36` into P as well.

DOWNSTREAM CORRUPTION (the load-bearing claim): Built a realistic descent (5s surface rest, 70s descent at ~0.7 dbar/s, 5s bottom rest) at fs=64Hz. Clean baseline: `get_profiles` returns one profile `(321, 4802)`. Injecting a single leaked fill value (9.96920997e+36) mid-descent makes `_smooth_fall_rate` produce a 4.47e35 dbar/s spike at that index, and the detected profile truncates to `(321, 2560)` — silently dropping the entire second half of the cast, with NO error raised. The 9.97e36 value also passes the `P_min < P` validity mask in get_profiles (scor160/profile.py:210), and `profile_P_start/end` and `profile_mean_speed` metadata would be corrupted if a fill lands at a boundary. Channel reads at line 427 would carry 9.97e36 into every downstream epsilon/chi computation.

HAPPY PATH SAFE (verified, supports the finding's own scoping): convert.py:137-138 calls `createVariable(...)` without `_FillValue` and writes `v[:] = data` fully, so the package's own NCs return MaskedArrays with no masked elements and `.data` is clean. So this never triggers on in-house pipeline data — it requires a partially-written/interrupted NC or an externally-produced NC carrying _FillValue (CF convention / ATOMIX tool output). `_load_from_nc` explicitly supports reading both root-format and ATOMIX L1_converted external NCs as intended input paths, so the exposure is realistic, not purely theoretical.

NO TEST COVERAGE: grep of tests/test_rsi_profile.py for mask/fill/9.96/9.97/1e30/1e36/isnan/filled returns nothing — confirmed uncovered.

Severity assessment: This is genuine silent data corruption (truncated/lost profiles, fill values leaking into computed quantities) with no exception and plausible-looking output — the worst kind of data-quality bug. It does NOT affect the package's own happy-path data and requires a non-default input condition (external/partial NC), which keeps it below blocker. The proposed fix (np.ma.filled(..., np.nan) on each read) is correct and minimal. 'major' is appropriate.

title: convert_all per-file isolation defeated by too-narrow exception allowlist
evidence: Both the serial (605) and parallel (616) loops catch only `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)`. The clear intent is per-file isolation: one bad .p file should log an error and let the batch continue. But several realistic failures raise other classes — e.g. `IndexError` from the single-sample GRADT path (convert.py:514-515 `dTdt[-1]`), `KeyError` from a missing expected channel, or a struct/ndarray error. In the parallel branch these escape `future.result()`, propagate out of the `as_completed` loop, exit the `with ProcessPoolExecutor` block, and abandon all remaining files; in serial they abort the loop. So one anomalous file kills the whole batch instead of being skipped.
suggested_fix: Broaden the caught type to `Exception` (still logging `p_path.name: {e}` and continuing) in both loops, or explicitly add `IndexError, KeyError, struct.error` to the tuple. Catching `Exception` best matches the documented batch-tolerance intent.
verification:
Verified against the actual code. convert.py:605 (serial) and :616 (parallel) both catch only `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)`, and the surrounding code (logging `{p_path.name}: {e}` then continuing) makes the per-file isolation intent explicit.

The exception-class claims hold. I confirmed `IndexError`, `KeyError`, and `struct.error` are NOT subclasses of `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)` (all `issubclass(...)` checks returned False). The concrete trigger at convert.py:514-515 is real: for a fast channel with a single sample, `np.diff(T_fast_ch)` returns an empty array and `np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1])` raises `IndexError: index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0` (reproduced directly). This occurs inside p_to_L1, which is called by _convert_one in both loops, so it is not caught.

I also reproduced the parallel-branch escape with an importable worker under a `__main__` guard: an `IndexError` raised by `future.result()` is not caught by the `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)` handler, propagates out of the `as_completed` loop, exits the `with ProcessPoolExecutor` block, and abandons the remaining files (escaped=True, zero other files completed). The serial branch likewise aborts the `for` loop. So one anomalous file kills the batch instead of being skipped — exactly as claimed. There is no broader wrapper handling these elsewhere.

The KeyError/struct.error parts of the claim are weaker (supplementary names are sourced from pf.channels via _classify_channels, so pf.channel_info[name] should exist), but the IndexError path alone substantiates the finding, and the class of uncaught failures is genuine.

Severity minor is appropriate: triggering requires an anomalous/truncated/degenerate .p file (a realistic but not routine condition in field acquisitions), yet when it occurs it defeats the documented batch-tolerance design and, in the parallel path, abandons all remaining files. The proposed fix (broaden to `Exception` while still logging and continuing, or explicitly add IndexError/KeyError/struct.error) is correct and matches the intent.

title: GRADT computation raises IndexError on a 1-sample fast thermistor channel
evidence: `dTdt = np.diff(T_fast_ch) * pf.fs_fast; dTdt = np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1])`. For a fast channel of length 1, `np.diff` returns an empty array and `dTdt[-1]` raises `IndexError: index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0` (verified). This occurs when `matrix_count*n_rows == 1`. Combined with the narrow except-allowlist above, this aborts conversion of the file (and, in parallel mode, the batch) rather than degrading gracefully.
suggested_fix: Guard the degenerate length: `if T_fast_ch.size < 2: gradt_arrays.append(np.zeros_like(T_fast_ch)); continue` before computing np.diff, or skip the GRADT variable when there are <2 fast samples.
verification:
The mechanical claim is verified. At src/odas_tpw/rsi/convert.py:514-515, `dTdt = np.diff(T_fast_ch) * pf.fs_fast; dTdt = np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1])`. I reproduced that for a length-1 array, `np.diff` returns an empty array and `dTdt[-1]` raises `IndexError: index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0`. I also confirmed `IndexError` is NOT a subclass of `(OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError)` (verified via issubclass → False), and those are exactly the exception tuples caught by the `convert_all` handlers at convert.py:605 and :616. So an IndexError here propagates uncaught and aborts conversion (and, in parallel mode, escapes `future.result()` the same way), matching the evidence.

The block is guarded by `if gradt_names:` and iterates fast thermistor channels; it does not guard the degenerate length-1 case, while the proposed fix (skip/zero-fill when size < 2) is sound.

The important caveat is reachability, which the finding slightly overstates. A fast channel that spans the full address-matrix column has length `matrix_count * n_rows` (p_file.py:440-475). Length 1 requires BOTH `matrix_count == 1` (a single scan) AND `n_rows == 1`. `n_rows == 1` is a degenerate matrix where `fs_slow == fs_fast` (p_file.py:323) — not a configuration any real Rockland microstructure instrument uses (the documented VMP-250 has 8 rows; the address-matrix design exists precisely to sample fast channels multiple times per slow scan). So the crash is only reachable on a crafted/corrupted/synthetic `.p` file that still passes the `n_records >= 1` check, never on genuine instrument output. speed_fast computation earlier in p_to_L1 uses np.interp on length-1 vectors (works) plus a `_safe_filtfilt` guard, so it would not necessarily fail first; line 514 is plausibly the first crash point on such a file.

Verdict: a genuine but very-low-impact robustness gap that triggers only on structurally degenerate/malformed files, never on real data. Defect holds, so real=true, but severity is better calibrated as nit than minor.

title: PFile reshape raises opaque ValueError when data_words is not a positive multiple of n_cols
evidence: `scans_per_record = data_words // self.n_cols; total_scans = n_records * scans_per_record; raw_flat = records['data'].reshape(total_scans, self.n_cols)`. `records['data']` has shape `(n_records, data_words)` = `n_records*data_words` elements, but the reshape target is `total_scans*n_cols = n_records*(data_words//n_cols)*n_cols`. When `data_words % n_cols != 0`, or when `data_words < n_cols` (so `scans_per_record == 0`), these differ and numpy raises a cryptic `cannot reshape array of size N into shape (M,n_cols)`. Verified by forcing record_size=130 (data_words=1, n_cols=10): `PFile FAILED: ValueError cannot reshape array of size 5 into shape (0,10)`. ODAS read_odas.m sidesteps this via `matrix_count = floor(n_records*data_size/(n_rows*n_cols))` on a flat read. Real RSI files have data_words % n_cols == 0, so this only bites malformed/non-standard configs, but the failure mode is an unhelpful numpy error rather than a clear diagnostic.
suggested_fix: Before the reshape, validate `data_words` and emit a clear error: `if self.n_cols <= 0 or data_words % self.n_cols != 0 or data_words // self.n_cols == 0: raise ValueError(f"{self.filepath.name}: record payload {data_words} words is not a positive multiple of n_cols={self.n_cols}")`. Optionally follow ODAS and floor to whole scans.
verification:
The technical claim is literally true and I reproduced both failure modes. At /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:377-379, `scans_per_record = data_words // self.n_cols; total_scans = n_records * scans_per_record; raw_flat = records['data'].reshape(total_scans, self.n_cols)`. Since `records['data']` has `n_records*data_words` elements but the reshape target has `n_records*(data_words//n_cols)*n_cols` elements, the two differ whenever `data_words % n_cols != 0` (or `data_words < n_cols`, giving `scans_per_record==0`). Forcing the header `record_size` word (index 18) to 150 (data_words=11, n_cols=10) on a real VMP file produced `ValueError: cannot reshape array of size 54461056 into shape (5444398,10)`; forcing record_size=130 (data_words=1) reproduced the `(0,10)`-style failure too. There is no guard in `_read()` before the reshape, and notably `_read` lacks the `record_size <= 0` / `record_size < header_size` validations that the sibling `extract_pfile_segment` helper has at lines 142-148, so this code path is genuinely less defensive.

However, the practical impact is very low and one of the auditor's supporting claims is overstated. (1) Trigger conditions: I confirmed real RSI files have data_words an exact multiple of n_rows*n_cols (5120 = 64*80), and a realistically truncated file (interrupted download/DAQ, header intact) loads fine because n_records is floored and the trailing partial record is dropped (lines 349-359). The reshape only blows up when the header's own record_size/header_size/fast_cols/slow_cols words are mutually inconsistent, i.e. a corrupted or forged header — the auditor concedes this only bites malformed configs. (2) The "ODAS read_odas.m sidesteps this" claim is misleading: the floor at read_odas.m:195 only sizes the time vectors (matrix_count), while the actual data extraction at read_odas.m:330 (`reshape(fileData, numel(ch_matrix), [])`, numel = n_rows*n_cols) would throw an equivalently opaque MATLAB reshape error on the same malformed header — the reference does not handle this case more gracefully. (3) The existing behavior is already a deterministic ValueError that names the mismatched shapes; it is not silent data corruption, not a crash without traceback, not a wrong scientific result, and has no security impact. It is purely a diagnostic-message-quality issue on an off-the-happy-path edge case (corrupted header). The proposed fix is a reasonable small robustness improvement but addresses cosmetic error clarity, not a functional defect. Downgrading from minor to nit.

title: matrix_count == 0 produces empty channels and empty time vectors with no diagnostic
evidence: When `data_words < n_cols`, `scans_per_record` and therefore `total_scans`/`matrix_count` become 0, yielding empty `t_fast`/`t_slow` (lines 475-476) and empty channel arrays. Nothing rejects this. Downstream consumers then fail opaquely: `summary()` does `self.t_fast[-1]` (line 629 -> IndexError), and convert.py:472 `np.interp(pf.t_fast, pf.t_slow, P_slow)` raises `array of sample points is empty`. ODAS rejects no-data files explicitly (read_odas.m:188 `if n_records <= 1 -> error('contains no data')`). The Python loader only checks `n_records < 1` (line 350), not whether any *scans* result.
suggested_fix: After computing `total_scans`/`matrix_count`, add `if matrix_count < 1: raise ValueError(f"{self.filepath.name}: file yields no complete scans (matrix_count=0)")`, mirroring ODAS's no-data guard.
verification:
The finding's mechanics are factually correct after reading the cited code. In PFile._read (src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:377-378): scans_per_record = data_words // self.n_cols; total_scans = n_records * scans_per_record; matrix_count = total_scans // self.n_rows (line 395). If data_words < n_cols (i.e. record_size - header_size < 2*n_cols), scans_per_record == 0 -> total_scans == 0 -> matrix_count == 0. Lines 475-476 then build empty time vectors (np.arange(0)), and every channel slice (e.g. lines 440, 443-446, 463-470) yields empty arrays. Nothing rejects this state. The only guard in _read is n_records < 1 at line 350; unlike the sibling extract_pfile_segment (lines 142-148) which validates record_size <= 0 and record_size < header_size, _read performs no record_size/header_size sanity check.

Downstream-failure claims verified: summary() at line 629 does self.t_fast[-1], which raises IndexError on an empty array; and I confirmed by running numpy that convert.py:472 np.interp(pf.t_fast, pf.t_slow, P_slow) raises "ValueError: array of sample points is empty" when t_slow is empty. So the failure is a confusing crash, not silent corruption.

Caveat that tempers (but does not refute) the finding: the ODAS comparison is partly overstated. read_odas.m:188 (if n_records <= 1 -> error 'contains no data') is a RECORD-count guard, the exact analog of the Python n_records < 1 check that already exists. ODAS computes matrix_count = floor(n_records*data_size/(n_rows*n_cols)) (read_odas.m:195) and would itself produce matrix_count==0 / empty t_slow/t_fast for a record too small to hold one scan matrix; it has no scan-count guard either. So the proposed fix is actually stricter than ODAS, not "mirroring" it.

Practical impact: this is only reachable with a structurally malformed/truncated/corrupt header (record body smaller than a single n_rows x n_cols scan matrix); it cannot occur for any valid RSI P-file, where each record holds many scans. It is a legitimate defensive-validation gap of the same class the surrounding code already guards against (partial-record warning at 352, extract_pfile_segment validations at 142-148), and it degrades to an opaque crash rather than a clear message. Real but low impact.

title: Profile detection uses raw P while extracted/L1 pressure uses deconvolved P_dP (inconsistent source)
evidence: `_load_from_pfile` returns `"P": pf.channels["P"]` (raw, un-deconvolved slow pressure) for fall-rate/profile detection, whereas convert.py:39 and convert.py:471 deliberately prefer the deconvolved `pf.channels.get("P_dP", ...)`. So when extracting profiles directly from a PFile, profile bounds and `profile_mean_speed` are computed from the noisier raw P, while the higher-precision P_dP is what gets written elsewhere. Both are slow-rate so bounds are nearly identical, making this low-impact, but it is an avoidable inconsistency in which pressure signal is authoritative.
suggested_fix: Mirror convert.py: `"P": pf.channels.get("P_dP", pf.channels["P"])` in _load_from_pfile so profile detection uses the same (deconvolved) pressure as the rest of the pipeline.
verification:
Verified the finding against the actual code. At src/odas_tpw/rsi/profile.py:316, `_load_from_pfile` returns `"P": pf.channels["P"]` — the raw, un-deconvolved slow pressure. This `data["P"]` flows into profile detection: line 115 (P_slow = data["P"]), line 122 (`_smooth_fall_rate(P_slow, ...)`), line 125 (`get_profiles(P_slow, W, ...)`), profile_P_start/end at lines 156-157, and profile_mean_speed's W at line 159.

Every other pressure consumer in the rsi pipeline deliberately prefers the deconvolved P_dP: convert.py:39 and convert.py:471 (both `pf.channels.get("P_dP", pf.channels.get("P"))`), adapter.py:67, pipeline.py:183, and notably profile.py:39 itself (`_compute_speed` uses P_dP). p_file.py:273 documents P_dP as the deconvolved slow pressure (ODAS's P_slow), i.e. the higher-fidelity signal. So the inconsistency claimed is structurally real and the cited convert.py references are accurate.

Impact is genuinely low, as the finding states: P and P_dP are both slow-rate on the same time axis, and deconvolution of a slow pressure channel changes the signal only marginally, so profile bounds are nearly identical. One concrete (still tiny) consequence the finding undersells: `profile_mean_speed` written into the profile NetCDF derives from raw-P-smoothed W, whereas the speed actually used downstream in dissipation (_compute_speed, profile.py:39) is computed from P_dP — so the same module computes "speed" from two different pressure sources. The raw P channel is also written into the output anyway via the channels loop, so P_dP is not lost.

This is an avoidable, real internal inconsistency with negligible numerical effect — a code-hygiene/consistency issue, not a correctness bug. The proposed fix (`pf.channels.get("P_dP", pf.channels["P"])`) correctly mirrors the established pattern. Severity "nit" is appropriate.

title: _load_from_nc days->seconds conversion indexes t_fast[0] without an empty-array guard
evidence: When time units are days, `t_fast = (t_fast - t_fast[0]) * 86400.0` (and same for t_slow). If a NetCDF has a zero-length TIME dimension (e.g. an empty/0-sample profile file), `t_fast[0]` raises `IndexError: index 0 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0` (verified) before any clearer error is produced. The seconds path doesn't index element 0, so only the days path has this trap.
suggested_fix: Guard before subtracting the origin: `if t_fast.size: t_fast = (t_fast - t_fast[0]) * 86400.0` (and the same for t_slow), or raise an explicit 'NetCDF has no time samples' error earlier.
verification:
Verified against src/odas_tpw/rsi/profile.py:436-441. In the days-to-seconds branch, the code runs `t_fast = (t_fast - t_fast[0]) * 86400.0` and the same for t_slow, indexing element 0 unconditionally. I reproduced that on a zero-length numpy array this raises `IndexError: index 0 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0`. The seconds path (units not containing "day") never indexes element 0, so only the days path has this trap, as claimed.

Tracing the function: t_fast is read at line 367-368 (`g.variables["TIME"][:].data`), and nothing between that read and line 438 validates non-emptiness or indexes element 0, so for an empty-TIME ATOMIX-format file the line-438 IndexError would indeed be the first/only failure — confirming the evidence.

The defect genuinely holds, but its scope is narrow: it only fires for L1_converted ATOMIX-format full-record NetCDF input whose time units contain "day" AND whose TIME dimension has zero length. That is a malformed/degenerate input this codebase does not itself produce; the result is a slightly cryptic IndexError instead of a clear "no time samples" error, on input that is already broken. No data corruption, no failure in normal operation. That matches the auditor's own "nit" severity — a real but very-low-impact defensive edge-case gap.

title: edit_config_text trusts unvalidated EditSpec values, allowing stanza injection that defeats the documented [root]/[matrix] unaddressability guarantee
evidence:
All value sanitization (no-newline, no-';', ASCII, no control chars) lives exclusively in load_edit_spec via _check_value/_check_keymap (lines 111-153). edit_config_text and the EditSpec dataclass perform NO validation. EditSpec is a public (non-underscore) dataclass constructed directly throughout the test suite. Building one from any non-YAML source and calling edit_config_text bypasses every guard. Verified: EditSpec(sections={'instrument_info': {'vehicle': 'rvmp
[root]
rate = 1'}}) -> parse_config(active)['root']['rate'] == '1'. This directly violates the module docstring's hard guarantee (lines 18-20: '[matrix]/[root] ... are unreachable by design — they cannot be addressed through the edit spec, so they can never be corrupted') and the comment at lines 130-132. The same gap exists for note: EditSpec(note='oops
[root]
rate = 7', ...) injects rate=7 because the provenance comment at lines 356-357 interpolates spec.note RAW (unlike the banner, which is sanitized via _one_line at 448/455). The CLI path is safe (it only reaches edit_config_text through load_edit_spec), but the invariant is presented as structural, not CLI-only.
suggested_fix: Enforce validation at the apply boundary, not just the YAML boundary. Add a validating __post_init__ to EditSpec (or a validate() called at the top of edit_config_text) that runs _check_value over every section/channel value and over note/author. Additionally sanitize the provenance comment line: pass ch.old/ch.new and spec.note through _one_line() in provenance() (lines 350-357) the same way _build_banner already does, so even a future bypass cannot spill past the '; ' prefix.
verification:
I empirically confirmed both claimed exploits against the actual code in /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/rsi/config_patch.py.

(1) Section-value injection: EditSpec(note='test', author='a', sections={'instrument_info': {'vehicle': 'rvmp\r\n[root]\r\nrate = 1'}}) passed to edit_config_text produces active config where the injected '[root]' / 'rate = 1' lines land outside any comment. parse_config() of the result yields root == {'rate': '1', 'recsize': '256', 'vehicle': 'rvmp'} — i.e. the [root] 'rate' acquisition parameter was overwritten, defeating the unaddressability claim. The value is written verbatim by _rewrite_value (line 293) with no validation.

(2) note injection: EditSpec(note='oops\r\n[root]\r\nrate = 7', ...) reaches provenance() at lines 356-357, which interpolates spec.note RAW into a '; ' comment line. The embedded newlines break out of the comment and parse_config() yields root rate == '7)'. This is asymmetric: _build_banner DOES sanitize note via _one_line (line 455), but provenance() does not — a clear, unintended inconsistency the author would want fixed.

The defect genuinely holds: all value sanitization (_check_value/_check_keymap, lines 111-153) lives only in load_edit_spec; edit_config_text and the EditSpec dataclass perform no validation, yet the module docstring (lines 18-20) asserts the [matrix]/[root] guarantee structurally ('unreachable by design ... can never be corrupted') and the EditSpec docstring (line 89) calls it 'A parsed, validated set'. The comment at lines 130-132 explicitly ties newline rejection to that guarantee — but only in the load_edit_spec path. The proposed fixes (validating __post_init__ on EditSpec / validate() at top of edit_config_text, and routing ch.old/ch.new/spec.note through _one_line in provenance()) are correct and cheap.

However, the 'major' security severity is overstated. I verified that the ONLY production caller of edit_config_text is the CLI (cli.py:281-282), which always goes through load_edit_spec and is fully validated. EditSpec is not re-exported in any __init__.py and has no __all__; the only non-validated constructors are in the test suite. No untrusted-input deserialization path (JSON/API/web) builds an EditSpec. To reach the exploit an attacker must already be executing arbitrary Python and hand-building an EditSpec, at which point they could call parse_config/write_patched_pfile or write raw bytes directly — no real trust boundary is crossed and there is no privilege escalation. So this is a robustness / defense-in-depth + doc-vs-code-invariant-consistency gap (and a real provenance-sanitization inconsistency), not an exploitable security vulnerability in the current codebase. That places it at minor, not major.

title: write_patched_pfile raises uncaught struct.error on a corrupt header_size (< 24 bytes)
evidence: write_patched_pfile reads header_region = f.read(header_size) then struct.pack_into(..., header_region, _H['config_size']*2 == 22, ...). If a corrupt source advertises header_size < 24, pack_into raises struct.error ('requires a buffer of at least 24 bytes'). struct.error is NOT a subclass of OSError or ValueError (verified: struct.error.__mro__ = [error, Exception, ...]), and _cmd_patch_config catches only (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError) at cli.py:291, so the user gets a raw traceback instead of 'Error: ...' + exit(1). Unlike extract_pfile_segment (p_file.py:138-148) which validates header_size >= HEADER_BYTES, the writer/reader here perform no header_size sanity check.
suggested_fix: Validate header_size in read_config_text and write_patched_pfile (e.g. require HEADER_BYTES <= header_size and config_size consistent with file size) and raise ValueError with an actionable message, mirroring extract_pfile_segment. Alternatively add struct.error to the CLI's caught-exception tuple at cli.py:291.
verification:
Verified the finding holds end-to-end. In src/odas_tpw/rsi/config_patch.py, write_patched_pfile reads header_region = bytearray(f.read(header_size)) (line 527) then struct.pack_into(f"{endian}H", header_region, _H["config_size"] * 2, ...) (line 528). _H["config_size"] = 11 (p_file.py:41), so the write offset is 22 and pack_into needs a buffer >= 24 bytes. If header_size < 24, the bytearray is shorter and pack_into raises struct.error.

The only guard between reading the header and the pack (lines 521-525) checks first_record_size = header_size + config_size >= file_size; this does NOT bound header_size below 24, so a corrupt small header_size (e.g. 16) in a sufficiently large file passes straight through. read_config_text (lines 473-485), which runs first in patch_files (line 638), also performs no header_size validation — it only seeks/reads, so it succeeds and the flow reaches the writer.

I confirmed struct.error.__mro__ = [error, Exception, BaseException, object]; issubclass(struct.error, OSError) is False and issubclass(struct.error, ValueError) is False. The CLI handler _cmd_patch_config catches only (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError) at cli.py:291, so struct.error escapes as a raw traceback instead of "Error: ..." + exit(1).

Empirical reproduction: (1) unit-level write_patched_pfile on a fabricated v6 header with header_size=16 raised "struct.error -> pack_into requires a buffer of at least 24 bytes ... (actual buffer size is 16)"; (2) full patch_files() on a copy of a real VMP file (VMP/ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0002.p) with only header_size corrupted to 16 (endian-consistently re-packed) and a real editable section raised the same struct.error, and isinstance(e, (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError)) was False — confirming it bypasses the CLI handler.

The contrast claim is also accurate: extract_pfile_segment (p_file.py:138-139) validates `if header_size < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(...)`, while the patch path does not, so this is a real inconsistency with the project's own validation pattern.

This is a robustness/error-handling defect, not a security/correctness bug: it only triggers on already-malformed input and yields an ugly traceback rather than data corruption. Minor severity is correct (it sits on the minor/nit boundary because it requires a corrupt file, but the CLI explicitly tries to present clean errors and the codebase already guards this exact case elsewhere).

title: write_patched_pfile silently produces a corrupt copy when header_size is wrong-but-plausible (24..127)
evidence: The writer never validates header_size against HEADER_BYTES (128). With header_size between 24 and 127 it writes header_region = f.read(header_size) (a truncated header), then copies data from first_record_size = header_size + config_size — an offset that falls INSIDE the real 128-byte header. Verified: a source with header_size=64 (real header 128) yields a 'successful' output whose header word reads back 64 and whose body is silently mis-sliced, with no exception. This is the silent-data-corruption sibling of the struct.error case: garbage-in produces garbage-out with no warning. Requires an already-corrupt source (real RSI files have header_size=128), so impact is bounded.
suggested_fix: Add header_size >= HEADER_BYTES validation (and that header_size+config_size is consistent) before copying, raising ValueError as extract_pfile_segment does. A single shared _read_and_validate_header helper used by read_config_text, write_patched_pfile, and extract_pfile_segment would remove this divergence.
verification:
Verified the defect holds, including by empirical reproduction. write_patched_pfile (src/odas_tpw/rsi/config_patch.py:488-542) never validates header_size against HEADER_BYTES (128). Its only header-geometry guard is line 521 (`first_record_size >= file_size`), which catches truncation but not a wrong-but-plausible header_size. I built a synthetic source with header_size=64 (config_size=19) plus fake data and called write_patched_pfile: it returned successfully with NO exception, wrote a 550-byte output, and the output's header_size word read back 64. With header_size=64, line 527 copies only the first 64 bytes as header_region, and line 531 seeks to first_record_size=83 — an offset that falls INSIDE the real 128-byte header — so the body is silently mis-sliced. This is silent garbage-in/garbage-out corruption.

The validation-divergence claim is accurate: extract_pfile_segment validates this exact condition at src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:138 (`if header_size < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(...)`), while both write_patched_pfile and read_config_text (config_patch.py:482-485, which just does f.seek(header_size); f.read(config_size)) do not. In the real CLI flow (apply path, config_patch.py:638 then 657) read_config_text runs first and would also read config from the wrong offset without raising, so the missing header_size check is a genuine shared gap.

Minor imprecisions in the finding, none material: extract_pfile_segment lives in p_file.py not config_patch.py; line 527 reads into a bytearray rather than the f.read(header_size) shown. The finding is correctly scoped — it requires an already-corrupt source; I confirmed a real VMP file (ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0019.p) has header_size=128, so normal operation is unaffected and no real data is at risk. Impact is bounded to producing a corrupt output from a corrupt input with no warning or crash, which is a robustness/data-integrity gap rather than a correctness bug on valid data. Severity minor is appropriate.

title: scaffold_yaml interpolates file-derived values into the YAML template without escaping
evidence: scaffold_yaml builds the template with raw f-strings, e.g. f'  vehicle: "{ii.get("vehicle", "")}"' (line 573) and f'#     {key}: "{ch[key]}"' (line 595). A config value containing a double-quote produces malformed YAML; verified a value like 'VMP" + evil: "x' renders an unparseable line and ruamel raises ScannerError on load. The source is the user's own .p config (low trust risk) and the template is meant to be hand-edited, so worst case is a template that fails to load with a clear YAML error rather than silent corruption. Real RSI configs do not contain quotes in these fields.
suggested_fix: Use the YAML emitter to write scalar values (e.g. ruamel's DoubleQuotedScalarString or yaml.dump for the value) instead of f-string interpolation, or at minimum skip/escape values containing '"', so a quirky config value cannot produce an unloadable template.
verification:
I read config_patch.py:550-597 (scaffold_yaml) and load_edit_spec (lines 156-184), and tested the behavior empirically.

CONFIRMED (partial): Line 573 emits an ACTIVE, uncommented YAML line: f'  vehicle: "{ii.get("vehicle", "")}"   # ...'. I reproduced that a vehicle value containing a double-quote (e.g. 'VMP" + evil: "x') makes ruamel (yaml.load in load_edit_spec, ruamel YAML typ="rt") raise ScannerError: "mapping values are not allowed here". So the active vehicle line genuinely can produce an unloadable template. This is a real robustness nit.

OVERSTATED: The finding also cites lines 583 and 595 as defective, but both are emitted with a leading '#': cruise_info uses f'#   {key}: "{val}"' (583) and channels uses f'#     {key}: "{ch[key]}"' (595). These are YAML comments. My test confirms a quote inside a commented line loads cleanly (ruamel ignores comments). So 2 of the 3 cited lines do NOT actually hold the defect — only line 573 does.

IMPACT (very low, contingent): The scaffold is a hand-edit template whose header explicitly tells the user to change values, QUOTE them, and delete lines they aren't editing. The bug only bites if (a) the source .p config's vehicle field literally contains a '"' AND (b) the user feeds the unedited template straight back into load_edit_spec. The data source is the user's own local .p file (no external/attacker trust boundary), and real configs don't contain quotes — I confirmed vehicle='VMP', model='VMP250IR_RT' in VMP/ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0002.p. Worst case is a clear ScannerError with line/column, not silent corruption.

Verdict: the defect is real for the active vehicle line (line 573), but the finding overstates it by including two commented lines that are not actually affected. Severity nit is appropriate.

title: Whitespace-only author collapses to an empty author in the provenance banner
evidence: author = str(author).strip() if author else (os.environ.get('USER') or 'unknown'). A YAML author of '   ' passes the ASCII/control-char checks (lines 190-193), is truthy, so the env/USER fallback is skipped, and then .strip() yields ''. The banner then emits '; PATCHED <when> by  -- ...' (empty author) and the provenance reads '[PATCH <when> ]'. The intended fallback to $USER/'unknown' is bypassed for whitespace-only authors.
suggested_fix: Compute stripped = str(author).strip() if isinstance(author, str) else '' and use `stripped or os.environ.get('USER') or 'unknown'`, so a blank/whitespace author falls back like a missing one.
verification: Finding holds after reading the cited code at /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/rsi/config_patch.py:189-194. Line 194 is `author = str(author).strip() if author else (os.environ.get("USER") or "unknown")`. The validation at lines 190-193 only rejects non-str, non-ASCII, or control-char authors; it does NOT reject whitespace-only strings. A YAML author of "   " is a str, isascii() True, has no control chars, so it passes validation. It is also truthy, so the ternary takes the `str(author).strip()` branch which yields '' — bypassing the intended $USER/'unknown' fallback. I verified this empirically in Python (the expression returns '' for author="   "). This contrasts with `note` at line 183, which IS explicitly guarded against whitespace-only via `or not note.strip()`; `author` lacks the equivalent guard. Downstream impact is real but cosmetic: line 351 `f"; [PATCH {when} {spec.author}]".rstrip()` produces `[PATCH <when> ]` (trailing space before ']' is not stripped since ']' follows it), and line 459 produces `; PATCHED <when> by  -- ...` with an empty author and double space. These are comment-line provenance/audit annotations (prefixed with ';'), not active config, so there is no functional or security harm — only a degraded audit trail (lost attribution). The proposed fix (compute stripped first, then `stripped or os.environ.get('USER') or 'unknown'`) is correct. Severity nit is well-calibrated: cosmetic, affects only an audit comment, requires a deliberately blank author field.

title: Provenance banner accumulates without bound on repeated re-patches
evidence: edit_config_text always prepends a fresh _build_banner to the active region (new_config = banner + edited_active). On each successive patch of an already-patched file the prior banner is inside `active` and is preserved, so banners stack: verified 3 banners ('; PATCHED' count == 3) after 3 sequential patches. The CONFIG_MARKER / embedded-original block stays correctly at 1 and inline [PATCH ...] provenance lines are intended to accumulate, so this is cosmetic config bloat bounded by the 65535-byte config_size limit, not corruption.
suggested_fix: When marker_idx >= 0 (re-patch), strip any prior leading banner block (delimited by the '; ===' bars) before prepending the new one, or only emit the banner on the first patch and rely on the per-change [PATCH ...] lines thereafter.
verification:
VERIFIED TRUE (cosmetic). I read src/odas_tpw/rsi/config_patch.py:edit_config_text and confirmed the cited mechanism. On re-patch, marker_idx>=0 so active = config_str[:marker_idx] (line 326), which captures everything ABOVE the CONFIG_MARKER — including the prior banner, since the banner is prepended at the very top of the config (line 434-435). Banner lines are comments, so _parse_blocks skips them (line 259-261) but they remain in raw_lines and are emitted verbatim by the reassembly loop (lines 419-428) into edited_active. Then line 434-435 unconditionally prepends a fresh _build_banner. Hence one extra banner per re-patch.

Empirically reproduced with the real code: applying three DISTINCT specs sequentially yields '; PATCHED' count == 3, while the CONFIG_MARKER ('; ### Original Configuration String Below ###') stays at 1 and the embedded-original block stays singular and correct; inline '; [PATCH ...]' provenance lines accumulate (count == 3), which is intended. The auditor's description matches exactly.

Impact is correctly characterized as cosmetic and bounded, NOT corruption: write_patched_pfile hard-rejects any config exceeding the uint16 config_size field with a ValueError (line 511: if len(new_bytes) > 0xFFFF). Each banner is ~5 lines/~250 bytes, so reaching the 65535-byte limit would require hundreds of re-patches on a typically multi-KB config — not a practical concern, and failure would be a clean error, never silent corruption. The existing test_idempotent_repatch (tests/test_config_patch.py:359) only covers re-applying the SAME spec (a no-op) and asserts CONFIG_MARKER==1; it neither covers distinct sequential re-patches nor asserts banner count, so no test contradicts the finding. The proposed fix (strip the prior leading banner bar-delimited block on re-patch, or emit the banner only on first patch) is sound. Severity nit is accurate: a real but purely cosmetic robustness/bloat issue with no functional consequence in realistic use.

title: YAML date/datetime config values crash compute_hash/resolve_output_dir with an opaque TypeError
line: 41 (and 21-41); reached via canonicalize json.dumps at 209, compute_hash 218-219, resolve_output_dir 238
category: edge-case / NaN-edge mishandling / silent crash on valid input
evidence: ruamel.yaml natively parses unquoted ISO dates/timestamps into Python datetime.date / datetime.datetime objects. _normalize_value() handles None/bool/int/float/str and then falls through with `return v` (line 41), passing the date object through unchanged. _normalize_nested likewise returns it. canonicalize() then calls json.dumps(...) which raises `TypeError: Object of type date is not JSON serializable`, crashing compute_hash -> resolve_output_dir / write_signature. This is REACHABLE end-to-end through a fully valid perturb config: the `netcdf` section's DEFAULTS define date fields (date_created, date_modified, date_issued, time_coverage_start, time_coverage_end, ...). Verified: a config file with `netcdf:\n  date_created: 2025-01-15` passes load_config/validate_config (valid section+key), then `resolve_output_dir(..., 'netcdf', loaded['netcdf'])` raises `TypeError - Object of type date is not JSON serializable` with no field name in the message. Note the code at lines 31-33 explicitly added a guard for non-finite floats specifically to avoid 'an opaque message' in this exact call chain, but datetime objects (a far more common YAML artifact than NaN) were not guarded. No test exercises a date-valued config (grep of test_config*.py shows none).
suggested_fix: In _normalize_value, before the final `return v`, coerce date/datetime (and any other non-JSON-serializable scalar) to a deterministic string, e.g. add `import datetime` and `if isinstance(v, (datetime.date, datetime.datetime)): return v.isoformat()`. More robustly, replace the bare `return v` fall-through with `return repr(v)` (mirroring the non-finite-float branch) or raise a clear ValueError naming the section/key so the user knows to quote the date. Add a regression test that hashes a netcdf config with a bare date_created.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL. I reproduced the crash end-to-end against the actual code.

Mechanism, confirmed by reading and running the code:
- `_normalize_value` (src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:21-41) handles None/bool/int/float/str and falls through with a bare `return v` at line 41. A `datetime.date`/`datetime` passes through unchanged; `_normalize_nested` (44-52) likewise returns it via `_normalize_value`.
- `canonicalize` (193-209) calls `json.dumps(sections, ...)` at line 209, which raises `TypeError: Object of type date is not JSON serializable`. This propagates through `compute_hash` (218) and `resolve_output_dir` (238).

Reachability, confirmed:
- The perturb `netcdf` section DEFAULTS (src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:176-225) include `date_created`, `date_modified`, `date_issued`, `time_coverage_start`, `time_coverage_end`, etc. (defaults are None, but they are valid user-settable keys).
- pipeline.py:221-224 inject `("netcdf", netcdf_p)` into the `combo`/`diss_combo`/`chi_combo`/`ctd_combo` upstream chains, and pipeline.py:2144-2147 (`_resolve_dst`) passes those chains as `upstream` into `resolve_output_dir`. So the netcdf params genuinely flow into `_canonicalize_section("netcdf", ...)` -> `_normalize_nested` -> `json.dumps`.
- I ran two reproductions in /tmp/sectest: (1) ruamel.yaml parses `date_created: 2025-01-15` into `datetime.date(2025,1,15)` and `time_coverage_start: 2025-01-15T08:30:00` into a `TimeStamp` (datetime subclass); `load_config`+`validate_config` accept it; `merge_config('netcdf', ...)` passes it through; `resolve_output_dir(...,'netcdf',merged)` raises `TypeError: Object of type date is not JSON serializable`. (2) Using the REAL `pipeline._upstream_for("combo", loaded)` chain (sections include 'netcdf'), `resolve_output_dir(base,'combo','binning',binning_p,upstream=chain)` raises the same TypeError.

Corroborating details the finding cited and I verified:
- The non-finite-float guard at lines 31-35 exists explicitly to avoid "an opaque message" in this exact canonicalize->compute_hash->resolve_output_dir call chain — establishing the maintainer's own precedent for handling JSON-unserializable scalars here. datetime objects were not guarded.
- No test in tests/test_perturb_config.py / test_config_shared.py exercises a date-valued config.
- The shipped template (config.py:526-537) presents these as `null` with comments ("auto-filled if null"), inviting a user to fill in a bare ISO date — the idiomatic YAML form for date fields, making this notably more likely than the already-guarded NaN case.

Severity: I downgrade major->minor. It is a genuine crash-on-valid-input with an opaque error and a trivial fix (coerce date/datetime via isoformat() or repr in _normalize_value), but: it fails fast at pipeline directory-resolution setup (not silent data corruption or wrong scientific output), requires the user to supply a date value in metadata, and has an easy workaround (quote the value, e.g. date_created: \"2025-01-15\", which is also a natural way to write it). The opaque message is real on older Pythons; on Python 3.14 json now appends 'when serializing dict item date_created', partially mitigating the diagnosability complaint. Net: a real, reachable, easily-fixable edge-case crash, but bounded impact -> minor rather than major.

title: resolve_output_dir 2-digit glob vs format mismatch causes directory collision and silent provenance corruption past 100 configs
category: off-by-N / silent data corruption / provenance loss
evidence: The discovery glob is `f"{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]"` (exactly two digits, line 241) but new directories are created with `f"{prefix}_{next_seq:02d}"` (line 256), which produces THREE digits for next_seq >= 100 (e.g. eps_100). Consequences once >100 distinct configs accumulate under one base: (1) eps_100+ are invisible to the glob, so max_seq is computed only from the 2-digit dirs (capped at 99) -> next_seq becomes 100 again, COLLIDING with the existing eps_100; (2) reuse-on-hash-match silently breaks for all configs stored in 3-digit dirs (their signature is never scanned), so each rerun creates a new colliding dir instead of reusing. Verified empirically: after creating 101 distinct configs (dirs eps_00..eps_100), requesting a brand-new distinct config lands in eps_100, leaving TWO distinct .params_sha256_* signature files in one directory. Output products from two different parameter sets are then co-mingled in a single dir with no error -- silent corruption and loss of the param->dir provenance that the whole signature mechanism exists to guarantee. This is an extreme but real case (e.g. a parameter sweep, or accumulated history) and fails silently with no diagnostic.
suggested_fix: Make the glob and the formatter agree and avoid a hard cap. Either (a) widen both: scan with `f"{prefix}_[0-9]*"` (or a regex `^{prefix}_(\d+)$`) and keep zero-padding only as cosmetic, OR (b) detect overflow and refuse rather than collide. Minimal fix: change the glob to `f"{prefix}_[0-9]*"` so 3+ digit dirs are discovered for both max_seq and signature reuse; keep `{next_seq:02d}` (it naturally widens past 99). Add a test that resolves 101+ distinct configs and asserts each gets a unique dir and that reuse works for dir 100.
verification:
The finding is structurally correct and I verified every load-bearing claim against the actual code at /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py.

Confirmed facts:
- Line 241: `pattern = str(base / f"{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]")` — exactly two `[0-9]` classes, matching ONLY 2-digit sequence names. I confirmed via glob that `eps_[0-9][0-9]` matches `eps_00`/`eps_99` but NOT `eps_100`/`eps_101`.

The proposed fix `eps_[0-9]*` matches both 2- and 3-digit dirs and resolves both symptoms.

So the bug is genuine, silent, has no guard/diagnostic, and defeats the param->dir provenance guarantee the signature mechanism exists for.

Severity downgrade to minor (from major): Triggering requires 100 DISTINCT parameter configurations accumulated under a single output base for a single processing stage — each `eps_NN`/`chi_NN`/`prof_NN` directory is one unique config hash. In this domain (microstructure processing tool; typical use varies a few params like fft_length/salinity, callers in rsi/cli.py and perturb/pipeline.py resolve a small number of stages per run), reaching 100+ distinct configs in one base is the "extreme but real" precondition the finding itself acknowledges — realistic only for a large deliberate parameter sweep or very long accumulated history, with no evidence of such sweeps in the codebase. The impact when triggered is severe in kind (silent co-mingling), but the likelihood in practice is low, which places it at minor rather than major.

title: _canonicalize_section and write_resolved_config crash with AttributeError on None params for static sections (inconsistent with dynamic-key guard)
evidence: The dynamic-key branches use `(params or {}).items()` / `dict(up_params or {})` (lines 174, 289, 299) to tolerate a None section body, but the STATIC branches do bare `params.items()` (line 178) and `up_params.items()` (line 293) and `params.items()` (line 302). Verified: `canonicalize('chi', {}, upstream=[('epsilon', None)])` and `write_resolved_config(Path(d), 'chi', {}, upstream=[('epsilon', None)])` both raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'items'`. Currently not reached via load_config (which converts a None-bodied section to {} at lines 104-105), but the public/re-exported canonicalize/compute_hash/write_resolved_config accept arbitrary params, and the CLI passes `file_config.get(real_section, {})` -- a single missed default or a programmatic None upstream entry produces an opaque AttributeError instead of being treated as 'empty section'. The asymmetry between the two branches is a latent trap.
suggested_fix: Make the static branches None-tolerant to match the dynamic ones: use `for k, v in (params or {}).items()` at lines 178 and 302, and `for k, v in (up_params or {}).items()` at line 293. (Or normalize params to `{}` once at the top of each method.)
verification:
Verified against /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py. The asymmetry is real and exactly as described: the dynamic-key branches guard with `sorted((params or {}).items())` (line 173) and `dict(up_params or {})`/`dict(params or {})` (lines 290, 299), while the static branches use bare `params.items()` (line 178), `up_params.items()` (line 293), and `params.items()` (line 302). I reproduced both crashes: `canonicalize('chi', {}, upstream=[('epsilon', None)])` and `write_resolved_config(Path(d), 'chi', {}, upstream=[('epsilon', None)])` both raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'items'`, as does passing the main static `params=None`. The functions are publicly re-exported in rsi/config.py:65-69 and perturb/config.py:298-302, so the inconsistency is on the public API surface, and the proposed `(params or {})`/`(up_params or {})` fix is correct and aligns the branches.

However, the finding's practical-reach narrative is overstated. No in-repo caller can trigger it: every `params` and upstream-param argument originates from `merge_config`, which always returns a dict (line 164's comprehension, never None); `_upstream_for` (pipeline.py:182-225) builds all upstream tuples from `merge_config(...)` results; the cited CLI path `file_config.get(real_section, {})` (rsi/cli.py:180) defaults to `{}` and is then passed through `merge_config`; and `load_config` normalizes None-bodied YAML sections to `{}` (lines 104-106). So the defect is a genuine but currently-unreachable latent edge case / defensive-coding inconsistency, triggerable only by a programmatic caller that bypasses merge_config/load_config and passes a literal None section body. No test pins the crashing behavior. Given the genuine asymmetry on a public API but zero current reachability and no data-correctness consequence, this is best classified as a nit rather than minor.

title: write_signature/write_resolved_config reject str directory argument, inconsistent with the str|Path contract of sibling functions
evidence: load_config, resolve_output_dir, and generate_template all accept `str | Path` and internally `Path(...)`-coerce. write_signature and write_resolved_config instead annotate `directory: Path` and immediately do `directory / "..."`, so a plain str raises `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for /: 'str' and 'str'`. Verified for both. The canonical caller (resolve_output_dir) always passes a Path, so this is latent rather than active, but it is a footgun for direct API users and breaks the uniform path-like convention used everywhere else in the module (and the test_write_signature_standalone test only ever passes a Path, so the gap is uncovered).
suggested_fix: Coerce at entry for symmetry: change signatures to `directory: str | Path` and add `directory = Path(directory)` as the first line of both write_signature and write_resolved_config.
verification:
Verified against the actual code in /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/config_base.py.

Facts confirmed:
1. Type-annotation inconsistency is real. `write_signature` (line 263) and `write_resolved_config` (line 277) annotate `directory: Path` and immediately do `directory / f".params_sha256_{h}"` (line 271) and `directory / "config.yaml"` (line 307) with no coercion. The sibling functions DO coerce: `load_config(self, path: str | Path)` opens path directly but `resolve_output_dir` does `base = Path(base)` (line 237) and `generate_template(path: str | Path)` does `path = Path(path)` (rsi/config.py:124). So the module's convention is str|Path + coerce, and these two functions break it.

2. Runtime behavior is exactly as claimed. I empirically ran both via the public re-exports in odas_tpw.rsi.config and both raised `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for /: 'str' and 'str'` when passed a plain str directory. Not overstated.

3. Both functions are part of the public API surface (re-exported as module-level functions in rsi/config.py:68-69 and perturb/config.py:301-302).

Mitigating / severity-lowering facts:
- It is fully latent in-tree. The only callers are resolve_output_dir (config_base.py:258, passes `new_dir = base / ...`, a Path) and rsi/cli.py:203 (passes `output_dir`, the Path return of resolve_output_dir at line 202). No in-tree caller passes a str.
- mypy would flag a str passed to `directory: Path`, so a type-checked direct caller is protected; the break only manifests for an un-type-checked direct call.
- The existing test (test_config_shared.py:207 / :249) only passes tmp_path (a Path), so the str path is uncovered, as the finding states.

Conclusion: the defect genuinely holds — both the annotation inconsistency and the TypeError are real and reproducible, and the functions are public. But the practical impact is small (latent, mypy-guarded, trivial fix). The claimed severity "minor" is slightly generous given full latency; "nit" is the more accurate label for a cosmetic-contract inconsistency with no active in-tree failure.

title: Unknown keys in params are silently dropped by canonicalize/merge_config instead of validated, so a typo'd key hashes identically to the clean config
line: 164 (merge_config drop), 178-179 (_canonicalize_section overlay only keeps keys in base)
evidence: validate_config (lines 117-133) rejects unknown keys, but it is only invoked by load_config. The hashing/merge path does NOT re-validate: _canonicalize_section overlays only `if k in base` (line 179) and merge_config keeps only `if k in merged` (lines 156, 162). So params containing an unknown key (e.g. a programmatically built dict, or a key that was renamed/removed from DEFAULTS between versions) are silently dropped. Verified: `compute_hash('epsilon', {'totally_bogus_key': 12345})` equals `compute_hash('epsilon', {})`, and `merge_config('epsilon', file_values={'totally_bogus_key': 999})` drops the key with no error. For the normal CLI/file flow this is shielded by load_config's validation, so impact is limited; but it means the two layers disagree on what is a 'valid' params dict and a stale/typo'd key never surfaces when callers bypass load_config.
suggested_fix: Optionally call self.validate_config({section: params}) (and the upstream sections) at the top of canonicalize/compute_hash, or document explicitly that these methods assume pre-validated params. At minimum, add a test asserting that an unknown key reaching compute_hash either raises or is documented as intentionally ignored.
verification:
Mechanical facts all reproduced. In src/odas_tpw/config_base.py: validate_config (lines 117-133) rejects unknown keys but is only called from load_config (line 114). merge_config keeps only known keys (`if k in merged`, lines 156 and 162) and strips Nones (line 164); _canonicalize_section overlays only `if k in base` (lines 178-179). I confirmed with python3: compute_hash('epsilon', {}) == compute_hash('epsilon', {'totally_bogus_key': 12345}) (both d7bf38cb...), and merge_config('epsilon', file_values={'totally_bogus_key': 999}) returns the clean defaults dict with the bogus key dropped and no error. So a typo'd/stale key hashes identically to the clean config.

The finding is genuine but very narrow, and the finding itself states the caveat correctly. Every production path is shielded: rsi/cli.py loads config via _load_file_config -> load_config (validates, line 88) before merge_config (line 182), and CLI overrides are built from a hardcoded mapping dict so they can only contain known keys; perturb/cli.py loads via _load_and_merge -> load_config (validates, line 33), then threads the validated config into pipeline.py merge_config calls and process_file. An unknown key therefore raises ValueError at file-load time before ever reaching the hash path. The only way to hit the silent drop is to bypass load_config and pass a hand-built dict directly to compute_hash/merge_config -- which is possible because these methods are publicly re-exported (perturb/__init__.py exports merge_config) and are exercised directly in tests (tests/test_config_shared.py). There is no docstring on merge_config/canonicalize/compute_hash stating a pre-validation precondition, so the merge/hash layer and the load layer genuinely disagree on what a valid params dict is.

Verdict: real, but nit. The behavior is reproducible and the methods are callable without load_config, yet the practical blast radius for CLI/file users is essentially nil (validation catches the same typo on load, including renamed/removed keys on the next run). The proposed fix (re-validate at the top of canonicalize, or document the precondition, or add a test asserting the intentional-ignore) is low-cost and reasonable. Severity nit is correct.

title: dissipation.py does not scrub NaN window temperature before viscosity; sibling l4.py does, so a NaN window silently yields NaN epsilon with method=0
evidence: In _compute_epsilon, nu_out is computed directly from l3.temp: `nu_out = visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres)` (line 218) or `visc35(l3.temp)` (line 220), then `nu = nu_out[j]` is passed into _estimate_epsilon (line 224-251). l3.temp can legitimately be NaN: scor160/l3.py:178-181 records np.nan when l1.temp is absent/mismatched, and `np.mean(l1.temp[s:e])` propagates NaN if any fast-temperature sample in the window is NaN (e.g. an FP07/T1 dropout interpolated through helpers.py:331). I verified visc35(nan)->nan and visc(nan,35,100)->nan (both pass NaN through, no raise), and that _estimate_epsilon(K, spec, nu=nan, ...) returns epsilon=nan, method=0, fom=nan, FM=nan: e_10=7.5*nan*trapz=nan, the guard `if e_10<=0` is False for NaN (l4.py:367) so it is never floored, and `use_isr = nan >= thr` is False so the variance branch runs and produces NaN throughout. The canonical process_l4 path guards exactly this case (l4.py:154-160: non-finite window temp -> 10 degC with a warning), but the rsi.dissipation L4 loop has no equivalent scrub, so the two epsilon code paths diverge on identical input. Net effect: a window with a transient temperature gap drops to NaN epsilon (data loss) instead of using a fallback viscosity, and the NaN window is reported with method=0, indistinguishable from a genuine variance-method estimate (epsilon=NaN remains the authoritative invalid marker, and `method` is metadata not consumed downstream, so this is robustness/consistency, not a silent wrong-number).
suggested_fix: Mirror l4.py: after computing l3.temp-derived viscosity (or before, on the temperature), replace non-finite temperatures with a fallback (10 degC) and emit a warning, e.g. `temp_w = np.where(np.isfinite(l3.temp), l3.temp, 10.0)` used for both the visc and visc35 branches; or guard nu in the j-loop: `nu = nu_out[j]; if not np.isfinite(nu): nu = visc35(10.0)`. Add a test feeding a window with NaN temperature and asserting epsilon is finite (matching process_l4 behaviour).
verification:
VERIFIED REAL. The code divergence is confirmed and reproduced.

Code: In dissipation.py:213-225 the per-window viscosity is computed directly from l3.temp with no finite-scrub: `nu_out = visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres)` (line 218) or `visc35(l3.temp)` (line 220), then `nu = nu_out[j]` is fed to `_estimate_epsilon` (line 225, 242-251). The canonical sibling l4.py:154-160 explicitly does `temp = np.where(np.isfinite(temp), temp, 10.0)` with a warning before computing `nu = visc35(temp[j])` (l4.py:176). Same input, two epsilon paths, divergent output.

Empirically confirmed: `visc35(nan)=nan` and `visc(nan,35,100)=nan` (no raise); in `_estimate_epsilon`, `e_10 = 7.5*nan*trapz = nan` and the floor guard `if e_10<=0` is False for NaN (l4.py:367), and `use_isr = e_1 >= thr*margin` is False for NaN, so it takes the variance branch (method=0). End-to-end reproduction with a finite positive shear spectrum: NaN viscosity -> `epsilon=nan, method=0, fom=nan`, whereas 10 degC -> `epsilon=9.6e-11, method=0, fom=1.02`. So a NaN window temperature silently yields NaN epsilon where process_l4 yields a finite fallback estimate.

l3.temp is built per-window (l3.py:171-181, all_temp.append in the window loop), so `l3.temp.size == n_spec`; thus the l4.py:152 size-mismatch fallback does not apply and the finite-scrub at 154-160 is the relevant guard — exactly what dissipation.py lacks. `np.mean(l1.temp[s:e])` NaN-poisons a whole window mean from a single NaN sample (l3.py:179). No test covers NaN window temperature in the rsi.dissipation path.

Two corrections to the finding's evidence that bound (but do not negate) the defect: (1) its cited NaN route via `np.interp` (helpers.py:331) and FP07 conversion is inaccurate — `np.interp` clamps out-of-range and does not introduce NaN, and `convert_therm` clips Z to [-0.6,0.6] before `log` (channels.py:119-121) so it does not produce NaN from raw counts; (2) a *missing* T1 channel in the `.p` path raises KeyError (`pf.channels[t_name]`, helpers.py:133), not NaN. The genuine NaN routes are NaN already present in the source temperature array: NetCDF fill values via `_channels_from_nc` (`ds.variables[t_name][:].data`, helpers.py:171) or perturb-merged temperature gaps. These are plausible but uncommon.

Impact is bounded as the finding itself concedes: `epsilon=NaN` remains the authoritative invalid marker, and `method` (0/1) is metadata not consumed downstream for any wrong-number decision (grep of processing/perturb shows only unrelated 'method' uses — binning/combo). So this is a port-consistency + robustness divergence with minor data loss (a recoverable window dropped to NaN instead of a fallback-viscosity estimate), not a silent wrong number. The defect holds and the proposed fix (mirror l4.py's finite-scrub) is correct.

title: Chi from per-profile NetCDF silently discards FP07 diff_gain and electronics calibration, biasing the observed gradient spectrum
evidence: In _load_therm_channels, the NetCDF source branch (lines 765-788) appends a hardcoded diff_gains.append(0.94) for every thermistor and never populates therm_cal, so line 792 sets data["therm_cal"] = [{}] * len(therm). The .p branch instead reads the real per-channel diff_gain (line 741) and extracts FP07 calibration from the base channel (line 750). For the actual SN479 data, PFile reports diff_gain=0.912 (T1_dT1) and 0.920 (T2_dT2) -- not 0.94 -- and beta_1=3143.55, e_b=0.68283 (not the noise-model defaults 3000.0/0.68). The diff_gain is not just a noise-model knob: in chi/l3_chi.py:296 bl_corrections = [_bilinear_correction(F_const, dg, fs) for dg in diff_gains] applies it as a pre-emphasis correction (G_2 = 1 + (2*pi*diff_gain*F)^2) directly to the OBSERVED temperature-gradient spectrum, and empty therm_cal makes gradT_noise_batch use default hardware parameters for the noise floor (which sets K_max and fom). The per-profile NC global attribute configuration_string IS written by convert.py:488 (ds.configuration_string = pf.config_str), so the real config is available but never parsed in this branch. The chi CLI accepts .nc input (cli.py:376 _resolve_files(..., {".p", ".nc"})), and the documented workflow `rsi-tpw prof VMP/*.p` then chi-on-profiles hits exactly this path. The divergence is silent (no warning) and untested: the NC tests only assert channels are located (tests/test_chi.py:1069-1071), never that diff_gain/therm_cal are recovered.
suggested_fix: In the NetCDF branch of _load_therm_channels, parse the embedded configuration_string global attribute via parse_config() and extract the per-channel diff_gain and FP07 calibration the same way the .p branch does (re.match(r"^(\w+)_d\1$", name) for the base channel, _extract_therm_cal on the base config, ch.get("diff_gain") for the gradient channel). If configuration_string is absent, emit a warnings.warn that chi noise-floor and pre-emphasis fall back to defaults so the calibration loss is not silent.
verification:
The core defect is real and verified empirically end-to-end. In `_load_therm_channels` (src/odas_tpw/rsi/chi_io.py:765-792), the NetCDF branch hardcodes `diff_gains.append(0.94)` (lines 779, 786) and never populates `therm_cal`, so line 792 yields `[{}] * len(therm)`. I ran both branches on the same data: the .p source returns `diff_gains=[0.912, 0.92]` with full therm_cal (beta_1=3143.55, e_b=0.6828, gain=6.0, T_0=289.301, beta_2=250000), while a per-profile NetCDF of the SAME file returns `diff_gains=[0.94, 0.94]` and `therm_cal=[{}, {}]`, with no warning. The cited SN479 config values are exactly correct (T1_dT1 diff_gain=0.912, T2_dT2=0.920, beta_1=3143.55, e_b=0.68283 — none match the defaults). The `configuration_string` global attribute IS written to the per-profile NetCDF (profile.py:312-313; I confirmed it is present, 10371 chars) but chi_io never calls parse_config on it. The chi CLI accepts .nc (cli.py), so the documented prof→chi workflow hits this path. The lost calibration is load-bearing for the noise floor: l3_chi.py:217-229 feeds therm_cal and diff_gains into gradT_noise_batch, and chi.py:151/223/287 use the noise spectrum to set K_max (integration limit) and fom. I measured the impact: wrong diff_gain shifts the noise spectrum ~6% and missing therm_cal shifts it ~10% (median), which propagates into K_max, K_max_ratio, fom, and the variance-corrected chi estimate.

HOWEVER, the finding is overstated in its headline mechanism. It claims the defect "biases the OBSERVED gradient spectrum" via `bl_corrections` applying `G_2 = 1 + (2*pi*diff_gain*F)^2` (l3_chi.py:296, 206). That is wrong: `bl_corrections` are `_bilinear_correction` values (chi/chi.py:616), which I measured to be diff_gain-INsensitive (max relative difference 9e-8 between 0.912 and 0.94). The actual G_2 pre-emphasis deconvolution happens at PFile read time in `_apply_deconvolution`/`deconvolve` (p_file.py:541-588) using the correct per-channel diff_gain, and the result is already baked into the stored T1_dT1 channel values in the NetCDF. So the observed gradient spectrum is NOT biased by this defect; only the noise floor / QC path is. The real magnitude is a ~6-10% noise-floor bias affecting K_max/fom, not a gross bias of the core spectrum. Given the silent, untested calibration loss on a supported workflow (genuine fidelity defect, fix is readily available) but the corrected and narrower impact (noise-floor/QC only, single-digit-percent), this is a minor defect rather than major.

title: Variance-correction integration grid is under-resolved when kB << K_max (up to ~2% chi bias), contradicting the documented <0.1% accuracy
evidence: In _variance_correction, K_upper = max(K_max * 5, kB * 5) and K_fine = np.linspace(K_upper/n_fine*0.01, K_upper, n_fine) with n_fine=2000. When kB is small relative to K_max (a low-epsilon window where the noise/anti-aliasing mask still pushes K_max out near K_AA), K_upper is driven by 5*K_max, spreading the fixed 2000-point grid so coarsely that the spectral peak near kB is under-resolved. Measured V_total error: for kB=5, K_max=200 -> grid dK=0.5 cpm -> 1.8% error; for kB=30, K_max=140 -> 0.025% error; for kB=228, K_max=228 -> ~0% error. This directly contradicts the docstring claim 'Uses a 2000-point grid (sufficient for <0.1% accuracy)' (line 93-94). The error propagates linearly into chi via chi = 6*KAPPA_T*obs_var*correction in the iterative (line 573) and MLE (line 433) paths, and into chi_vc in Method 1 (line 244, though there it is only a grid centre so largely washed out). So the affected output is chi for low-kB windows in Methods 2/3.
suggested_fix: Decouple the integration extent from K_max for the V_total grid: the Batchelor/Kraichnan spectrum has negligible content beyond ~5-8 kB, so use K_upper = max(K_max, 8*kB) (still covers K_max for the V_resolved [K_min,K_max] mask while keeping the grid fine near the peak). Verified: this cuts the kB=5,K_max=200 error from 1.8% to 0.07% and leaves the kB~K_max cases unchanged. Update the docstring accuracy claim accordingly, or increase n_fine adaptively when K_upper/kB is large.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL, and actually understated. The under-resolution defect at src/odas_tpw/chi/chi.py:98-99 holds, contradicting the docstring's "<0.1% accuracy" claim (lines 93-94).

I attempted to refute it.
