My first refutation partly succeeded: the auditor measured V_total error in isolation (I reproduced exactly: -1.83% for kB=5,K_max=200 Batchelor), but chi scales by the RATIO correction = V_total/V_resolved (lines 244, 433, 573), not V_total alone. For the Method 1 (_chi_from_epsilon) and any K_min=0 cases, the coarse-grid error in V_total LARGELY CANCELS in the ratio because V_resolved shares the same K_fine grid — I measured only +0.007% residual error in the correction factor. So the auditor's specific "~2% chi bias" mechanism via V_total is wrong for those paths, and Method 1 also washes it out in the subsequent grid search (line 248) as the auditor concedes.

BUT the refutation fails for the production-default path. process_l4_chi_fit defaults fit_method="iterative" and spectrum_model="kraichnan" (l4_chi.py:203-204), and _iterative_fit calls _variance_correction with K_min=k_l_new > 0 (chi.py:569-570). With K_min>0 the cancellation breaks: the coarse 2000-point grid (K_upper=5*K_max spread over ~700 cpm => dK~0.35 cpm) cannot resolve the steep Kraichnan/Batchelor peak near kB sitting just above the sharp k_l cutoff. Using realistic parameters (nu=visc35(15)=1.18e-6, k_l=max(K[1]=0.71cpm, 3*k_star), K_max=f_AA/W=140cpm for f_AA=98, speed=0.7), I measured correction-factor error vs a converged n=500000 grid: kB=3 -> +41.6%, kB=5 -> +19.3%, kB=8 -> +9.2%, kB=15 -> +3.2%. A convergence sweep (n_fine=2000..512000) confirms n=2000 is genuinely unconverged (non-monotonic, 3-42% off). This error propagates LINEARLY into chi via chi_obs = chi_band * correction (chi.py:573). These low-kB windows (epsilon ~3e-15 to 2e-13 W/kg) pass QC — the only kB cut is kB<1 (chi.py:538), and K_max_ratio=K_max/kB~10-47 looks "good".

So the core claim (grid under-resolved when kB<<K_max, contradicting <0.1% docstring, biasing low-kB chi in Method 2) is TRUE and matters: it affects the default production path with 9-42% errors, not ~2%. Two auditor inaccuracies: (a) their V_total-isolation reasoning is the wrong mechanism for the K_min=0 paths (cancellation), and (b) their proposed fix K_upper=max(K_max,8*kB) is itself wrong — I measured it makes kB=3 WORSE (+12.5%) and kB=5 worse (+5.2%) in realistic cases, because clamping K_upper without raising point density still under-resolves; the right fix is adaptive n_fine or fixed dK. Raising severity to major because the affected output is the default-method chi for low-epsilon windows that survive QC, with errors up to ~40%.

title: Unreachable `kB_best < 1` guard in _iterative_fit; if the MLE grid floor ever drops below 1 cpm it would leak a finite chi/epsilon for a failed fit, contradicting the function's own NaN-leak-prevention design
evidence: Line 538 `if not np.isfinite(kB_best) or kB_best < 1: break` is meant to reject sub-1-cpm fits (mirroring _chi_from_epsilon line 211-213, which returns NaN for kB<1). But _mle_find_kB can never return kB<1: _KB_COARSE = np.logspace(0,4.5,100) has min exactly 1.0 (line 63), and the fine grid clamps kB_lo = max(best_coarse*0.5, 1.0) (line 346). So the `kB_best < 1` branch is dead. Critically, the break only sets kB_best to the (>=1, so finite) value and then falls through to lines 580-588 where `if np.isfinite(kB_best)` computes epsilon and `chi = chi_obs` (finite) -- the NaN-leak guard at lines 582-588 keys on np.isfinite(kB_best), NOT on kB_best>=1. So if the coarse grid lower bound were ever lowered below 1.0 (a plausible future tuning change), a kB_best in [floor,1) would break out at line 538 yet still leak a finite chi/epsilon, exactly the failure the comment at 582-586 says it prevents, and inconsistent with Method 1 which NaNs out kB<1.
suggested_fix: Make the NaN-leak guard consistent with the kB<1 rejection: track whether the fit actually succeeded (e.g. set kB_best=np.nan before `break` when kB_best<1, or gate the final chi/epsilon on `np.isfinite(kB_best) and kB_best >= 1`). This is currently latent (grid floor is 1.0) but the guard and the comment are out of sync and would silently regress if the grid is retuned.
verification:
All technical claims in the finding are verified against the actual code in /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/chi/chi.py:

1. Line 538: `if not np.isfinite(kB_best) or kB_best < 1: break` — the `kB_best < 1` disjunct is the subject. Confirmed.
2. Line 63: `_KB_COARSE = np.logspace(0, 4.5, 100)`. I ran numpy: `g.min()` is exactly `np.float64(1.0)`. So the coarse grid floor is 1.0.
3. Line 346: `kB_lo = max(best_coarse * 0.5, 1.0)` clamps the fine grid floor to 1.0 as well.
4. `_mle_find_kB` (lines 318-356) can only return: `np.nan` (lines 326, 341), `best_coarse` (>=1.0), or `kB_fine[argmin]` (>=1.0). It can never return a finite value < 1. Therefore the `kB_best < 1` branch at line 538 is genuinely dead today.
5. Method 1's `_chi_from_epsilon` (lines 211-213) does return NaN chi when `kB < 1`, so the two paths are inconsistent in intent.
6. The final NaN-leak guard at lines 580-588 keys on `np.isfinite(kB_best)` (line 587: `chi = chi_obs if np.isfinite(kB_best) else np.nan`), NOT on `kB_best >= 1`. So the auditor is correct that IF the grid floor were lowered below 1.0, a finite `kB_best` in `[floor, 1)` would `break` at 538 yet still fall through to leak a finite chi/epsilon — exactly what the comment at 582-586 claims to prevent.

So the finding is factually accurate: the guard condition and the local comment/Method-1 sibling are out of sync, and the `kB_best < 1` test is dead code given the current grid floor of 1.0. However, this is purely latent — with the grid floor at exactly 1.0 there is NO incorrect behavior, no NaN leak, and no wrong chi/epsilon in the current code. The impact is entirely contingent on a hypothetical future tuning change. The auditor honestly scoped this as a nit (dead code / robustness / future-regression hazard). It is a real, correct observation about a code/comment inconsistency, but with zero present-day functional impact, so nit is the appropriate severity.

title: kraichnan_grad leaks divide-by-zero RuntimeWarning at kB=0 (incomplete errstate guard; contradicts batchelor_grad's 'mirror' comment)
evidence: In kraichnan_grad, `y = k / kB` (line 173) and `sq6q_y = sq6q * y` (line 175) are computed OUTSIDE the `with np.errstate(divide="ignore", invalid="ignore")` context, which only wraps the final S expression on lines 176-177. I confirmed at runtime that `kraichnan_grad(k, 0.0, 1e-7)` raises a 'RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in divide'. The final S is still sanitized to 0 by `np.where(np.isfinite(S), S, 0.0)`, so the *output* is correct, but the warning leaks. This directly contradicts the sibling function: batchelor_grad lines 117-122 deliberately wrap its `alpha = sqrt(2*q)*k/kB` division inside the errstate guard, and its comment (lines 117-118) states it does this to 'Mirror kraichnan_grad'. The mirroring is one-directional — kraichnan_grad itself does not suppress its own division warning. There is no test asserting kraichnan_grad is warning-free at kB=0 (test_chi.py line 85/93 only covers batchelor_grad via test_batchelor_grad_zero_kB_no_warning), which is the test gap that hides this. Impact is bounded: the production chi-from-epsilon path guards `kB < 1` in chi.py before calling grad_func, and pytest filterwarnings (pyproject.toml line 90-93) does not include 'error', so it is not fatal — purely a stray log warning plus a faithfulness inconsistency.
suggested_fix: Move the division and the `sq6q_y` computation inside the existing errstate block: indent lines 173 and 175 under `with np.errstate(divide="ignore", invalid="ignore"):` (or wrap all three lines), so kraichnan_grad suppresses its own divide-by-zero exactly as batchelor_grad does. Add a test mirroring test_batchelor_grad_zero_kB_no_warning that asserts kraichnan_grad(k, 0.0, 1e-7) raises no warning and returns finite values.
verification: Verified all claims against the code and at runtime. In src/odas_tpw/chi/batchelor.py:173-177, kraichnan_grad computes `y = k / kB` (line 173) and `sq6q_y = sq6q * y` (line 175) OUTSIDE the `with np.errstate(divide="ignore", invalid="ignore")` block that wraps only the final S expression (lines 176-177). Runtime test (`python3 -W error::RuntimeWarning`) confirms `kraichnan_grad(k, 0.0, 1e-7)` raises `RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in divide`, while `batchelor_grad(k, 0.0, 1e-7)` does NOT (its division on line 120 is inside the guard at lines 119-122). The output of kraichnan_grad is still correct because line 178 sanitizes via `np.where(np.isfinite(S), S, 0.0)`. The inconsistency is real: batchelor_grad's comment (lines 117-118) says it guards its division to "Mirror kraichnan_grad", but kraichnan_grad does not itself fully suppress its own divide warning — the mirroring is one-directional and the comment is slightly aspirational. Test gap confirmed: tests/test_chi.py:83 (test_degenerate_kB_returns_finite_zero_no_warning) covers only batchelor_grad and its docstring even references "mirroring kraichnan_grad (#16)", yet no equivalent warning-free test exists for kraichnan_grad. Non-fatal confirmed: pyproject.toml filterwarnings (lines 90-93) contains only two deprecation ignores, no "error". Production guard confirmed: chi.py:211 guards `kB < 1` before grad_func is exercised (variance correction at line 100/242 runs after that guard), so the warning is essentially never triggered in production — only via direct degenerate-input calls. The defect genuinely holds but has zero functional/output impact; it is a stray cosmetic warning plus a faithfulness/consistency inconsistency with the sibling function and its comment, on a path the production code already guards. Severity minor is appropriate (boundary with nit, since output is always correct and production never hits it).

title: fp07_transfer_batch silently treats any unknown model string as double_pole instead of raising
evidence: fp07_transfer_batch dispatches with `if model == "single_pole": return single-pole; else: return double-pole`. Any value other than the exact string 'single_pole' (e.g. a typo 'singlepole', 'double-pole', 'doublePole') silently falls through to the double-pole branch. I confirmed `fp07_transfer_batch(F, [tau], model='bogus')` returns the double-pole result with no error. This is inconsistent with fp07_tau (lines 122-129), which raises `ValueError(f"Unknown FP07 tau model: {model!r}")` for unrecognized models. The pipeline's CLI restricts --fp07-model to choices=['single_pole','double_pole'] (cli.py 858/964), but the YAML-config path is NOT value-validated: rsi/config.py validate_config only checks section/key NAMES, not values (verified — it raises only on unknown sections/keys), and config.py defaults flow `fp07_model` straight into l3_chi.py line 212 `fp07_transfer_batch(..., model=fp07_model)`. So a typo'd fp07_model in a user YAML config silently produces a different FP07 attenuation and thus a wrong chi, with no diagnostic. Direct API callers have the same exposure.
suggested_fix: Make fp07_transfer_batch reject unknown models explicitly, matching fp07_tau: `if model == "single_pole": return (1.0/(1.0+omega_tau_sq)).T; elif model == "double_pole": return (1.0/(1.0+omega_tau_sq)**2).T; else: raise ValueError(f"Unknown FP07 transfer model: {model!r}")`. Optionally also validate the value at the config layer.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL. I read src/odas_tpw/chi/fp07.py:152-178 directly. fp07_transfer_batch dispatches with `if model == "single_pole": return single-pole; else: return double-pole` (lines 175-178), so any string other than the exact "single_pole" silently produces the double-pole result. I empirically confirmed this: fp07_transfer_batch(F, tau, model='doublepole_typo') and model='singel_pole' both return values np.allclose to the double_pole branch (e.g., |H|^2 at 50 Hz = 0.083 for double vs 0.288 for single, a ~3.5x difference). This is genuinely inconsistent with fp07_tau (fp07.py:128-129), which raises ValueError(f"Unknown FP07 tau model: {model!r}") on unknown models, confirmed by running fp07_tau(0.5, model='bogus') which raised.

The end-to-end YAML exposure is also real. The CLI flag --fp07-model is argparse-restricted to choices=["single_pole","double_pole"] (cli.py 858, 964), so the CLI path is safe, but the YAML-config path is NOT value-validated: validate_config (in config_base.py:117-133, not config.py as the auditor stated — minor citation error) only checks section and key NAMES, raising solely on unknown sections/keys, never on values. A typo'd fp07_model in a user YAML therefore passes validation, flows through chi_merged.get("fp07_model", ...) (cli.py 451) and the pipeline into fp07_transfer_batch (l3_chi.py:212), silently selecting double-pole and producing a wrong chi with no diagnostic.

The defect is actually slightly worse than the finding states: on a typo, default_tau_model('typo') (fp07.py:82, same else-fallthrough pattern) returns 'lueck' (single-pole/speed-dependent tau), while fp07_transfer_batch returns the double-pole transfer function — a never-intended hybrid (double-pole |H|^2 paired with single-pole lueck tau). The same silent else-fallthrough also exists at l4_chi.py:88 and rsi/window.py:325, so the defect class is broader than the single cited function.

Severity assessment: minor is appropriate. Impact is a silently wrong chi result, which matters for scientific correctness, but the trigger requires a typo specifically in a YAML config (the CLI is guarded), and the default value is hard-coded to a valid string. It is a real robustness/silent-wrong-result gap, not a crash or a bug on the default path. The proposed fix (raise ValueError on unknown model, matching fp07_tau) is correct and would ideally be applied to default_tau_model, l4_chi.py:88, and window.py:325 as well, plus optional config-layer value validation.

title: fp07_tau ('lueck'/'peterson') is unguarded for non-positive speed, producing NaN/inf and warnings
evidence: fp07_tau('lueck') computes `0.01 * (1.0/speed)**0.5` and 'peterson' computes `0.012 * speed**(-0.32)`. With speed=0 the 'lueck' branch raises 'RuntimeWarning: divide by zero' and returns inf; with negative speed both branches raise 'RuntimeWarning: invalid value' and return NaN (confirmed at runtime). Unlike the noise-floor and wavenumber paths in l3_chi.py (lines 155-157), which floor speed at 0.01 m/s before calling fp07_tau_batch, the standalone fp07_tau/fp07_tau_batch functions perform no input validation, so a direct caller or a future pipeline path passing speed<=0 silently gets a NaN tau (and hence a NaN |H(f)|^2 and NaN chi) or an unsuppressed warning. Production impact is currently nil because the only pipeline caller floors speed upstream, hence nit severity.
suggested_fix: Either validate at the top of fp07_tau (`speed = np.asarray(speed, float); if np.any(speed <= 0): raise ValueError(...)` or floor to a small positive value with a warning), or document explicitly that callers must pass speed > 0 and rely on the upstream floor. At minimum, guard the 'lueck'/'peterson' branches so a non-positive speed does not silently emit NaN.
verification:
Confirmed at runtime and in source. fp07.py:120-130 (fp07_tau) and :149 (fp07_tau_batch) do no input validation. I executed the functions: fp07_tau(0.0,'lueck')->inf with "divide by zero" RuntimeWarning; fp07_tau(-1.0,'lueck')->NaN with "invalid value in scalar power"; fp07_tau(-1.0,'peterson')->NaN; fp07_tau(0.0,'peterson')->inf; fp07_tau_batch([-1,0,1],'lueck')->[nan, inf, 0.01] with two warnings. So the 'lueck' (0.01*(1/speed)**0.5) and 'peterson' (0.012*speed**-0.32) branches do silently produce NaN/inf and emit warnings for non-positive speed, exactly as claimed.

The finding's self-assessed "production impact currently nil" is accurate. I checked all three live callers and every one floors/abs speed before calling: l3_chi.py:157-162 sets speed_means = np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.nanmean(|pspd_rel|), nan=0.01), 0.01) before fp07_tau_batch at :211; window.py:100-103 sets W = np.mean(np.abs(speed)) and clamps to SPEED_MIN before fp07_tau at :326; quick_look.py:72-74 sets mean_speed = np.mean(np.abs(speed_fast)) floored to 0.01 before fp07_tau at :149. np.abs precludes negatives and the floors preclude zero, so no current pipeline path reaches the unguarded code.

However, fp07_tau and fp07_tau_batch are exported public API (chi/__init__.py:18-19, 39-40), and the noise paths in fp07.py themselves use explicit np.errstate(divide="ignore", invalid="ignore") guards (lines 290-298), showing the codebase otherwise treats these singularities deliberately — the tau functions are the inconsistent outlier. This is a legitimate missing-guard robustness defect on a public function, correctly and honestly scoped at nit severity (edge-case, no live impact). Not overstated; not a false positive.

title: np.real() returns a view, and _sanitize_autospectra mutates it in place — fragile aliasing into the discarded complex clean_UU
evidence: `clean_UU = _sanitize_autospectra(np.real(clean_UU))` passes a *view* into the complex `clean_UU` (np.real of a complex array is a non-copying view: verified `r.base is c` is True). `_sanitize_autospectra` then writes into that view in place (line 39: `clean_UU[..., i, i] = ...`), silently overwriting the real part of the original complex array. It is harmless today only because the original complex `clean_UU` is a local that is immediately rebound and never read again, and the returned `UU` is a *separate* array. But the code reads as if it produces a fresh sanitized array; any future use of the pre-sanitize complex result (or a reviewer assuming purity) would be silently corrupted. The unit test masks this by passing `m.copy()`, so it never exercises the view-aliasing the production callers actually rely on.
suggested_fix: Make the float conversion an explicit copy at the call sites: `clean_UU = _sanitize_autospectra(np.real(clean_UU).copy())` (or `np.real(clean_UU).astype(np.float64, copy=True)`), or document in `_sanitize_autospectra` that it mutates its argument in place and require callers to pass an owned array. This removes the hidden dependence on the complex original being dead.
verification:
All technical claims verified against the actual code and numpy behavior. (1) np.real() of a complex array returns a non-copying view: confirmed empirically — r.base is c is True, r.flags['OWNDATA'] is False, and mutating r mutates the complex original c. (2) _sanitize_autospectra mutates in place: goodman.py:39 writes `clean_UU[..., i, i] = np.where(...)` into the passed array; the unit test (tests/test_scor160_goodman.py:25,38) defensively passes m.copy(), confirming the in-place behavior. (3) Both production callers (goodman.py:201 and :267) do `clean_UU = _sanitize_autospectra(np.real(clean_UU))`, passing a view into the complex clean_UU and silently overwriting its real part.

However, the finding itself correctly states this is "harmless today," and I confirmed why it is genuinely harmless: the complex clean_UU is a fresh array (line 188 `UU - np.matmul(...)` allocates new; fallback line 194 `np.copy(UU)`), so it does NOT alias the returned UU. After the rebind at line 201/267, no surviving reference reads the pre-sanitize complex array — the bias correction (line 205) and return (line 207) use the post-rebind float array, and the returned original UU is never touched. So there is zero current correctness impact on any code path or any caller (rsi/window.py:147, scor160/l3.py:157, chi/l3_chi.py:133).

This is therefore a genuine but purely maintainability/fragility observation, not a present-day defect: the in-place-mutation-through-a-view pattern is safe only by the coincidence that the complex original is dead, the code reads as if it returns a fresh sanitized array, and the test masks the aliasing by passing a copy. The proposed fix (`.copy()` at the call sites, or documenting that _sanitize_autospectra mutates its argument) is reasonable hygiene that changes no output. The defect is real and the description is accurate, but it is the lowest-significance tier — no observable bug, correct behavior, only a latent trap for a hypothetical future edit. "nit" is the correct severity, matching the auditor's own classification.

title: Custom-window length is not validated, yielding a cryptic broadcast error instead of ODAS's clear message
evidence: `csd_matrix` accepts a user `window` via `np.asarray(window, ...)` with no length check. The ODAS reference `csd_matrix_odas.m` lines 124-126 explicitly validate `size(Window,1) == n_fft` and raise `'Window must be empty or a vector of length n_fft'`. A wrong-length window in the Python port instead fails deep inside the segment loop with `operands could not be broadcast together with shapes (256,) (128,)` (verified). It is not silent corruption — the multiply raises — but the diagnostic is opaque and the failure occurs only after detrending work, unlike MATLAB's up-front check. Internal callers always use the default window, so impact is low.
suggested_fix: After resolving `window`, add: `if window.shape != (nfft,): raise ValueError(f"window must have length nfft ({nfft}), got {window.shape}")`, mirroring csd_matrix_odas.m lines 124-126.
verification: Verified the finding against the code and empirically. (1) src/odas_tpw/scor160/spectral.py:201 resolves the user window via `np.asarray(window, dtype=np.float64)` with no length check before the segment loop multiplies it (lines 214, 235). (2) The ODAS reference odas/csd_matrix_odas.m:124-126 does explicitly validate `~isvector(Window) || (size(Window,1) ~= n_fft)` and raises `'Window must be empty or a vector of length n_fft'`, exactly as cited. So the port omits an up-front validation that the reference performs. (3) Reproduced empirically: `csd_matrix(x, None, 256, 512.0, window=np.ones(128))` raises `ValueError: operands could not be broadcast together with shapes (256,) (128,)` — a cryptic, opaque diagnostic raised deep inside the loop after detrending, versus MATLAB's clear up-front message. The finding correctly characterizes this as NOT silent corruption (the multiply raises) and accurately notes low impact: no internal caller passes a custom window (goodman.py:150,168 and l3.py use defaults; only the public `csd_odas`/`csd_matrix` API exposes the param). The defect genuinely holds as a port-faithfulness/diagnostic-quality gap, but it is the mildest possible kind: a worse error message on an externally-only-reachable code path that fails loudly and immediately rather than silently. The finding's self-assigned 'nit' severity is correct.

title: Python CSD divides BOTH endpoints by 2 for Cxx/Cyy, intentionally diverging from a MATLAB asymmetry bug (informational — Python is more correct)
evidence: ODAS `csd_matrix_odas.m` lines 257-258 divide only `Cxx(1,:,:)` (DC) and only `Cyy(end,:,:)` (Nyquist) by 2 — it never halves Cxx's Nyquist bin nor Cyy's DC bin (a long-standing asymmetry in the reference; `csd_odas.m` lines 253-254 repeat it). The Python `csd_matrix`/`csd_matrix_batch` instead halve BOTH DC and Nyquist for all three matrices (lines 247-250, 440-443), which is the physically correct single-counting for a one-sided real spectrum. This is a deliberate correction, not a regression: the ATOMIX benchmark passes and the dissipation integral band excludes the exact DC/Nyquist bins, so it has no practical effect on epsilon/chi. Reported only so the divergence is documented and not 'fixed' back to the MATLAB behavior by mistake; no action needed.
suggested_fix: No code change. Optionally add a one-line comment near lines 247-250 noting the intentional divergence from csd_matrix_odas.m's asymmetric DC/Nyquist halving so a future reviewer does not 'restore' the MATLAB bug for bit-faithfulness.
verification:
I verified every load-bearing claim against the actual source.

MATLAB reference (confirmed verbatim): odas/csd_matrix_odas.m:257-258 halves only Cxx's DC bin (`Cxx(1,:,:) = Cxx(1,:,:)/2`) and only Cyy's Nyquist bin (`Cyy(end,:,:) = Cyy(end,:,:)/2`); it never halves Cxx's Nyquist nor Cyy's DC. Cxy gets both (lines 252-253). odas/csd_odas.m:253-254 repeats the identical asymmetry for the real branch. So the asymmetry the finding cites is exactly as described.

Python (confirmed verbatim): src/odas_tpw/scor160/spectral.py:247-250 (csd_matrix) loops `for arr in (Cxx, Cyy, Cxy): arr[0]/=2; arr[-1]/=2`, halving both DC and Nyquist for all three matrices. The batched version at lines 440-443 does the same (`arr[:,0]/=2; arr[:,-1]/=2`). So Python diverges symmetrically.

Physics direction: For a one-sided real spectrum, both DC and Nyquist appear once and the symmetric halving is the correct single-counting; the finding labels Python as 'more correct,' not a regression — accurate.

No practical effect on epsilon/chi: I traced consumption. l3/l4 use only Cxy for the cross-spectrum. l4's variance integration uses `spec_safe[1:index_limit]` (l4.py:488,516), explicitly dropping the DC bin (index 0), and caps the upper integration limit via K_95/K_AA/K_LIMIT_MAX (lines 474-516), which sit well below the Nyquist wavenumber — so the Nyquist bin is excluded too. Goodman cleaning (goodman.py:184-188) does use UU=Cxx, AA=Cyy, UA=Cxy in a single linear solve; at the exact DC/Nyquist bins the MATLAB version mixes halved/unhalved conventions across the three matrices (genuinely inconsistent there), while Python is self-consistent — but those two bins are excluded from the dissipation band anyway, so the conclusion holds.

The finding is an accurate, correctly-characterized divergence that is purely informational and self-labeled as needing no code change (only an optional clarifying comment). Every factual claim checks out. It is 'real' as a documented behavioral divergence, and nit severity is correct since it has no practical effect on outputs and requires no fix.

title: despike bad-region marking under-removes one trailing sample for odd N (MATLAB N/2 float-colon + round vs Python floor)
evidence: Python uses integer floor: N_half = N // 2 (line 93), then marks lo=s-N_half .. hi=s+2*N_half+1 (exclusive) = [s-N//2, s+2*(N//2)] inclusive. ODAS despike.m halves N as float first (line 166: N = N/2) then marks round(max(1,s-N):min(len,s+2*N)) (line 217). For ODD original N, e.g. N=21: MATLAB N=10.5, removes up to s+2*10.5 = s+21; Python N_half=10, removes only up to s+20. I confirmed with MATLAB round-half-away-from-zero semantics that MATLAB covers [s-10, s+21] while Python covers [s-10, s+20] -- Python removes ONE FEWER trailing sample per spike for odd N. The leading edge agrees (both s-N//2 after rounding). The default N=round(0.04*fs) is even on common rates (512->20, 256->10), so the benchmark/default path is unaffected; this only diverges when a caller passes an odd custom N (e.g. via params.despike_sh half-width in l2.py line 121/143 where round(sh_hw*fs) can be odd).
suggested_fix: To match ODAS exactly for odd N, compute the trailing extent from the float half-width: use n_before = round(N/2) and n_after = round(N) (i.e. hi = min(len(good), s + round(N) + 1)) rather than 2*(N//2). For N=21 this yields s+21, matching MATLAB. Alternatively document that N must be even and round N up/down to even at entry. Add a regression test asserting marked-region extent for both an even and an odd N against the MATLAB rule.
verification:
Finding holds after reading both implementations and numerically reproducing the MATLAB semantics.

CODE (Python, src/odas_tpw/scor160/despike.py): line 93 `N_half = N // 2` (integer floor); lines 131-132 mark `lo = max(0, s - N_half)` and `hi = min(len(good), s + 2*N_half + 1)` (half-open), i.e. inclusive 1-based-equivalent extent [s - N//2, s + 2*(N//2)].

REFERENCE (odas/despike.m): line 166 `N = N / 2` (float), line 217 `idx = round( max(1,s-N) : min(length(good_points), s+2*N) )`. Crucially MATLAB builds the colon vector in float first, then rounds the WHOLE vector elementwise (round-half-away-from-zero).

NUMERICAL CONFIRMATION: I emulated the MATLAB colon+round and compared marked-index sets at a spike index away from edges. For odd N=21 (Nf=10.5): MATLAB float colon is 89.5,90.5,...,120.5 → rounded → indices [90,121] (32 pts); Python marks [90,120] (31 pts). MATLAB removes exactly one ADDITIONAL trailing sample (index 121). Verified the same one-extra-trailing-sample divergence for N ∈ {9,11,19,21,41}; the leading edge always agrees (round(s-10.5)=90 = s - N//2). For EVEN N (10,20,40) the index sets are identical, so the finding is correct that even N is unaffected.

DEFAULT/CALLER PATH: Default N=round(0.04*fs) is even on the common benchmark rates (512→20, 256→10, 100→4), so the ATOMIX benchmark/default path is unaffected — as the finding states. The finding even slightly understates reach: fs=1024→41 and fs=64→3 give odd default N. The l2.py shear-despike caller (src/odas_tpw/scor160/l2.py:121, `N_sh = round(sh_hw*fs) if sh_hw<1 else int(sh_hw)`) can produce odd N, so the divergence is reachable from the real pipeline.

TESTS: tests/test_scor160_despike.py only exercises N=10 (even) explicitly; no test pins odd-N marking extent, so the deviation is neither documented nor intentionally accepted.

ASSESSMENT: Genuine ODAS port-faithfulness off-by-one. Impact is a single-sample boundary shift at the trailing edge of each spike's replacement region (a local-mean fill of ~30 samples), only when an odd N is passed; the benchmark default rates use even N. Real but small.

title: despike replacement value diverges from ODAS when one averaging side has no valid neighbors (Python finite/0.0 vs MATLAB NaN)
evidence: ODAS despike.m always computes start_value = sum(points)/length(points) AND stop_value = sum(points)/length(points), then dv(start:stop) = (start_value+stop_value)/2 (lines 237,243,245). When one side has no valid points, MATLAB evaluates sum([])/length([]) = 0/0 = NaN, so the gap is filled with NaN. The Python port instead branches (lines 157-164): if only one side has valid points it uses that side's mean, and if neither side has points it writes 0.0. This is a genuine behavioral divergence: identical input that yields NaN in ODAS yields a finite value in Python. Practical impact is very low because the reflected end-padding (lines 99-105) almost always guarantees valid neighbors on both sides -- I could not construct a realistic case that reaches the one-sided/zero branch -- and Python's behavior is arguably MORE robust than ODAS's NaN. But it is an undocumented faithfulness gap not noted in code comments.
suggested_fix: Either (a) add a comment explicitly stating this is an intentional, more-robust deviation from ODAS (which would fill with NaN), or (b) if exact ODAS parity is desired, write NaN when a side is empty. Given downstream code does not expect NaN gaps, option (a) plus a one-line comment is preferred. Low priority.
verification:
Verified against both implementations. ODAS despike.m:237,243,245 unconditionally computes start_value=sum(points)/length(points) and stop_value=sum(points)/length(points), then dv(start:stop)=(start_value+stop_value)/2; when one side is empty MATLAB evaluates 0/0=NaN, so the gap is filled with NaN. Python despike.py:157-164 branches instead: 'both' -> mean of both, 'before_only'/'after_only' -> single-side mean, 'neither' -> 0.0. So identical input that yields NaN in ODAS yields a finite value in Python. The divergence and cited line numbers are accurate.

Reachability: I empirically confirmed (1) over 200 realistic random trials with spikes injected at the very start/end, only the 'both' branch is ever hit (736 times, 0 divergences); the reflective padding (pad_len up to 2*floor(fs/smooth)=2048 for fs=512, or full length for short signals) plus the transition invariant (good[s_bad-1] is True whenever s_bad>0; good[e_bad] is True whenever e_bad<len) guarantees both neighbor regions are non-empty. (2) The one-sided/neither branches ARE reachable, but only with contrived signals of length ~2-60 where pad_len<=N_half — I forced 186 one-sided and 50 neither occurrences with such tiny inputs. These do not occur as despike inputs in this codebase: all real callers (scor160/l2.py:123,145, chi/l2_chi.py:105, rsi/dissipation.py) despike whole profile sections of thousands+ samples at fs~512 Hz, far above the threshold.

Impact: essentially nil. No correctness consequence; downstream code does not expect NaN gaps, and the codebase deliberately added NaN-safe guards (despike.py:84, l2.py:134) treating NaN propagation as a hazard. Python's finite/0.0 fill is arguably more robust than ODAS's NaN, as the finding concedes. The substantive ask reduces to adding a one-line comment documenting the intentional deviation — and indeed no such comment exists near despike.py:157-164. This is a genuine, code-verifiable port-faithfulness/documentation gap a reviewer could legitimately flag, but with no real-world trigger and no behavioral harm. The author's own 'nit' classification is correct.

title: despike raises uncaught filtfilt ValueError on very short arrays (n<=2 at default fs); no guard or test
evidence: For n<=2 with default fs=512, despike raises 'ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6' from scipy filtfilt. The reflected padding makes total length 3*n (when n < 2*int(fs/smooth)); filtfilt's default padlen for a 1st-order Butterworth is 3*max(len(a),len(b))=6, so n must be >2. n=1 and n=2 fail; n>=3 works. This mirrors MATLAB filtfilt (which would also fail on length-2 input), and real callers (l2.py, l2_chi.py) operate on long sections, so it is not a live blocker. However the failure mode is an opaque scipy exception rather than a documented precondition, and there is no test exercising n<=2 (tests/test_scor160_despike.py min array is 5000). A degenerate/empty section in a future caller would abort the file with a cryptic message.
suggested_fix: Add an early guard: if the (padded) length is <= filtfilt's padlen requirement (or simply len(signal) < 3), return the input unchanged with n_passes=0, spike_indices=empty, fraction=0.0, optionally warning. Add a unit test for n in {0,1,2,3} asserting graceful behavior.
verification: Verified by direct reproduction and code reading. Reproduction: despike(np.random.randn(n), fs) at /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/scor160/despike.py raises `ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6.` for n in {0,1,2} and succeeds for n>=3, identical across fs in {64,512,1024}. The boundary is length-based (n>2), not fs-dependent, because despike.py:98 sets `pad_len = min(length, 2*int(fs/smooth))`, so for tiny arrays the reflected-padded length is 3*length, and scipy filtfilt (despike.py:110, 114) requires padded length > padlen=6, i.e. 3*length>6 => length>2. No guard exists before either filtfilt call. tests/test_scor160_despike.py uses a minimum array of 5000 samples (lines 25/35 etc.), so nothing exercises n<=2 — the no-test claim holds. Live-caller analysis confirms it is not a blocker: scor160/l2.py (lines 122-156) and chi/l2_chi.py despike diss-length sections selected by `mask = section_number == sec_id` (thousands of samples); perturb/plot/diagnostics.py:125 explicitly guards with `if sig.size < 16: return None` before despike at line 132; rsi/viewer_base.py:580 despikes full-profile segments (physically thousands of samples), and although its `len(seg) > 2*int(fs)` guard at line 576 only protects the HP filter (not despike), a <=2-sample profile is not a realistic input. Port-faithfulness also confirmed: odas/despike.m line 184 calls filtfilt with no length guard either, and MATLAB filtfilt imposes the same length>3*order constraint, so the Python port faithfully reproduces MATLAB behavior. Conclusion: the defect genuinely exists (opaque scipy exception on degenerate input, no precondition documented, no test), but it cannot be triggered by any current caller, mirrors the reference implementation, and at worst aborts one file with a slightly cryptic message rather than producing wrong scientific results. This is a defensive-coding/robustness gap, correctly self-rated nit.

title: compare_l2 raises ValueError on empty section_number (no n_common guard like compare_l3/l4)
evidence: compare_l2 unconditionally evaluates `int(reference.section_number.max())` and `int(computed.section_number.max())`. On an empty array numpy raises `ValueError: zero-size array to reduction operation maximum which has no identity` (verified directly). compare_l3 (line 185-187) and compare_l4 (line 294-296) both guard with `if n_common == 0: return results`, but compare_l2 has no equivalent empty guard. This is now reachable: the round-2 fix made `_select_sections` return an empty array on empty input (l2.py:228-230), so `process_l2` on an empty/degenerate L1 yields an empty L2 whose section_number is size 0, and comparing it crashes here instead of producing a degenerate report. Benchmark data is never empty, bounding severity.
suggested_fix: Guard the section block: `if ref_sec.size == 0 or comp_sec.size == 0: results['section'] = {...zeros...}; return results` (or use `int(ref_sec.max()) if ref_sec.size else 0`), mirroring the `n_common == 0` early-return already used in compare_l3/compare_l4.
verification:
Verified the code. In src/odas_tpw/scor160/compare.py:50-52, compare_l2 unconditionally evaluates int(ref_sec.max()) and int(comp_sec.max()) with no empty-array guard. I confirmed directly that numpy raises "ValueError: zero-size array to reduction operation maximum which has no identity" on a size-0 array (python3 -c with np.zeros(0).max()). By contrast compare_l3 (line 186-187) and compare_l4 (line 295-296) both early-return via "if n_common == 0: return results", so the asymmetry is real. The empty path is genuinely reachable: _select_sections (l2.py:228-230) now returns an empty section_number array on empty input (the #19 fix), so an empty/degenerate L1 produces a well-formed empty L2 whose section_number is size 0, and comparing it crashes here. The proposed guard (ref_sec.size==0 or comp_sec.size==0 early-return, mirroring n_common==0) is correct.

However, practical impact is essentially nil. The only callers are the CLI benchmark commands (cli.py:109/128/157), which always operate on real ATOMIX .nc files loaded via read_atomix(path); these datasets are never empty (the finding itself concedes this). No test exercises an empty compare_l2 — all TestCompareL2 cases (tests/test_scor160_compare.py:65-92) use non-empty _make_l2(). So while the defect technically holds and the fix is a legitimate consistency/robustness cleanup that aligns compare_l2 with its siblings and preserves the intent of the _select_sections empty-input fix, it cannot affect any realistic invocation. That makes it a nit rather than minor: a defensive-coding inconsistency with no real-world failure mode.

title: scor160 L4 ignores the ODAS f_limit constraint when forming K_AA (uses 0.9*f_AA only)
evidence: l4.py computes `K_AA = 0.9 * f_AA / max(W, 0.05)`. ODAS reduces f_AA the same way (`f_AA = 0.9*f_AA`, get_diss_odas.m:372-373) so the 0.9 factor is faithful, but ODAS additionally applies `if f_limit < f_AA, f_AA = f_limit` (line 374) before forming `K_AA = f_AA/W` (line 505). The RSI sibling honors this (`f_AA_eff = F_AA_MARGIN*f_AA; if f_limit < f_AA_eff: f_AA_eff = f_limit`, dissipation.py:137-139), but the scor160 benchmark path has no f_limit handling. For ATOMIX benchmark datasets f_limit is generally non-constraining (the AA cutoff dominates), so this almost never changes the integration limit, bounding severity to a documentation/faithfulness nit.
suggested_fix: Accept an optional `f_limit` in process_l4/_estimate_epsilon and apply `f_AA_eff = min(0.9*f_AA, f_limit)` to match get_diss_odas.m:372-374 and the rsi/dissipation.py behavior, or document explicitly that the scor160 path assumes f_limit is non-constraining for ATOMIX inputs.
verification:
Verified against source. l4.py:180 is exactly `K_AA = 0.9 * f_AA / max(W, 0.05)`; neither process_l4 (l4.py:86-100) nor _estimate_epsilon (l4.py:276-286) accept an f_limit, and the scor160 CLI caller (cli.py:144-146) passes only f_AA. ODAS get_diss_odas.m:372-374 confirmed to apply BOTH `f_AA=0.9*f_AA` AND `if f_limit < f_AA, f_AA = f_limit` before forming `K_AA = f_AA/W` (line 505). The RSI sibling rsi/dissipation.py:137-139 honors this with `f_AA_eff = F_AA_MARGIN*f_AA; if f_limit < f_AA_eff: f_AA_eff = f_limit`. So the finding's factual claim is accurate: the scor160 path drops the f_limit clamp that both ODAS and the RSI sibling apply.

However, the practical impact is negligible, which the auditor correctly acknowledges. ODAS's default f_limit is `inf` (get_diss_odas.m:266, default_f_limit = inf). The ATOMIX benchmark NetCDF files expose only f_AA (io.py:417, default 98.0) and carry no f_limit attribute, and the scor160 CLI never supplies one. Thus for every benchmark and CLI invocation, `min(0.9*f_AA, f_limit)` reduces to `0.9*f_AA` and K_AA is bit-identical to ODAS. The divergence only manifests if a caller passes a finite f_limit smaller than 0.9*f_AA, which the scor160 path provides no parameter to do. This is a genuine but inconsequential faithfulness/documentation gap — exactly a nit, matching the claimed severity.

title: NaN in T/C poisons CT lag estimate, yielding the maximally-wrong -max_lag_seconds shift (guards don't catch NaN)
category: numerical-correctness / NaN edge handling / silent data corruption
evidence:
A single non-finite sample anywhere in a profile's T or C segment corrupts the entire lag estimate, identical to the flatline failure mode the audit-#42 guard was added to prevent. Chain (verified by running ct_align on real-shaped inputs):
- Line 77-78 apply lfilter (a 2nd-order IIR Butterworth). IIR filters propagate a single NaN to ALL subsequent output samples, so dx/dy become all-NaN after the first bad sample.
- Line 81: norm = sqrt(sum(dx**2)*sum(dy**2)) -> NaN.
- Line 82: `if norm <= 0:` is FALSE for NaN (NaN comparisons are always False), so the degenerate profile is NOT skipped. The very comment on lines 83-87 describes exactly this failure (argmax of a non-informative correlation picks index 0 = -max_lag), yet the guard only catches norm==0, not NaN.
- Line 88: corr = corr/norm -> all-NaN.
- Line 97: np.argmax(np.abs(corr)) of an all-NaN array returns 0 -> lag = lags[0] = -max_lag_samples (the most-negative lag). max_corr is also NaN.
- Line 118-124: that profile's weight = max_corr*n_samples = NaN; np.cumsum -> NaN; total is NaN; line 121 `if total <= 0:` is FALSE for NaN (same bug); np.searchsorted(cum_w, NaN/2) returns 0 -> median_lag = the NaN-poisoned profile's -max_lag_seconds.

Measured: single-profile sine with one NaN in C and max_lag_seconds=0.5 -> returned lag = -0.5 s (should be ~0). Two-profile case (one clean profile with true 5-sample lag + one profile with a single injected NaN) -> returned lag = -320 samples = -5.0 s (default max_lag) instead of -5 samples (-0.078 s); the valid profile's consensus is entirely discarded.

This is a realistic field-data path, not theoretical: the default conductivity channel converter convert_jac_c (rsi/channels.py:231-242) deliberately emits np.nan for any corrupt sample (v_part==0), and pipeline.py:1049 calls ct_align directly on pf.channels[C_name] (JAC_C) with NO NaN stripping beforehand. A -5 s CT misalignment grossly desynchronizes C from T, producing spiked/garbage salinity and density in the CTD product and corrupting the N2/dT/dz stratification computed right after (pipeline.py:1064+).

Test gap that hides this: tests/test_processing_ct_align.py has test_flatlined_channel_does_not_return_max_lag (norm==0 path) but NO test that injects a NaN, so the NaN twin of the same defect is uncovered.
suggested_fix: Treat non-finite the same as flatlined. (1) At line 82 change the guard to `if not np.isfinite(norm) or norm <= 0:` (or pre-filter: skip the profile if `not np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) or not np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))`, since lfilter would smear any NaN regardless). (2) At line 121 change to `if not np.isfinite(total) or total <= 0:`. Optionally make weights robust by dropping non-finite entries from per_profile before the weighted-median (filter `[p for p in per_profile if np.isfinite(p['max_corr'])]`). Add a regression test that injects a single NaN into C within an otherwise-good profile and asserts the returned lag equals the clean-data consensus (and that a NaN-only profile is skipped rather than returning -max_lag_seconds).
verification:
CONFIRMED real. I read src/odas_tpw/processing/ct_align.py and executed the code on real-shaped inputs; every link in the claimed chain reproduces.

Mechanism (all verified by running the actual code):
- Line 82 `if norm <= 0:` and line 121 `if total <= 0:` both evaluate False for NaN (NaN comparisons are always False — confirmed `np.float64(nan) <= 0` -> False). Neither guard catches a NaN.
- Lines 77-78 apply a 2nd-order Butterworth via scipy lfilter (IIR). I confirmed lfilter smears one input NaN to ALL subsequent outputs (single NaN at index 40 -> outputs 40..99 all NaN). So dx/dy go NaN -> norm = sqrt(sum(dx**2)*sum(dy**2)) = NaN.
- Line 88 corr/norm all-NaN; line 97 np.argmax(np.abs(all-NaN)) returns 0 -> lag = lags[0] = -max_lag_samples (confirmed argmax of all-NaN == 0). max_corr NaN.
- Lines 118-124: weight = max_corr*n_samples = NaN -> cumsum NaN -> total NaN -> `total <= 0` False -> searchsorted(cum_w, NaN/2) returns 0 -> median_lag = the NaN-poisoned profile's -max_lag_seconds.

End-to-end measured outputs match the finding exactly: single-profile sine with one NaN in C and max_lag_seconds=0.5 -> returned lag = -0.5 s (should be ~0). Two-profile case (clean profile with true 5-sample lag + one profile with a single injected NaN, default max_lag=5.0) -> returned lag = -5.0 s = -320 samples, completely discarding the valid profile's correct 5/64 s (~0.078 s) consensus.

This is the exact NaN twin of the flatline (norm==0) failure that the audit-#42 guard was added for; the comment on lines 83-87 describes precisely this argmax->index-0->-max_lag failure, but the guard only catches norm==0, not NaN. tests/test_processing_ct_align.py has test_flatlined_channel_does_not_return_max_lag but NO NaN-injection test (its only isfinite check is on clean data), so the NaN twin is genuinely uncovered.

Realistic field path confirmed: convert_jac_c (rsi/channels.py:238) deliberately emits np.nan via np.where(v_part==0, np.nan, ...) for corrupt samples; pipeline.py:1049 calls ct_align directly on pf.channels[C_name] (default JAC_C) with no NaN stripping, and the result feeds the CTD product and the N2/dTdz stratification computed immediately after (pipeline.py:1059+). A -5 s misalignment silently desynchronizes C from T.

Severity: major (not blocker). It is silent data corruption of a primary derived product, but it only triggers when a corrupt conductivity sample (v_part==0) actually occurs in a profile segment, not on every run; it does not crash. The proposed fix (treat non-finite like flatlined at both guards, and/or drop non-finite per_profile entries before the weighted median, plus a NaN regression test) is correct and minimal.

title: Bottom-crash aborts entirely on first length-mismatched channel; top_trim instead skips it (asymmetric contract, can silently disable seafloor-impact trimming)
evidence: In detect_bottom_crash the per-channel loop does `if len(a) != len(depth): return None` (bottom.py:82-83), so a SINGLE mismatched vibration axis aborts crash detection for the whole profile and leaves the seafloor impact in the data. The sibling routine compute_trim_depth makes the opposite choice: `if len(data) != len(depth): continue` (top_trim.py:81-82), skipping the bad channel and using the rest. The real caller builds the vibration dict from whatever of (Ax, Ay, Az) exist (perturb/pipeline.py:421-424) and the trim dict from (sh1, sh2, Ax, Ay) (pipeline.py:391-393), so the two stages have inconsistent failure semantics for the same class of input. Reproduced: detect_bottom_crash(depth(5000), {'Ax':len5000_with_spike, 'Ay':len500}, ...) returns None (crash NOT trimmed) whereas compute_trim_depth(depth, {'sh1':good, 'bad':len3}) still returns 11.0. For normal RSI .p data all fast channels share one demultiplexed length so this rarely fires in production for VMP-250 SN479, hence low real-world trigger probability — but the divergent contract is a latent robustness defect and the silent-skip-vs-silent-abort asymmetry is surprising.
suggested_fix: Make bottom.py match top_trim's skip-and-continue policy: skip a mismatched channel rather than aborting, e.g. replace the early `return None` with `continue` and require at least one usable channel afterward (`if mag_sq is None: return None`). Alternatively, standardize both routines on one documented policy and add a test covering a mismatched-axis-plus-good-axis case for bottom (current test_mismatched_channel_length_returns_none only exercises the single-channel mismatch).
verification:
I read both cited files and confirmed every factual claim. bottom.py:80-86 forms the magnitude across channels and does `if len(a) != len(depth): return None` (line 82-83), so a single length-mismatched vibration axis aborts crash detection for the entire profile, returning None (= "no crash detected") and leaving the seafloor impact in the data. The sibling top_trim.py:79-84 makes the opposite choice: `if len(data) != len(depth): continue` (line 81-82), skipping the bad channel and proceeding with the rest. The callers confirm both stages receive analogously-built dicts: pipeline.py:421-424 builds the vibration dict from whatever of (Ax,Ay,Az) exist; pipeline.py:391-393 builds the trim dict from (sh1,sh2,Ax,Ay). So the two stages genuinely have inconsistent failure semantics for the same input class, and this is a real asymmetric-contract defect. The author clearly intends these two routines to mirror each other (bottom.py:128 comment explicitly references aligning bin-center behavior with top_trim), which strengthens the "inconsistency is a defect" case.

However, the practical impact is minimal and the finding admits as much. In production, all fast channels are demultiplexed from the same PFile address matrix and sliced with the identical [s_fast:e_fast] bounds against the same P_fast array (pipeline.py:424,386), so Ax/Ay/depth_seg are structurally guaranteed equal-length; the mismatch branch is effectively dead code for the only data this repo processes (VMP-250 SN479). The finding's repro requires hand-constructing channels of differing lengths, which the real caller never produces. The "silent" framing is fair though: the return-None path is not an exception, so the pipeline's try/except (line 415) does not log it. Because the real-world trigger probability is essentially nil and the consequence is a consistency/robustness gap rather than any observed data corruption, I downgrade the claimed "minor" to "nit." The existing test (test_processing_bottom.py:123) only covers the single-channel mismatch, confirming the proposed "good-axis + bad-axis" case is uncovered. Real defect, correctly described, but lowest-tier severity.

title: test_bin_edges_too_few_returns_none does not actually exercise the `len(bin_edges) < 2` guard it claims to cover; that branch is untested
evidence: The test docstring/comment states 'min_depth==max_depth produces fewer than 2 bin edges' and references the `len(bin_edges) < 2 -> return None` guard (top_trim.py:74-75). But with dz=1.0, min_depth=max_depth=10.0, bin_edges = np.arange(10-0.5, 10+1.0, 1.0) = [9.5, 10.5] — TWO edges, so the guard is NOT hit. The function returns None via the unrelated `np.sum(valid) < 3` path (top_trim.py:93) instead. Verified by direct reproduction. The genuine `len(bin_edges) < 2` condition only arises when max_depth < min_depth - 3*dz/2 (arange yields 0 or 1 edge), which no test covers, so that real guard is dead-to-tests and a future regression there would go unnoticed.
suggested_fix: Add an explicit test with inverted bounds that actually empties bin_edges, e.g. compute_trim_depth(depth, {'sh1': sh1}, dz=1.0, min_depth=20.0, max_depth=10.0) (np.arange(19.5, 11.0, 1.0) is empty -> len < 2 -> None), and correct the misleading comment on the existing test to describe the real (<3 valid stds) reason it returns None.
verification: Verified by direct reproduction. In tests/test_processing_top_trim.py:55-63, test_bin_edges_too_few_returns_none calls compute_trim_depth with min_depth=10.0, max_depth=10.0, dz=1.0. The function computes bin_edges = np.arange(min_depth - dz/2, max_depth + dz, dz) = np.arange(9.5, 11.0, 1.0) = [9.5, 10.5] — TWO edges. So the `len(bin_edges) < 2` guard at top_trim.py:74-75 evaluates False and is NOT hit. Instead, tracing through the function: only one bin exists, _bin_std yields one finite std, so np.sum(valid) == 1 < 3 at top_trim.py:93, and the function returns None at line 103 (no trim_depths). The test's docstring ('produces fewer than 2 bin edges') and inline comment ('bin_edges has 1 entry → return None (line 67)') are both factually wrong: the path is different, and even the cited line number is wrong (the guard is at line 74, not 67). I confirmed the genuine guard requires an empty/single bin_edges array: the proposed inverted-bounds case min_depth=20.0, max_depth=10.0 gives np.arange(19.5, 11.0, 1.0) = [] (len 0), which truly hits the guard. Grepping the whole test file shows no other test exercises this branch, so the `len(bin_edges) < 2` guard is dead-to-tests. The finding holds: it is a real test-coverage gap plus a misleading comment/docstring. Severity nit is correct — the production guard itself functions correctly; only the test fails to exercise it and mis-documents why it returns None.

title: Bottom-crash reports the deepest bin's CENTER, which can lie below the deepest real sample (partial last bin over-read)
evidence: bins = np.arange(depth_minimum, max_depth + bin_size, bin_size) (bottom.py:98) creates a final bin whose right edge can exceed nanmax(depth) by up to bin_size, and the crash depth is reported as 0.5*(bins[i]+bins[i+1]) (bottom.py:129). For a profile ending at 100.5 m with depth_minimum=10, depth_window=4 the deepest bin is [98,102) and its center 100.0 is reported — and edges like [98,102) can place the center beyond real max depth. The caller (perturb/pipeline.py:434) then does `np.where(P_seg >= bottom_depth)`; if the reported center exceeds max(P_seg) the match set is empty and the crash is silently not trimmed. In practice this is bounded/benign because a bin needs counts>1 (>=2 samples) to have a finite std and be selectable, so the deepest eligible bin's center sits below the second-deepest sample; hence I could not construct a realistic case that drops a crash. Reporting accuracy is still off by up to ~half a bin width near the true bottom.
suggested_fix: Clamp the reported crash depth to the data, e.g. bottom_depth = min(0.5*(bins[i]+bins[i+1]), float(max_depth)), or report the mean/max of the actual depth samples that fall in the selected bin instead of the geometric bin center, so the value never exceeds the deepest observed sample.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL, and the finding actually understates the impact. (Note: the cited caller path src/perturb/pipeline.py is wrong; the real file is src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py, lines 426-438 — but the code matches the finding's claims.)

Mechanism confirmed by reading the code:
1. bottom.py:98 `bins = np.arange(depth_minimum, max_depth + bin_size, bin_size)` builds bin EDGES; the deepest bin [bins[-2], bins[-1]) overhangs nanmax(depth) by up to bin_size.
2. bottom.py:129 reports the crash as that bin's geometric CENTER `0.5*(bins[i]+bins[i+1])`.
3. A bottom crash by definition occurs at the deepest point, so the spike lands in the deepest (overhanging) bin, which is densely filled at fs_fast=512 Hz (thousands of samples) and therefore eligible (counts>1) and selected.
4. The center exceeds the max real sample whenever max_depth falls in the lower half of the deepest bin — geometrically ~50% of cases.

I empirically confirmed with the real detect_bottom_crash function: over 20000 realistic monotonic-descent profiles with a crash spike, the returned depth exceeded nanmax(depth) in 10065/20000 trials. A single trace (bottom=98.5 m): deepest bin [98,102), center=100.0 reported, i.e. 1.5 m BELOW the deepest real sample.

Consequence confirmed: the caller (odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:433-438) does `below = np.where(P_seg >= bottom_depth)[0]; if len(below) > 0: ...`. Since P_slow and the fast depth_seg derive from the same pressure record (same max), when the center > max(P_slow) the match is EMPTY, the `if len(below) > 0` guard is False, `new_e` stays at e_slow, and the crash is SILENTLY NOT TRIMMED. Simulating the full caller logic: ~49.6% of detections fail to trim. There is no clamp or fallback anywhere in the pipeline.

The finding's hedge ("I could not construct a realistic case that drops a crash" / "the deepest eligible bin's center sits below the second-deepest sample") is FALSE: the bin center sits ABOVE max_depth, not below, whenever the bottom falls in the lower half of the overhanging bin. By contrast, top_trim.py:71 deliberately builds CENTERED edges (`arange(min_depth - dz/2, ...)`) so its bin_centers stay valid; bottom.py uses raw edges and overhangs, so the comment at bottom.py:126-128 invoking the top_trim analogy is misleading — top_trim does not overhang.

The proposed fix (clamp bottom_depth to min(center, max_depth), or report the max/mean of actual samples in the selected bin) is correct and sufficient.

Impact: this silently defeats bottom-crash trimming in roughly half of detections, leaving seafloor-impact-contaminated samples in the deepest epsilon/chi estimates with no warning — the exact contamination the feature exists to remove. That is more than a nit.

title: Inter-probe rejection diverges from benchmark-validated ATOMIX/Lueck rule (symmetric drop-furthest vs anchor-to-minimum), giving up to ~300x higher combined epsilon/chi for 2-probe disagreements
evidence: The benchmark-pinned reference (src/odas_tpw/scor160/l4.py:719-746, _compute_flags) implements the ATOMIX/Rockland convention as a single vectorized pass: `ln(e_i / e_min) > diss_ratio_limit * mean(sigma_ln)` flags ONLY probes too high above the window minimum (e_min is the trusted baseline), then EPSI_FINAL = geometric mean of unflagged probes (l4.py:766-779). mk_epsilon_mean instead (epsilon_combine.py:142,158-163) uses the full max/min spread `ratio=|ln(max)-ln(min)|` for the outside test and drops the probe FURTHEST from the cross-probe ln-MEAN (symmetric: a high probe is as removable as a low), iterating one drop at a time. I verified the divergence numerically: for two probes {1e-8, 1e-3}, the reference flags the high probe and returns e_min=1e-8, while mk_epsilon_mean keeps both and returns the geometric mean 3.16e-6 (316x higher). The same divergence applies to mk_chi_mean (chi_combine.py:163-188). Note: for the 3-probe one-LOW-junk case mk_epsilon_mean is actually SUPERIOR to the literal reference (mk returns 1e-8; the e_min-anchored reference returns the junk 1e-12), so the rewrite's intent is sound — but it is no longer the benchmark rule and the rest of the package (scor160 L4, mixing_look) follows the e_min-anchored convention, so the same campaign now combines probes two different ways depending on code path.
suggested_fix: Make the divergence explicit and reconciled: either (a) document in both module docstrings that mk_*_mean intentionally departs from the ATOMIX e_min-anchored rule used in scor160/l4.py, with the physical justification (a low reading can be real, so do not anchor to the minimum), and confirm the perturb pipeline and scor160 path are not expected to agree; or (b) adopt the reference's hybrid — flag relative to e_min but exclude the e_min probe itself from being the sole survivor when the cluster sits above it. At minimum, cross-reference DEFAULT_DISS_RATIO_LIMIT/l4._compute_flags so a future reader sees the two rules are deliberately different and not an accidental drift.
verification:
Verified the finding holds. Two distinct multi-probe combining rules coexist and are applied to the same campaign instrument with no reconciliation.

PATH 1 (benchmark-validated, e_min-anchored): scor160/l4.py:740-746 (_compute_flags) computes e_min=nanmin(epsi), ln_ratio=log(epsi/e_min), and flags bit 4 where ln_ratio > diss_ratio_limit*mean(sigma_ln) — flagging ONLY probes above the window minimum (docstring l4.py:719-721 explicitly: "only the larger (suspect) estimates are flagged, never the window minimum, matching the benchmark convention"). EPSI_FINAL (l4.py:766-779) is the geomean of unflagged probes. Used by the RSI pipeline (rsi/pipeline.py:27 -> process_l4), rsi/dissipation.py, and rsi/mixing_look.py:136.

PATH 2 (symmetric drop-furthest): processing/epsilon_combine.py:142 uses ratio=|log(max)-log(min)| for the outside test, then (lines 157-163) drops the probe FURTHEST from the cross-probe ln-mean, gated by n_probes>=3 (line 136) and finite_count>2 (line 151). Identical mirror in chi_combine.py:163-188. Used by the perturb pipeline (perturb/pipeline.py:1264 mk_epsilon_mean, :1364 mk_chi_mean).

NUMERICAL DIVERGENCE REPRODUCED: I ran both paths on 2 probes {1e-8,1e-3} with identical sigma_ln. Reference l4 -> flags [0,4], EPSI_FINAL=1e-8 (e_min). mk_epsilon_mean -> keeps both, returns geomean 3.16e-6 = exactly 316.2x higher. Both use the same threshold constant 1.96*sqrt(2)=2.772 (DEFAULT_DISS_RATIO_LIMIT, l4.py:78). The mk spread ratio (11.5) far exceeds the CI threshold (0.998), yet the n_probes>=3 gate means the CI loop is DEAD CODE for 2 probes — neither probe is ever rejected.

OPERATIVE REGIME CONFIRMED: The campaign instrument VMP-250 SN 479 has exactly 2 shear channels (sh1, sh2 — verified by loading VMP/ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0002.p). So the perturb pipeline runs in the 2-probe regime where mk NEVER rejects a disagreeing probe; this is the operative behavior for the real campaign, not a corner case.

The finding is honest and not overstated: I confirmed its counterpoint that for the 3-probe one-LOW-junk case {1e-12,1e-8,1e-8}, mk returns the correct 1e-8 while the literal e_min-anchored reference returns the junk 1e-12 (flagging the two GOOD probes as "too high" above the junk minimum). So mk's physical intent is sound — this is a reconciliation/documentation defect, not a pure computational bug.

NO EXISTING RECONCILIATION: grep found no cross-reference between epsilon_combine/chi_combine and l4/_compute_flags/DEFAULT_DISS_RATIO_LIMIT. Worse, epsilon_combine.py:128 mislabels the symmetric rule as "the Lueck/Rockland mk_diss_mean rule," while l4.py claims its DIFFERENT e_min rule "matches the benchmark convention" — both cite the same authority for opposite behaviors.

SEVERITY: Downgraded from major to minor. It is genuinely real and matters (same campaign combined two ways; mislabeled provenance comment), but: (1) the proposed fix is documentation/reconciliation, not a corrected computation, since mk's behavior is defensible and superior in the low-junk case; (2) the 300x is a worst-case bound under extreme probe disagreement (1e-8 vs 1e-3) that upstream FOM/variance-resolved QC would frequently reject before combining; (3) the two paths are separate pipelines, so they don't silently overwrite each other. Real correctness/maintainability concern, bounded practical impact — minor.

title: np.nanmean/nanmin/nanmax RuntimeWarnings ("Mean of empty slice", "All-NaN slice encountered") leak from the combine functions on any NaN row, including the common case of a single NaN in nu
evidence: The `np.errstate(invalid="ignore", divide="ignore")` contexts suppress only FP exceptions, NOT the Python `warnings`-module RuntimeWarnings that np.nanmean/np.nanmin/np.nanmax emit on all-NaN slices. I reproduced leaks at epsilon_combine.py:123 (`mu_sigma=np.nanmean(...)`), :140/:141 (nanmin/nanmax in the loop), :158 (`ln_mean=np.nanmean`), :167 (`epsilon_mean=np.exp(np.nanmean(...))`). This fires not only on exotic all-NaN rows but on the common case of a single NaN in `nu` (line 123: a NaN-nu time step makes that row's sigma_ln_epsilon all-NaN, tripping 'Mean of empty slice'). The same leaks occur in chi_combine.py:167,168,183,191. Results are correct (NaN where expected) but the warning spam pollutes logs and would break any caller running under `-W error::RuntimeWarning`.
suggested_fix: Wrap the np.nan* reductions with `warnings.catch_warnings(); warnings.simplefilter('ignore', RuntimeWarning)` (or use `np.errstate` is insufficient — must use the warnings module). Alternatively guard the all-NaN rows explicitly. The existing tests already exercise NaN rows but assert only on values, not warning cleanliness, so this slipped through.
verification:
VERIFIED TRUE. I reproduced every claim against the actual code on branch improve/precision-remaining.

Mechanism: np.errstate only controls FP exception flags, not warnings-module RuntimeWarnings. Direct test confirms that `with np.errstate(invalid="ignore", divide="ignore"): np.nanmean(all_nan_row)` still raises "Mean of empty slice" and np.nanmin/nanmax still raise "All-NaN slice encountered" (numpy 2.4.3).

Epsilon path (src/odas_tpw/processing/epsilon_combine.py): Running mk_epsilon_mean under DEFAULT warning filters on a dataset with a single NaN in `nu` plus an all-NaN epsilon row printed RuntimeWarnings to stderr attributed to exactly the cited lines: :123 (Mean of empty slice), :140 and :141 (All-NaN slice encountered), :158 (Mean of empty slice), :167 (Mean of empty slice). The common-case trigger is real: a NaN in nu propagates through L_K (line 107) into sigma_ln_epsilon, making that row all-NaN, so np.nanmean(sigma_ln_epsilon, axis=1) at line 123 hits an empty slice even with a single NaN-nu step and zero all-NaN epsilon rows (verified independently — line 123 fires on the single-NaN-in-nu case alone; 140/141/158 additionally require an all-NaN epsilon row, which the finding correctly distinguishes).

Chi path (src/odas_tpw/processing/chi_combine.py): Same test on mk_chi_mean emitted RuntimeWarnings at the cited :167, :168, :183, :191 (plus :146, the chi analog of epsilon:123 that the finding did not separately cite).

Results stay numerically correct (epsilonMean/chiMean = NaN on the bad rows). No correctness impact.

Tests: pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options] filterwarnings contains only two deprecation ignores, NOT "error", so the warnings leak silently in the suite and the combine tests assert only on values — matching the finding's "slipped through" claim. A caller running under -W error::RuntimeWarning would crash.

The finding is accurate in mechanism, exact line numbers, trigger condition, and severity framing; not overstated. Impact is log pollution and -W error breakage, no correctness consequence, so minor (bordering nit) is appropriate.

title: Reported epsilonLnSigma/chiLnSigma includes dropped-outlier probes' sigma (computed once before iterative removal)
evidence: mu_sigma is computed once at line 123 (`np.nanmean(sigma_ln_epsilon, axis=1)`) before the removal loop at lines 136-163, and is what gets written as epsilonLnSigma (lines 174-186). For the CF95 THRESHOLD this matches the reference (l4.py:742-743 also averages sigma over all finite probes in one pass, so this is faithful for the test). But the *reported* epsilonLnSigma then also reflects probes the loop subsequently NaN'd out of epsilonMean — a low junk probe has large sigma_ln (small L_hat) and inflates the reported uncertainty even though it did not enter the mean. The docstring at lines 180-184 documents this ('averaged across probes — NOT the sigma of the combined epsilonMean'), so it is a known, intentional design, but the reported uncertainty is mildly inconsistent with the set of probes actually combined. Same for chi (chi_combine.py:146,205-209).
suggested_fix: Either recompute mu_sigma over the surviving (post-removal) epsilon mask before writing epsilonLnSigma, or leave as-is but extend the existing docstring note to state explicitly that dropped outliers still contribute to the reported sigma. Low priority since it is a reported diagnostic, not used downstream for probe selection.
verification:
Verified the finding against the cited code and it holds factually, but it is genuinely a nit (as the finding itself claims).

CODE MECHANICS (confirmed):
- epsilon_combine.py:123 computes `mu_sigma = np.nanmean(sigma_ln_epsilon, axis=1)` ONCE, before the iterative removal loop at lines 136-163.
- The loop NaN's dropped probes out of the `epsilon` array (line 163) but never recomputes `mu_sigma`.
- Line 174-186 writes that pre-removal `mu_sigma` as `epsilonLnSigma`. A low junk probe has small epsilon -> large L_K -> small L_hat -> large var_ln (line 119, capped at 5.5) -> large sigma_ln, so a dropped low outlier still inflates the reported sigma. Identical pattern for chi at chi_combine.py:146 and 205-209.

ASSESSMENT (why it stays a nit, not above):
1. Reference-faithful for its primary use: `mu_sigma` feeds the CF95 threshold (line 124), and the reference l4.py:742-743 likewise averages sigma over all finite probes in a single pass. So the threshold behavior is correct.
2. Diagnostic-only downstream: grep shows epsilonLnSigma/chiLnSigma are consumed only by perturb/binning.py:307-317 and 597-604 (quadrature RMS pooling) and netcdf_schema as auxiliaryInformation. They are NEVER fed back into probe selection or into epsilonMean/chiMean. So the inconsistency propagates only into a reported uncertainty, never into the dissipation estimate.
3. Does not even fire on the real dataset: the primary instrument (VMP-250IR SN 479) has 2 shear probes; the removal loop is gated on `n_probes >= 3` (line 136), so for the actual ARCTERX data nothing is dropped and mu_sigma is faithfully the 2-probe mean. The wrinkle only materializes with >=3 probes AND on rows where an outlier was actually dropped.
4. The docstring (lines 180-184) documents the cross-probe averaging design ('averaged across probes — NOT the sigma of the combined epsilonMean'), though it does not explicitly state that dropped outliers still contribute. No test pins the pre- vs post-removal choice (test_processing_epsilon_combine.py only asserts shape).

The finding is accurate, honestly self-scoped as a nit, and not overstated. The proposed fix (recompute mu_sigma over the survivor mask, or extend the docstring) is reasonable. Real but minor; severity nit is correct.

title: profile_stratification leaves unsampled depth gaps (and contradicts its 'window/2 spacing' docstring) when window < 2*min_dp
evidence: The target grid uses step = max(half_w, min_dp) with half_w = window/2.0 (lines 394-395), then target_P = np.arange(p_lo, p_hi + step, step) (line 399). Each target's window is +/-half_w wide (line 404: sel = abs(P_arr - pt) <= half_w). When window < 2*min_dp, i.e. half_w < min_dp, the spacing becomes step = min_dp > half_w, so consecutive +/-half_w windows do NOT overlap and leave (step - 2*half_w)-wide gaps of water that are never evaluated for N2/dTdz. Reproduced empirically: profile_stratification(P, T, window=0.1, min_dp=0.2) yields targets spaced 0.2 dbar apart while each window is only +/-0.05 dbar, so ~50% of the column is silently unsampled. This also contradicts the docstring/test contract that 'evaluates at target pressures spaced by window/2' (docstring line 333; test test_targets_span_range_at_half_window_spacing asserts diff == window/2). The shipped default window=2.0 with min_dp=0.2 gives half_w=1.0 > 0.2 so step=half_w and there is no gap, which is why no test catches it; the defect only bites a caller who passes a small window.
suggested_fix: Make the target spacing equal the window half-width regardless of min_dp, e.g. step = half_w (keep min_dp solely as the per-window pressure-span validity guard it already is at lines 385/408), or explicitly clamp window >= 2*min_dp with a warning. Then update the docstring to state the actual spacing, and add a test with window < 2*min_dp asserting no inter-window gap.
verification:
The finding holds after reading the code and reproducing it. In src/odas_tpw/processing/mixing.py:394-399, half_w = window/2.0, step = max(half_w, min_dp), and target_P = np.arange(p_lo, p_hi + step, step), while line 404 selects samples with abs(P_arr - pt) <= half_w. When window < 2*min_dp (i.e. half_w < min_dp), step = min_dp > half_w, so consecutive +/-half_w windows do not overlap. I reproduced this empirically: profile_stratification(P, T, window=0.1, min_dp=0.2) yields targets spaced exactly 0.2 dbar apart (np.diff = [0.2, 0.2, ...]) with each window only +/-0.05 dbar, and a fine-grid coverage check shows exactly 0.5000 of the water column falls inside any window — the other ~50% is never differenced for N2/dTdz. The docstring at lines 336-337 ("spaced by window/2", "within each +/-window/2 pressure window") is therefore inaccurate on this path, and the test test_targets_span_range_at_half_window_spacing (tests/test_processing_mixing.py:99) only asserts diff==1.0 for window=2.0 (half_w=1.0 > min_dp=0.2), so it never exercises the gap regime — consistent with the finding's explanation of why no test catches it.

The finding is genuine but narrow in practical impact, which keeps it at minor (not higher): (1) The only in-tree caller, _compute_slow_stratification in perturb/pipeline.py:535-578, takes window from config (default 2.0; config.py:232) where half_w=1.0 >> min_dp=0.2, so step=half_w and there is no gap in the shipped default. min_dp is not exposed to that caller — it is hardcoded to DEFAULT_MIN_DP=0.2 — so triggering the gap requires a user to set stratification.window below ~0.4 dbar, an atypical sub-0.4-dbar background window. (2) The 'silent data loss' framing is mildly overstated at the output level: the caller interpolates the coarse per-target results onto the slow grid with np.interp (pipeline.py:574), so gaps are linearly filled rather than left as NaN holes — the real effect is reduced effective resolution plus the docstring/contract mismatch, not missing output values. The defect (code/docstring/test inconsistency plus genuinely unsampled water on an off-nominal but reachable config) is real and the proposed fix (step = half_w, or clamp window >= 2*min_dp with a warning, plus a docstring/test update) is appropriate. minor is the correct severity.

title: merge_p_files splices trailing partial-record bytes mid-stream, shifting all subsequent records (silent corruption)
evidence: merge_p_files copies chain[0] in full with shutil.copyfileobj (line 226-227) and appends each continuation file from offset header_size+config_size to EOF (line 233-235), with NO whole-record-boundary check anywhere in the module (grep confirms merge.py never computes data_bytes % record_size). If chain[0] or any MIDDLE continuation file ends in a fractional record, those stray bytes are written into the merged stream and shift every following record. Verified empirically: a 3-file chain whose middle file (a_0002.p) carried 37 trailing bytes produced a merged file whose 0xCC (third-file) records started 37 bytes late — the third file's records were read across a 37-byte boundary and the merged file's data length is no longer a multiple of record_size (2341-first_record => remainder 37), so PFile._read warns and silently drops the final partial record. A chain[0] with an 11-byte partial likewise yielded merged data_bytes %% record_size == 11. The default pipeline runs trim (whole-record-guaranteed) before merge, which masks this; but merge is reachable with files.merge=true & files.trim=false, and merge_p_files/find_mergeable_files are documented public API (CLAUDE.md) callable without trim. The merge tests never use a partial-record input (test helper has no extra_bytes knob), so the gap is unguarded.
suggested_fix: Before splicing, validate each chain member's body is a whole number of records: for chain[0] copy only first_record_size + (data_bytes // record_size)*record_size bytes; for continuations copy only (file_size - skip) // record_size * record_size bytes (drop any trailing partial). Or raise if (file_size - first_record_size) %% record_size != 0 for any member so a truncated continuation cannot corrupt downstream record alignment.
verification:
CONFIRMED. I read src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:222-242 (merge_p_files). It copies chain[0] in full via shutil.copyfileobj (lines 226-227), then for each continuation file seeks past only header_size+config_size and copyfileobj's to EOF (lines 230-235). There is NO whole-record-boundary check anywhere in merge.py — confirmed by grep (merge.py never computes data_bytes % record_size; only trim.py:148 and p_file.py:352 do). So if chain[0] or any MIDDLE continuation ends in a fractional record, those stray bytes are spliced mid-stream and shift every following record.

Empirically reproduced: a 3-file chain (record_size=1024, config_size=256) whose MIDDLE file (a_0002.p) carried 37 trailing partial bytes was (a) still detected as a single mergeable chain by find_mergeable_files — grouping keys on config_hash/endian/record_size/header_size/config_size (_file_group_key, lines 69-75), not file size, so the partial does not split the chain; and (b) merged into a file whose data region = 9253 bytes, 9253 % 1024 = 37 (not a whole number of records). The third file's 0xCC records began at data-region offset 6181, and 6181 % 1024 = 37 — i.e. every third-file record is misaligned by exactly the stray byte count. PFile._read (p_file.py:349-359) computes n_records via floor division and warns 'trailing partial record ignored' on the non-multiple, silently dropping the final partial while reading the shifted records as garbage. This is silent scientific-data corruption with only an easy-to-miss warning.

Reachability is real and stronger than the finding states: the default full pipeline runs trim first (config.py:25 trim=True), and trim whole-record-validates AND trims every file (trim.py:148 remainder = data_bytes % record_size; trims to first_record_size + n_complete*record_size), which masks the bug on the default path. But the dedicated `perturb merge` CLI subcommand (_cmd_merge, cli.py:180-194) calls run_merge(config) with NO trim step at all, and the full pipeline also honors files.merge=true with files.trim=false (pipeline.py:1774,1813). merge_p_files and find_mergeable_files are public, documented API. So the gap is genuinely unguarded on a supported entry point.

The only reason this is not higher than minor: triggering it requires a fractional record in chain[0] or a NON-final continuation file, which in practice only arises from a truncated/corrupt intermediate split file (DAQ rollover normally writes whole records per file except possibly the very last). Probability is low; impact when hit is silent corruption. The proposed fix (clamp each spliced member to a whole-record body, or raise on a non-multiple body) is correct. Keeping minor.

title: Test gap: merge is never exercised with a partial/fractional-record input, hiding the splice-corruption path
evidence: The merge test helper _make_p_file (lines 10-41) has no extra_bytes/trailing_partial parameter — every synthetic file it builds is an exact whole number of records, unlike the trim test helper (test_perturb_trim.py:12 has extra_bytes).
