Consequently no test in TestMergePFiles or TestFindMergeableFiles feeds merge_p_files a file with a fractional final record, so the mid-stream splice corruption (finding above) is invisible to CI. grep over test_perturb_merge.py for partial/extra_bytes/fractional returns nothing.
suggested_fix: Add a trailing_partial parameter to the merge test helper and a test that merges a chain whose middle member has a fractional final record, asserting the merged file's data length is a whole multiple of record_size (i.e. partial bytes are dropped, not spliced).
verification:
VERIFIED REAL (nit, test-gap). The finding's two factual claims both hold against the code I read:

1) The merge test helper genuinely lacks fractional-record coverage. `_make_p_file` in tests/test_perturb_merge.py:10-41 always builds an exact whole number of records (`for _ in range(n_records): data += bytes([data_byte]) * record_size`, lines 37-38) with no `extra_bytes`/`trailing_partial` parameter — unlike the trim helper at tests/test_perturb_trim.py:12 which has `extra_bytes=0` and appends a `\xff`-filled fractional record (lines 39-41). My grep over test_perturb_merge.py for partial/fractional/extra_bytes/trailing returned nothing. No test in TestMergePFiles or TestFindMergeableFiles feeds a fractional-record file.

2) The splice-corruption mechanism is real. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:230-235, continuation files are spliced by `in_f.seek(header_size + config_size)` then `shutil.copyfileobj(in_f, out_f)` — copying ALL remaining bytes including any trailing partial record, with no record-boundary trimming. A middle chain member with a fractional final record would inject partial bytes mid-stream, mis-aligning every subsequent record when the merged file is reparsed with the first file's geometry (the _file_group_key docstring at lines 58-75 confirms reparse uses the first file's header_size/record_size).

The finding is NOT overstated and correctly self-labels as a nit/test-gap rather than a live bug. Practical impact is genuinely narrow, which the nit severity reflects: in run_pipeline the default order is trim (files_cfg trim=True, pipeline.py:1774) BEFORE merge (merge=False by default, pipeline.py:1813), and trim_p_file strips fractional final records, so in the standard flow merge receives whole-record files. The corruption path requires a non-default config (trim=False + merge=True) or calling merge on raw split files, plus the partial record being in a MIDDLE chain member (a trailing partial in the last file goes to the end of the stream harmlessly). The proposed fix (add a trailing_partial param and assert merged length is a whole multiple of record_size) is a reasonable defensive test for this rare-but-possible truncated-middle-file case. Severity nit is correct: it is missing test coverage for a robustness edge that the default pipeline already mitigates, not a defect producing wrong results in the normal flow.

title: trim_p_file and _read_merge_info accept geometrically-impossible headers (record_size < header_size, header_size < 128) that PFile cannot parse
evidence: trim_p_file only validates record_size <= 0 (line 145-146) and data_bytes < 0 (line 142-143); it never checks header_size >= HEADER_BYTES or record_size >= header_size. _read_merge_info (merge.py:21-55) validates nothing beyond the 128-byte read. By contrast extract_pfile_segment (p_file.py:138-148) rejects header_size < HEADER_BYTES and record_size < header_size. Verified: trim_p_file accepts a file with header_size=512, record_size=128 (returns action='referenced'/'trimmed' on a record geometry PFile._read computes as data_words=(record_size-header_size)//2 = negative), and accepts header_size=64 (< HEADER_BYTES). Such a file passes trim/merge silently and only fails (or mis-parses) far downstream in PFile, where the error is harder to attribute to the source file.
suggested_fix: In trim_p_file and _read_merge_info, mirror extract_pfile_segment's checks: raise ValueError when header_size < HEADER_BYTES or record_size < header_size, so a malformed .p is rejected at the trim/merge stage with a clear, source-attributed message rather than corrupting or crashing the reader later.
verification: Verified empirically and by reading the code. trim_p_file (src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:141-146) validates only data_bytes >= 0 and record_size > 0; it never checks header_size >= HEADER_BYTES or record_size >= header_size. _read_merge_info (src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:21-55) validates nothing past the 128-byte header read. By contrast extract_pfile_segment (src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:138-148) rejects header_size < HEADER_BYTES and record_size < header_size with clear, source-attributed ValueErrors. I constructed the two cited malformed files: (1) header_size=512, record_size=128 -> trim_p_file returns action='referenced' and _read_merge_info returns the geometry silently; (2) header_size=64 (< 128) -> trim_p_file returns action='trimmed'. Both pass trim/merge without error but then crash in PFile._read (observed ZeroDivisionError; with nonzero cols a negative data_words = (record_size-header_size)//2 would instead break the np.fromfile reshape). So the factual claims hold: malformed geometry is accepted at trim/merge and only fails far downstream with a poorly-attributed error, inconsistent with extract_pfile_segment in the same package. Caveats that bound severity rather than refute it: the ODAS reference read_odas.m also does not validate record_size >= header_size (it just computes data_size = record_size - header_size), real RSI instruments never emit such geometries, and the failure is a crash on corrupt/non-RSI input, not silent corruption of valid data. The finding is correctly scoped to IO robustness, explicitly limited to malformed input, and not overstated. minor (a defense-in-depth / error-attribution gap, mirroring an existing sibling validator) is the right level; it borders on nit since it never affects valid data, but the in-module inconsistency with extract_pfile_segment justifies keeping minor.

title: Trim skip-guard still serves stale content for an equal-trimmed-size content change with equal/older mtime
evidence: The skip guard reuses an existing trimmed output when dest size == freshly-computed trimmed_size AND dest_mtime_ns >= source_mtime_ns (lines 165-171). This is the audit-#68 fix and correctly catches repaired-to-complete and geometry/size changes. But a source edited to keep the EXACT same trimmed size (same complete-record count and same fractional-byte count) while changing data content, then restamped with an equal/older mtime (cp -p, rsync --times, tar/backup restore, NFS clock skew), still passes both checks and is skipped. Verified empirically: re-trimming a source whose data bytes changed 0xAA->0xBB but kept identical trimmed size with an older mtime returned action='skipped' and the trimmed output retained the STALE 0xAA bytes. This is the residual hole inherent to any size+mtime cache key; the docstring (lines 106-113) does not claim to cover same-size content edits, so it is a known limitation rather than a contract violation.
suggested_fix: Document the residual same-size+older-mtime hole explicitly, or for full content-correctness make the skip guard hash the first_record (header+config) and/or compare a content digest rather than size+mtime alone. Lowest-effort mitigation: also require dest_mtime_ns > source_mtime_ns (strictly newer) is insufficient here, so a digest is the only complete fix; flag force_trim as the operator escape hatch in the docstring.
verification:
Confirmed by reading the code and reproducing empirically. The skip guard at src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:165-171 reuses an existing trimmed output when `not force AND dest.exists() AND dest.stat().st_size == trimmed_size AND dest.stat().st_mtime_ns >= source.stat().st_mtime_ns`. I wrote a harness that: (1) trimmed a fractional source filled with 0xAA, (2) rewrote the source with 0xBB keeping the byte-exact same total length (so trimmed_size is unchanged: same complete-record count and same fractional remainder), and (3) restamped the source mtime 100s older than the dest via os.utime. The second trim_p_file call returned action='skipped' and the trimmed output still held the STALE 0xAA bytes — exactly as the finding describes. So the technical claim and the empirical claim are both accurate.

However, this is correctly scoped as a nit, not a real correctness bug to fix:
- It is the inherent, standard limitation of ANY size+mtime cache key (make, rsync default, ccache without content mode all share it). It is a deliberate design tradeoff, not an introduced defect.
- The trigger is jointly narrow and adversarial: content must change while the trimmed size stays byte-identical AND the mtime must be restamped equal/older. For real RSI .p files copied off an instrument this is not a plausible workflow; the reproduction required hand-crafted byte buffers and an explicit os.utime backdate.
- A documented operator escape hatch already exists: files.force_trim: true (config.py:322; pipeline.py:1519, 1530) re-trims unconditionally.
- The docstring (trim.py:104-113) does not over-claim — it enumerates the cases the guard DOES catch (newer mtime, repaired-to-complete, geometry change) and never asserts coverage of same-size content edits with older mtime, so there is no contract violation. The finding itself concedes this is a known limitation.

Net: the defect genuinely holds and is reproducible, but its practical impact is minimal (standard cache-key tradeoff, adversarial trigger, existing escape hatch). The auditor's own primary proposed fix is essentially documentation. That matches a nit. Relevant file: /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:165-171 (guard) and :104-113 (docstring).

title: find_mergeable_files chains files on sequential file_number alone, with no time-continuity / restarted-flag guard (known unfixed major)
evidence: find_mergeable_files chains any same-(config_hash,endian,record_size,header_size,config_size) files whose file_number is exactly previous+1 (line 123), consulting NO time-continuity, the RSI 'restarted' header flag (word 16), record_number (word 1), n_records_written (word 19), or buffer_status — grep confirms merge.py reads none of these fields, and git history (-S 'restarted'/'start_time'/'time_continuity' over merge.py) shows the guard was never added. This is the prior-audit major (2026-06-19, merge.py:99-114): on the real ARCTERX SN479 set all 29 independent casts share one config hash with sequential file numbers, so files.merge=true byte-concatenates the whole deployment into one .p. NOT a regression — it is the documented files.merge=true caveat (audit summary line 160), and merge defaults off (config.py:27). Re-listed at reduced severity only because it remains live in the audited file and bounds the partial-record finding above; the original audit entry stands.
suggested_fix: Per the original audit: add a positive split-file discriminator beyond file_number adjacency — require restarted==0 on the continuation file and/or start_time contiguity within a tolerance (read year/month/day/hour/minute/second/millisecond, already in _H), before chaining. Until then, keep files.merge defaulted off and document that it must only be enabled for genuine RSI size-rollover splits.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL (minor). Every factual claim checks out, and I empirically reproduced the practical impact on the real ARCTERX data.

Code trace (src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py): find_mergeable_files groups files by _file_group_key = (config_hash, endian, record_size, header_size, config_size) (lines 69-75), then within each group chains files solely on file_number adjacency: `group[i]["file_number"] == chain[-1]["file_number"] + 1` (line 123). _read_merge_info (lines 21-55) reads ONLY file_number, endian, header_size, config_size, record_size, config_hash, file_size — it reads NONE of the split-continuity discriminators. grep over merge.py confirms zero references to restarted, record_number, n_records_written, buffer_status, or any time field. These fields all exist in _H (src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:30-49): restarted=word16, record_number=word1, n_records_written=word19, buffer_status=word15, plus year/month/day/hour/minute/second/millisecond=words3-9 — so a guard is implementable from already-parsed data.

Empirical reproduction on the real 29 ARCTERX SN479 .p files (.venv/bin/python running find_mergeable_files on VMP/*.p): all 29 files collapse into a SINGLE group key (hash 787bb757, big-endian, record_size 10368, header_size 128, config_size 10371) with consecutive file_numbers 2..30, and find_mergeable_files returns exactly ONE chain of all 29 files. Their header timestamps prove they are independent casts, not a size-rollover split: file 2 = 2025-01-14 15:30:17, file 3 = 2025-01-15 14:00:18, file 4 = 2025-01-15 18:00:37, file 5 = 21:57:36, etc. — separated by hours to a day, each with record_number=0 (a fresh recording start). The `run` subcommand (_cmd_run) and the dedicated `merge` subcommand (_cmd_merge -> run_merge, cli.py:193) both honor files.merge from user YAML, and run_merge -> plan_merge_outputs -> find_mergeable_files has no additional time/restarted guard (pipeline.py:1575-1624). One correction to the proposed fix (does not change validity): every ARCTERX file here has restarted=0, so the proposed `restarted==0` discriminator alone would NOT block the bad merge; only the start_time-contiguity check would actually catch it. The fix's "and/or start_time contiguity" wording covers this, but the restarted-flag half is ineffective on this dataset.

Severity: minor is correct. The defect is genuine and the failure mode is severe (silent data corruption) IF triggered, but it is gated behind a flag that is off by default and force-disabled on the common analysis commands — a latent footgun rather than an active default-path bug. Category correctly correctness. title: FP07 in-situ lag search restricts argmax to negative lags instead of ODAS's full-window-search-then-clamp, diverging from cal_FP07_in_situ.m on positive-peak profiles
evidence: Python _calc_lag with must_be_negative=True (the default, perturb/config.py:60) does `neg_mask = lags <= 0; corr_search = corr[neg_mask]; idx = argmax(|corr_search|)` — it searches only the negative half. ODAS cal_FP07_in_situ.m:502-505 instead takes `[max_corr, m_lag] = max(abs(correlation))` over the ENTIRE ±max_lag window, then at line 533 `if m_lag > 0, m_lag = 0; end` — i.e. find the global |corr| peak anywhere, and only clamp a positive result to zero. The two differ when the global peak is at positive lag: ODAS yields lag 0 (no shift), the Python yields the best negative-side peak (potentially a spurious sidelobe and a real negative shift). Per-profile median (fp07_cal.py:267) dampens outliers, so impact is bounded.
suggested_fix: Mirror ODAS: search the full window for argmax(|corr|), compute the lag, then clamp lag>0 to 0 (return 0.0 lag) rather than pre-restricting the search domain. e.g. `idx = argmax(|corr|); lag = lags[idx]/fs; lag = min(lag, 0.0) if must_be_negative else lag`.
verification:
Verified by reading both implementations.

Python (src/odas_tpw/perturb/fp07_cal.py:149-156): with must_be_negative=True (the operative default — config.py:60 and the fp07_calibrate signature default at fp07_cal.py:168 are both True), `_calc_lag` does `neg_mask = lags <= 0; corr_search = corr[neg_mask]; idx = argmax(|corr_search|)`. It searches ONLY the negative half of the ±max_lag window and returns lags_search[idx]/fs.

ODAS (odas/cal_FP07_in_situ.m): line 499-501 `xcorr(..., max_lag, 'coeff')` yields a symmetric two-sided correlation over lags -max_lag:+max_lag (no one-sided restriction). Line 502 `[max_corr, m_lag] = max(abs(correlation))` takes the GLOBAL |corr| peak anywhere in that full window. Line 504 converts to a signed lag. Only at line 533, `if m_lag > 0, m_lag = 0; end`, is a positive result clamped to zero — and that clamp applies to the alignment shift used for the regression (lines 535-537). So ODAS = global-peak-search-then-clamp, exactly as the finding states.

Divergence analysis (all three cases checked): (1) global peak at negative lag — ODAS and Python both return it; identical. (2) global peak at lag 0 — both return 0; identical. (3) global peak at POSITIVE lag — ODAS clamps the alignment shift to 0 (no shift); Python instead returns the best negative-side peak, i.e. a nonzero negative shift, potentially a sidelobe. Divergent, exactly as claimed. This Python lag then drives the per-profile median (line 267), i_shift (line 268), and T_ref_shifted (line 274), so on a positive-peak profile Python shifts the reference where ODAS would not.

The mitigating factor cited is real: per-profile median aggregation (line 267) bounds the campaign-level impact, and on the actual ARCTERX data FP07 genuinely physically leads the reference so peaks are expected negative (ODAS line 31/505 comments). The proposed fix (search full window, then clamp lag>0 to 0) faithfully mirrors ODAS's load-bearing alignment behavior. Finding's code reading, ODAS reading, and bounded-impact characterization are all accurate; minor severity is justified — a real reference-faithfulness divergence confined to an edge case (positive-peak profiles) and damped by the median, with no evidence of gross error on the operative dataset.

title: JAC low-pass cutoff uses mean(|W|) where ODAS uses |mean(W)|, differing on mixed up/down profile sets
evidence: _lowpass_filter computes `W_sum += np.sum(np.abs(W[s:e+1]))` then `W_mean = W_sum/count`, i.e. the mean of |W| over all profile samples. ODAS cal_FP07_in_situ.m:485 uses `W_mean = abs(mean(W_slow(m)))`, i.e. the absolute value of the mean of W. For a single-signed (pure downcast) profile these are identical, but when up and down casts are pooled into `profiles`, mean(|W|) > |mean(W)| and the cutoff fc = 0.73*sqrt(W_mean/0.62) is slightly higher than ODAS. The sqrt damps the effect and the cutoff only shapes the lag/regression filter, so the numerical impact on the calibration is small.
suggested_fix: Match ODAS by averaging signed W first: `W_mean = abs(W_sum_signed/count)` where W_sum_signed accumulates `np.sum(W[s:e+1])` (not abs), or compute per the same convention ODAS uses. Document the (intentional) pooling of all profiles if kept.
verification:
Code reading verified against both sources. Python src/odas_tpw/perturb/fp07_cal.py:97-100 accumulates `W_sum += np.sum(np.abs(W[s:e+1]))` over every (s,e) in `profiles` then divides by total count, i.e. mean(|W|) pooled across all profiles. ODAS odas/cal_FP07_in_situ.m:485 is `W_mean = abs(mean(W_slow(m)))`, i.e. |mean(W)|. The two formulas genuinely differ when W has mixed sign. W here is the SIGNED fall rate: src/odas_tpw/scor160/profile.py:58 `W = np.gradient(P, 1/fs)` is positive on descent, negative on ascent (docstring line 173 confirms "positive = downward"), matching ODAS W_slow. So the finding's reading of both files is correct.

Scoping is also correct. For a pure downcast set (vehicle "vmp" → direction "down", src/odas_tpw/rsi/vehicle.py:12), every W>0 so mean(|W|)==|mean(W)| exactly — the formulas are bit-identical, and the shipped ARCTERX VMP data is downcast, so the real-world numerical impact on the actual dataset is ZERO. The divergence only appears when up (W<0) and down (W>0) casts are pooled, which happens only in "glide" mode where get_profiles merges down+up segments (scor160/profile.py:193-198, and pipeline.py:1031 passes the merged list straight into fp07_calibrate → _lowpass_filter).

One nuance the finding slightly misframes but which does not invalidate it: ODAS never actually computes |mean(W)| over a pooled mixed set. cal_FP07_in_situ.m processes ONE profile per invocation — even in its glide branch (lines 395-403) it sorts down+up profiles into a single `profile` matrix and then selects a single column via profile_num (lines 425-427), so `m` always spans one single-direction cast where |mean(W)|==mean(|W|). The genuine architectural deviation is therefore that the Python POOLS all profiles for the cutoff (and medians the per-profile lags), whereas ODAS is per-profile; the abs-vs-mean ordering is a symptom of that pooling. Notably the Python's mean(|W|) is the physically sensible aggregator for a pooled glide set: the proposed fix `abs(W_sum_signed/count)` would collapse fc toward ~0 for a symmetric glide (up speed ≈ down speed makes mean(W)≈0), which is worse, not better. So the "numerical impact is small" caveat holds for realistic per-direction cases and the proposed fix is questionable.

Net: a real but trivial reference-faithfulness discrepancy with zero impact on the shipped downcast data and a fix that is arguably harmful in the glide case it targets. Genuine, correctly self-rated as a nit.

title: Chi viscosity mixes FP07 (T1) temperature with JAC_T-derived salinity, an internal provenance inconsistency under chi.salinity='measured'

title: epsilon.salinity has no 'measured' resolution path (unlike chi.salinity), so configuring it crashes deep in prepare_profiles
evidence: The pipeline resolves chi.salinity='measured' per-profile (pipeline.py:1312-1351) but passes epsilon.salinity straight through: `_compute_epsilon(prof_path, **{k:v for k,v in eps_cfg.items() if k not in (...)})` keeps 'salinity' unfiltered. The epsilon DEFAULTS comment (config.py:399) documents salinity as 'null = 35' and does not mention 'measured', so this is a latent foot-gun rather than a current data corruption.

title: bin_by_depth drops the deepest sample (and can add a spurious empty deepest bin) at non-binary-exact depth bin widths due to np.arange float accumulation
evidence:
The global depth grid is snapped with floor/ceil to d_min/d_max, then built with `bin_edges = np.arange(d_min, d_max + bin_width, bin_width)` (lines 440-442). For non-binary-exact bin_width, np.arange accumulates floating-point error so bin_edges[-1] != d_max. Two failure modes result:

(A) UNDERSHOOT: bin_width=0.1, depths up to g_max=5.0 -> d_max=5.0 but bin_edges[-1]=4.999999999999999. In _bin_indices (line 46) the last-bin guard `np.where(coords == bin_edges[-1], n_bins-1, idx)` compares against 4.9999..., so a sample at exactly 5.0 gets idx=n_bins and is marked out-of-range by the in_range mask (line 47) -> SILENTLY DROPPED. Reproduced end-to-end: a 94-sample profile (deepest=5.0 m) binned at bin_width=0.1 yields diagnostics n_samples.sum()==93; the deepest bin (center 4.95) excludes the 5.0 m sample.

(B) OVERSHOOT: bin_width=0.2, g_max=49.7 -> d_max=49.8 but bin_edges[-1]=50.0000000000004, creating an extra trailing bin (center 49.9) that is always empty (no data above d_max) -> spurious all-NaN deepest row.

The samples affected are exactly those at the global maximum depth, i.e. the deepest/bottom-turn row this audit targets. Production ARCTERX configs use depth `width: 1.0` (binary-exact, last edge lands on d_max exactly), so the bug does not currently manifest there; it triggers for valid, supported sub-metre widths (0.1 m and other width/range combinations where the arange last edge undershoots d_max). bin_by_time (line 576) is NOT affected: it builds edges from raw t_min/t_max with `t_max + bin_width`, so the last edge always exceeds t_max and the final sample lands in a real bin.
suggested_fix:
Build the depth edges from an exact integer bin count instead of arange-with-fractional-stop, so bin_edges[-1] == d_max bit-for-bit:

    n_bins = int(round((d_max - d_min) / bin_width))
    bin_edges = d_min + bin_width * np.arange(n_bins + 1)

This yields bin_edges[-1] exactly equal to d_max for every width tested (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0), so the `coords == bin_edges[-1]` last-bin guard fires for the deepest sample (no drop) and no spurious trailing bin is created. Alternatively, harden _bin_indices to snap on `coords >= bin_edges[-1]` combined with an explicit `coords <= d_max` range check, but the integer-count edge construction is the cleaner root-cause fix and also benefits combo's join="exact" widthwise glue by making grids reproducible across runs.
verification:
CONFIRMED REAL. Read src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:440-442 (bin_by_depth edge construction) and lines 43-48 (_bin_indices: last-bin guard `coords == bin_edges[-1]` and in_range mask `idx < n_bins`). The code on the target branch matches the citation exactly, with no float tolerance handling. Empirically verified both failure modes:

(1) OVERSHOOT (spurious empty deepest bin): `np.arange(d_min, d_max+bin_width, bin_width)` overshoots d_max due to float accumulation, adding an extra trailing bin that no in-range sample can populate -> all-NaN deepest row. Reproduced the auditor's exact Case B (bw=0.2, g_max=49.7, g_min=0.0): arange yields 250 bins vs integer-count 249, spurious center 49.9. Pervasive: 4267 of ~50000 scanned (g_max, bin_width) combos with g_min=0 across widths {0.1,0.2,0.25,0.3,0.5}. This needs NO sample on an edge — it is a pure arange artifact.

(2) UNDERSHOOT (dropped deepest real sample): when arange undershoots so bin_edges[-1] < d_max and the deepest real sample sits at g_max == d_max (an exact bin-edge multiple), the `coords == bin_edges[-1]` guard fails to fire, np.digitize returns idx==n_bins, and the in_range mask silently drops the sample. Reproduced 7610 such real-sample drops across the scan (e.g. bw=0.1, g_min=0.31, g_max=0.80 -> edge[-1]=0.7999999999999999 != d_max=0.8).

Two auditor overstatements that do NOT invalidate the finding: (a) the auditor's specific Case A numbers (g_min=0.0, g_max=5.0, bw=0.1, '94-sample profile reproduced end-to-end') do NOT reproduce — bin_edges[-1] lands exactly on 5.0 and the 5.0 sample is correctly binned (idx=49, in_range=True); the drop only occurs for other inputs such as g_min=0.31. (b) The proposed fix `d_min + bin_width*np.arange(n_bins+1)` is NOT bit-exact to d_max as claimed (20791 mismatches in my scan due to the bw*arange multiply), though it does correctly fix the bin-count (no extra/missing bin) by pinning n_bins.

Correctly stated by the auditor: production ARCTERX configs use binary-exact `width: 1.0` so the bug is latent, not active; `width`/`diss_width`/`chi_width` are unrestricted user-configurable floats (cli.py:232,271,292), so sub-metre widths are valid supported inputs; and bin_by_time (line 576) is genuinely unaffected (t_max is not snapped up, so the last edge always exceeds it — verified 0 drops).

Severity: minor is correct. Latent (not triggered by current production width=1.0), but trivially triggered by valid sub-metre widths; the overshoot empty-bin manifestation is common and silent, the undershoot data-loss is rarer (requires edge-aligned deepest sample). Real numerical-correctness/edge-case defect.

title: Explicit --clim/--vmin minimum on a log variable is silently clamped to 6 decades below the max
evidence: In _make_norm() the log path does `vmin = max(vmin, vmax / 1.0e6)` AFTER honoring the user's explicit limit. layout.quantile_limits(z, lo, hi) returns `lo` unchanged when the user passes --clim/--vmin, but line 155 then silently overrides it. Verified: `_make_norm('epsilonMean', z, args, {'epsilonMean': (1e-14, 1e-6)})` returns norm.vmin == 1e-12, NOT the requested 1e-14 — an 8-decade explicit request is silently truncated to 6 decades with no warning. This also makes the two subcommands inconsistent: eps_chi.py's _safe_lognorm (line 376) applies NO decade clamp, so `--eps-vmin 1e-14 --eps-vmax 1e-6` is honored there but clamped in `profiles --product diss`. The 6-decade guard is sensible for the AUTO-quantile case but should not override an explicit user limit.
suggested_fix: Only apply the `vmin = max(vmin, vmax/1e6)` floor when limits are NOT explicit (the function already computes `explicit = name in clim or args.vmin is not None or args.vmax is not None` at line 114). Guard the clamp with `if not explicit:` so user-supplied --clim/--vmin minima pass through verbatim, matching eps_chi's behavior.
verification:
VERIFIED REAL, present on this branch. I read the cited code and reproduced the exact claimed behavior.

Code trace (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/profiles.py):
- Line 113: `lo, hi = clim.get(name, (args.vmin, args.vmax))` pulls the user's explicit per-var --clim or global --vmin/--vmax.
- Line 114: `explicit = name in clim or args.vmin is not None or args.vmax is not None` is computed but only consulted on the DIVERGING path (line 142), NOT the log path.
- Line 152 (log path): `vmin, vmax = layout.quantile_limits(z, lo, hi)`. I confirmed in layout.py:52 that quantile_limits returns `lo if lo is not None else float(q[0])` — i.e. it passes the explicit minimum through verbatim.
- Line 155: `vmin = max(vmin, vmax / 1.0e6)` then unconditionally overrides that explicit minimum if it is more than 6 decades below vmax. The only inline comment (line 156) addresses the separate all-equal-data guard, not this clamp; the 6-decade floor is undocumented and is not a stated design contract.

Empirical reproduction (matches the finding exactly): `_make_norm('epsilonMean', z, args, {'epsilonMean': (1e-14, 1e-6)})` with args.vmin/vmax=None returns norm.vmin == 1e-12 (== vmax/1e6), NOT the requested 1e-14. An 8-decade explicit request is silently truncated to 6 decades with no warning. epsilonMean is confirmed a member of _LOG_VARS (profiles.py:79), so the path is reachable for real variables.

Cross-subcommand inconsistency confirmed: eps_chi.py's _safe_lognorm (lines 370-380) applies NO decade clamp — it only fixes the all-equal case (vmn>=vmx -> vmx/10). So `--eps-vmin 1e-14 --eps-vmax 1e-6` is honored in eps_chi but clamped in `profiles --product diss`. The two subcommands genuinely diverge.

Proposed fix is sound and consistent with the file's own pattern: the `explicit` flag already exists (line 114) and is already used to gate behavior on the diverging path (line 142). Guarding line 155 with `if not explicit:` makes the auto-quantile 6-decade floor still apply while letting user-supplied minima pass through, matching eps_chi.

Severity: I downgrade minor -> nit. The defect is real and present, but (1) it triggers only when a user EXPLICITLY requests a log dynamic range wider than 6 decades — an unusual request for ε/χ/K fields whose real-data ranges and typical explicit limits span far fewer decades, so the common auto-quantile case (the clamp's intended target) is unaffected; (2) the impact is purely presentational (colorbar floor), no scientific/computed quantity is wrong; (3) the outcome is benign — a valid plot with a narrower-than-requested floor, no crash, no data corruption. Real but low-impact, narrow-trigger presentation-faithfulness issue.

title: Legacy chi binning uses an order-dependent running 0.5-average, not a mean — biased toward the last sample and inconsistent with its own docstring
evidence: _load_chi_legacy_per_profile does `chi[i, j] = v if np.isnan(cur) else 0.5 * (cur + v)` when multiple source probe-mean values fall in the same depth bin. For 3 values v1,v2,v3 this yields weights (0.25, 0.25, 0.5) — e.g. mean of (1,2,9) becomes 5.25 instead of the true 4.0, biased toward whichever sample is digitized last. The companion _reindex_rows_to_depth docstring (line 112) claims reduce='mean' is 'consistent with the legacy path,' but _reindex_rows_to_depth uses a TRUE mean (acc/cnt), so the two chi-loading paths are NOT consistent: the same input produces different chi (and therefore different gamma=chi/eps) depending on whether chiMean is on the combo or the legacy fallback fires.
suggested_fix: Accumulate sum + count per (i,j) and divide once at the end (a true nanmean), matching _reindex_rows_to_depth. e.g. keep parallel `acc`/`cnt` arrays and finalize `chi = np.where(cnt>0, acc/cnt, np.nan)`. Then the 'consistent with the legacy path' docstring claim becomes true.
verification:
Verified the finding against the actual code at /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py.

Line 87 reads verbatim: `chi[i, j] = v if np.isnan(cur) else 0.5 * (cur + v)`, inside a loop `for i, v in zip(idx[ok], c[ok])`. This is an order-dependent running 0.5-average, not a mean. I confirmed the math by execution: for inputs [1,2,9] it yields 5.25 (weights 0.25, 0.25, 0.5) vs the true mean 4.0, and reversing the input order [9,2,1] yields 3.25 — proving both the last-sample bias and order dependence the finding describes.

The companion function `_reindex_rows_to_depth` (lines 94-143) uses a genuine mean: it accumulates `acc[dst, ok] += row[ok]` and `cnt[dst, ok] += 1`, then returns `acc / cnt` (line 143). So the two chi-loading paths reduce within-bin collisions differently. The docstring at lines 111-114 explicitly claims `reduce='mean'` is "consistent with the legacy path" — this is false whenever 3+ source values share a destination bin. The inconsistency is real.

The mechanism is reachable: chi profiles carry `chi(probe, time)` and `P_mean(time)` (confirmed in rsi/chi_io.py lines 514-603); the legacy loader reduces over probe (`np.nanmean(..., axis=0)`) leaving one value per time window, then `np.digitize` maps many time-window pressures onto the coarser eps `depth` grid (edges from layout.depth_edges, half-bin = median diff/2). When 3+ chi windows fall in one eps depth bin — possible because chi and diss bin widths are separately configurable (the code's own docstring at line 108-109 notes this) — the bug fires and biases the displayed log10(chi) and gamma=chi/eps.

Mitigating factors that bound severity: (1) this is a plotting/presentation-only module (`perturb plot eps-chi`, wired only via perturb/plot/cli.py), not a computed scientific data product; (2) it is a legacy fallback branch that fires only for old perturb runs where `chiMean` is absent from the chi_combo (run() lines 287-296); the primary/current path uses the correct `_reindex_rows_to_depth`. So the defect genuinely holds and matters for faithfulness of legacy plots, but its blast radius is narrow. "minor" is the correct severity.

title: latitude/longitude x-axis is dateline-unsafe: a transect crossing ±180° gets reordered and stretched in profiles/scalar
evidence: profiles._build_profiles_figure sorts columns by raw x via `col = finite[np.argsort(x[finite])]` (line 208) and scalar bins on raw x (scalar.py line 188-194). For method='longitude', xaxis.compute returns raw degrees with no wrapping (xaxis.py line 327). Verified: lon=[179, 179.5, -179.5, -179] yields x=[179,179.5,-179.5,-179]; argsort reorders to [-179.5,-179,179,179.5], so two physically-adjacent casts (179.5°E and 179.5°W) render 359 units apart and out of geographic order, while the distance/along_line/signed methods ARE dateline-safe (they use _wrap_deg / _circmean_deg). Root cause is in xaxis.py (out of audit scope), but it surfaces as misregistered/disordered columns through the audited files. Saipan/ARCTERX (~145°E) is far from the dateline, so impact is low for the current campaign but the code is advertised as generic.
suggested_fix: Either document the limitation in the longitude method's label/docstring, or (preferred) when the finite longitudes span the dateline (range > 180° but circular spread is small), unwrap them about their circular mean before sorting/binning so adjacent casts stay adjacent. Could be handled centrally in xaxis.compute for the 'longitude' branch.
verification: The finding is technically accurate on every cited point. xaxis.py:327 (`longitude` branch) returns raw `np.asarray(lon, float)` with no longitude unwrapping. profiles.py:208 then sorts columns by raw x via `col = finite[np.argsort(x[finite])]`, and scalar.py:188-194 bins on raw x via `finite_x.min()/max()` and `grid.make_edges(...)`. I reproduced the empirical claim: lon=[179, 179.5, -179.5, -179] (a ~1° span across the dateline) argsorts to [-179.5, -179, 179, 179.5] and is treated as a 359° span, so two physically-adjacent casts (179.5°E and 179.5°W) render 359 units apart and out of geographic order. The contrast with the dateline-safe methods is real: haversine_m (line 117), the distance/along_line/signed branches use `_wrap_deg` (line 67-69) and `_circmean_deg` (line 72-88), and the module docstrings explicitly call out dateline-safety as a design concern — so the `longitude` branch passing raw degrees is a genuine inconsistency in a module advertised as "Dimension-agnostic by design." Mitigating: (1) it is presentation-only — epsilon/chi data values are untouched; only the x-ordering/spacing of columns in an optional section plot is wrong; (2) it triggers only on a dateline-crossing transect with the `longitude` x-axis method specifically; (3) the ARCTERX/Saipan campaign sits at ~145°E, far from ±180°, so current-data impact is zero; (4) even when triggered, the result is a visibly-broken plot, not silently-wrong science. The root cause is in xaxis.py (which the finding honestly flags as out of scope), but the symptom does surface through the two audited files. Given it is cosmetic, dormant for the actual campaign, and confined to one optional plot mode, the claimed 'minor' is slightly high; 'nit' is the better fit, but the defect genuinely holds.

title: eps_chi negative --eps-vmin/--chi-vmin silently yields a 'no data' panel instead of an error
evidence: profiles._make_norm explicitly rejects a non-positive --clim minimum on a log variable with a clear SystemExit (profiles.py lines 148-151). eps_chi has no such guard: a user passing `--eps-vmin -5` flows through quantile_limits (which returns -5 because lo is not None), then _safe_lognorm returns None on `vmn <= 0` (line 376), and the panel renders the 'no finite ε data' placeholder (line 393) even though there IS finite positive data. The user gets a blank panel rather than a message that their minimum is invalid for a log scale.
suggested_fix: After computing eps_vmin/chi_vmin/gam_vmin from CLI overrides, validate that any explicitly-supplied *_vmin is > 0 and raise SystemExit with a message (mirroring profiles.py's 'colour-scale minimum for log variable must be > 0'), instead of silently producing an empty panel.
verification:
Verified the full claimed chain in src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/eps_chi.py and confirmed it with a runtime reproduction.

1. add_arguments registers --eps-vmin/--chi-vmin/--gam-vmin as plain `type=float, default=None` (lines 228-239) with NO validation.
2. run() calls layout.quantile_limits(eps, args.eps_vmin, args.eps_vmax) at line 366. quantile_limits (layout.py:46-54) returns `lo if lo is not None else q[0]` — so an explicit negative override like -5 passes through verbatim whenever finite positive data exists.
3. _safe_lognorm (eps_chi.py:370-380) returns None at line 376 because `vmn <= 0` is true for -5.
4. The panel loop at lines 388-394 sees `norm is None` and renders the "no finite {short} data" placeholder (line 393) even though positive finite data is present.

Runtime check confirmed: quantile_limits(eps, -5.0, None) -> (-5.0, 9.8e-08), and _safe_lognorm(-5.0, 9.8e-08) -> None -> placeholder panel.

The contrasting precedent is real: profiles.py _make_norm at lines 148-151 raises SystemExit("colour-scale minimum for log variable {name!r} must be > 0 (got {lo})") for exactly this misuse on log variables. eps_chi has no equivalent guard, so the two surfaces handle the same user error inconsistently.

Severity assessment: this is a presentation/UX inconsistency, not a correctness defect in the scientific output. It only triggers on explicit user misuse (a negative minimum is meaningless on a log color scale), is low-frequency, and the worst outcome is a misleading "no data" label instead of a clear error message. The proposed fix (validate explicit *_vmin > 0 and SystemExit mirroring profiles.py) is small and reasonable. "nit" is the appropriate severity.

title: No test covers the explicit-clim decade clamp or the profiles/eps_chi log-limit inconsistency
evidence: test_make_norm_picks_scale and test_log_clim_nonpositive_errors cover the LogNorm type and the non-positive rejection, but nothing asserts that an explicit --clim minimum survives unmodified. Because of the line-155 clamp, a test like `_make_norm('epsilonMean', z, args, {'epsilonMean': (1e-14, 1e-6)})` asserting `norm.vmin == 1e-14` would currently FAIL (it returns 1e-12) — i.e. the clamp defect is invisible to the suite. This test gap is what let the silent-override behavior persist.
suggested_fix: Add a regression test asserting that an explicit per-variable --clim minimum is preserved exactly in the resulting LogNorm.vmin (after the suggested fix to gate the clamp on `not explicit`), and one asserting profiles and eps_chi treat the same explicit log limits identically.
verification:
Verified all three sub-claims of the finding against the actual code and by running it.

1. The line-155 clamp defect is real. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/profiles.py:155, `vmin = max(vmin, vmax / 1.0e6)` runs unconditionally on the log path, even when the minimum came from an explicit --clim. I ran `_make_norm('epsilonMean', z, args, {'epsilonMean': (1e-14, 1e-6)})` and it returned LogNorm.vmin == 1e-12, not the requested 1e-14. quantile_limits (layout.py:51-53) faithfully passes the explicit lo=1e-14 through ("CLI overrides win"), so the only thing that changes it is the line-155 clamp. The `explicit` flag (profiles.py:114, `name in clim or args.vmin is not None or args.vmax is not None`) already exists and already gates the diverging branch at line 142 (`if not explicit`), but is NOT applied to the log clamp — confirming the proposed fix is coherent and matches the existing pattern.

2. The profiles/eps_chi inconsistency is real. eps_chi.py's `_safe_lognorm` (lines 370-380) has no `vmax/1e6` decade clamp; it passes explicit limits from quantile_limits straight into LogNorm. So identical explicit log limits (1e-14, 1e-6) yield vmin=1e-14 in eps_chi but vmin=1e-12 in profiles — the two paths genuinely treat the same explicit limits differently.

3. The test gap is real. I grepped all of tests/. The three closest tests only assert: norm TYPE with empty clim (test_make_norm_picks_scale, line 96-108), non-positive rejection MIN<=0 (test_log_clim_nonpositive_errors, line 189-194), and rc==0 + PNG existence (test_clim_and_no_qc, line 236-242). None asserts that an explicit log --clim minimum survives in LogNorm.vmin. Even test_clim_and_no_qc happens to use 1e-9 with max 1e-5, where 1e-9 > 1e-5/1e6 = 1e-11, so the clamp never fires and that test could not catch the defect regardless. The finding's own proposed test (asserting norm.vmin == 1e-14) would indeed currently FAIL (returns 1e-12), exactly as claimed.

The test-gap framing and "nit" severity are accurate and appropriately conservative; the underlying clamp/inconsistency is a genuine presentation-faithfulness defect that the missing assertion allowed to persist.

title: Stale mypy per-module override list references three nonexistent rsi modules (config drift)
evidence: The [[tool.mypy.overrides]] module list (lines 65-79) names `odas_tpw.rsi.spectral`, `odas_tpw.rsi.ocean`, and `odas_tpw.rsi.nasmyth`. None of these files exist: `find src/odas_tpw/rsi -name '*.py'` shows convert.py, profile.py, helpers.py, deconvolve.py exist (correctly listed) but there is no rsi/spectral.py, rsi/ocean.py, or rsi/nasmyth.py. `git log --all -- src/odas_tpw/rsi/{spectral,ocean,nasmyth}.py` returns empty (they never existed in rsi/), and rsi/__init__.py does not re-export them as shims. The real modules exist in scor160/ (scor160/spectral.py, scor160/ocean.py, scor160/nasmyth.py all present on disk) and are correctly and separately listed at pyproject.toml:77-79. The deconvolve/profile/helpers/convert rsi entries are real. mypy src/odas_tpw/ passes (Success: no issues found in 91 source files), so there is no active breakage. The defect is exactly as described: dead, misleading config-drift entries that would silently apply broad disable_error_code suppression if a real rsi/spectral.py etc. is ever added. The finding genuinely holds but is purely cosmetic with no current functional/correctness impact; minor (bordering on nit) is appropriate.

title: pandas used directly by perturb subpackage but not declared as a direct dependency (relies on xarray transitively)
evidence: perturb imports pandas directly at four call sites: gps.py:214 (`import pandas as pd` then `pd.read_csv(file)` at :216), hotel.py:177/186 (`pd.to_datetime(...)` for ISO/datetime time parsing), and hotel.py:285 (`pd.read_csv(path)` for CSV hotel files). pandas appears nowhere in [project].dependencies (lines 16-25). It currently resolves only because every perturb module imports xarray (binning.py, pipeline.py, ctd.py, hotel sibling modules, etc.) and `importlib.metadata.requires('xarray')` shows `pandas>=2.2` as a hard requirement, so pip always installs it (confirmed: pandas 3.0.1 is importable in this env). This is therefore a latent fragility rather than a present runtime bug: PEP 508 / packaging best practice is to declare every directly-imported runtime dependency, because relying on a transitive guarantee breaks if a future xarray release makes pandas optional, or under a constraints/minimal-install resolver. The lazy (function-level) imports also mean the failure, if it ever occurred, would surface only at perturb CSV/ISO runtime, not at install or import time.
suggested_fix: Add an explicit `"pandas>=2.2,<4"` entry to [project].dependencies (the >=2.2 floor matches xarray's own requirement; pandas 3.x is already in use here, hence <4). This makes the direct dependency self-documenting and resolver-robust without changing current behavior.
verification: All factual claims verified against the actual code. (1) pandas is directly imported in the perturb subpackage at exactly the cited sites: gps.py:214 (`import pandas as pd`) used at :216 (`pd.read_csv(file)`), hotel.py:177/179 and 186/188 (`pd.to_datetime(...)`), and hotel.py:285/287 (`pd.read_csv(path)`). (2) pandas appears nowhere in pyproject.toml — grep returned "NO pandas in pyproject.toml"; [project].dependencies (lines 16-25) lists numpy, netCDF4, scipy, xarray, gsw, ruamel.yaml, matplotlib, cmocean, but not pandas. (3) The transitive-resolution claim is correct: `importlib.metadata.requires('xarray')` returns `pandas>=2.2` as a hard (non-extra) requirement, and every perturb module imports xarray, so pip always installs pandas. (4) The imports are lazy/function-level, so any failure would surface at CSV/ISO runtime rather than install/import. No other module in src/ imports pandas, so this is the sole site. The finding correctly characterizes this as a latent fragility, not a present runtime bug — it holds per PEP 508 packaging best practice (declare every directly-imported runtime dependency) and would break under a future xarray release that makes pandas optional or under a constraints/minimal-install resolver. The proposed fix (`"pandas>=2.2,<4"`) is behavior-preserving and the >=2.2 floor matches xarray's own requirement. The defect is genuine but cosmetic/hygiene-level with no current functional impact, so the claimed severity of "nit" is accurate.

title: L1 converter emits non-UDUNITS unit 'micro_Tesla' for the MAG variable (newly reachable after the magn-classification fix)
evidence: The MAG spec hardcodes units="micro_Tesla" (convert.py:361). cf_units rejects it ([UT_UNKNOWN] Failed to parse unit "micro_Tesla"), while the equivalent forms 'uT' and 'microtesla' both parse. The file declares Conventions="CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3" (convert.py:478), so this is a hard CF/UDUNITS violation. Critically this is a NEW exposure: the prior round-1 audit fixed _classify_channels to match type 'magn' (it previously checked 'mag', so MAG was never written and the bad unit never reached NetCDF). Now any instrument with a magnetometer writes an unparseable unit. The channel converter itself already returns the parseable 'uT' (channels.py:212), which convert.py overrides with the bad string. The SN479 sample has no magnetometer, so test_cf_compliance never exercises this path.
suggested_fix: Change units="micro_Tesla" to "uT" (or "microtesla"/"1e-6 T") in the MAG spec at convert.py:361, matching what convert_magn returns and what UDUNITS accepts.

(1) cf_units rejects the hardcoded unit: `cf_units.Unit("micro_Tesla")` raises `[UT_UNKNOWN] Failed to parse unit "micro_Tesla"`, while `uT`, `microtesla`, and `1e-6 T` all parse. The MAG *data* comes from `pf.channels[name]` (the convert_magn output), but convert_magn's returned unit string "uT" (channels.py:212) is NOT used for the attribute — the hardcoded spec value overrides it. So the file genuinely carries `units="micro_Tesla"`. (3) The file declares `ds.Conventions = "CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3"` (convert.py:478), so an unparseable UDUNITS string is a real CF violation. (4) New exposure confirmed: commit 579ce0f ("classify magnetometer channels by their actual config/CONVERTERS type key 'magn' (was 'mag')") changed _classify_channels; line 64 now reads `pf.channel_info[n]["type"] == "magn"`, so MAG channels are now actually written (previously they were misclassified to 'supplementary' and the spec was never reached). (5) The SN479 sample has no magn channel (channel types: aroft_o2, aroft_t, inclt, inclxy, jac_c, jac_t, piezo, poly, raw, shear, therm, voltage — no 'magn'), so test_cf_compliance (test_convert.py:149) never exercises this path; that test also only checks specific standard_names/time-units, never validating units against UDUNITS. Checking the full set of hardcoded L1 units shows only 'micro_Tesla' and 'FTU' fail cf_units; all 10 others (decibar, s-1, 1, 'm s-2', degrees_Celsius m-1, degree_Celsius, mS cm-1, degrees, ug L-1, umol L-1) parse cleanly — confirming UDUNITS-compliant intent and that micro_Tesla is an outlier bug, not a deliberate convention. Note the accel spec correctly uses 'm s-2' (with space, parseable), distinct from the converter's 'm_s-2' string. The proposed one-line fix (units="uT") is correct and matches the converter. Downgrading severity to minor: this is metadata-only on an optional channel that the production instrument lacks, does not affect any scientific computation, and does not crash NetCDF writing — it only fails downstream CF/UDUNITS validators. Real defect, low practical impact.

title: L1 converter emits non-UDUNITS unit 'FTU' for the TURB variable
evidence: The TURB spec hardcodes units="FTU" (convert.py:383). Running p_to_L1 on VMP/ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0002.p and validating every L1 variable's units with cf_units yields exactly one FAIL: TURB 'FTU'. The perturb schema explicitly recognises this and maps FTU->"1" via UNITS_FIXUP/canonicalize_units (netcdf_schema.py:31-32), documenting that FTU is absent from UDUNITS, but the convert.py L1 path never applies canonicalize_units, so the L1 product remains non-CF for turbidity despite declaring CF-1.13.
suggested_fix: Set TURB units to "1" and document 'formazin turbidity units' in long_name (matching netcdf_schema.UNITS_FIXUP), or route convert.py's hardcoded unit strings through canonicalize_units.
verification: Verified against the actual code and empirically. (1) convert.py:383 hardcodes units="FTU" for the TURB variable spec. (2) _create_l1_variables (convert.py:139-140) writes every attr including units verbatim via setattr; grep confirms canonicalize_units is never imported or called anywhere in convert.py — it is used only on the profile.py path (profile.py:235,245). (3) p_to_L1 sets ds.Conventions = "CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3" (convert.py:478), so the L1 file declares CF-1.13. (4) cf_units.Unit('FTU') raises ValueError [UT_UNKNOWN], confirming FTU is non-UDUNITS. (5) Running p_to_L1 on VMP/ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0002.p and validating every variable's units across all groups yields exactly one failure: L1_converted/TURB 'FTU'. The TURB channel ('Turbidity') is genuinely present and emitted; MAG is empty for this instrument (mag: []), which is why micro_Tesla (which also fails cf_units in isolation) does not appear — so the finding's 'exactly one FAIL' claim is accurate for this file. (6) The perturb schema (netcdf_schema.py:24-33) explicitly maps 'FTU'->'1' with a comment stating FTU is absent from UDUNITS and CF guidance is to mark it dimensionless and document in long_name, confirming the project already recognizes this and that the L1 path is inconsistent with it. The finding holds in full. It is purely a metadata/CF-compliance defect (no effect on computed values), affecting strict CF/UDUNITS validation of the L1 product for one variable while the file declares CF-1.13. Minor severity is appropriate.

title: Supplementary channels in L1 write raw RSI unit strings without UDUNITS canonicalization
evidence: Unmapped channels are written with v.units = info["units"] (convert.py:541), i.e. the verbatim RSI INI unit string. RSI emits forms like 'umol_L-1' and 'deg' that are not UDUNITS-parseable (cf_units rejects both) — this is exactly the class of strings UNITS_FIXUP/canonicalize_units was built to repair (netcdf_schema.py:24-38), but the convert.py supplementary path bypasses it. On the SN479 sample the supplementary channels happened to carry parseable units (counts, V, unknown, deg_C), so the existing tests do not surface it, but any config with a 'deg' or 'umol_L-1' supplementary channel produces a CF-non-compliant L1 file.
suggested_fix: Wrap the supplementary unit write in canonicalize_units(info["units"]) (import it from perturb.netcdf_schema, as profile.py already does), so the L1 path matches the per-profile/perturb path.
verification:
Verified against the actual code; the finding holds.

1. convert.py:541 writes `v.units = info["units"]` for supplementary channels — i.e. the verbatim RSI INI unit string (units come from p_file.py:539, the converter return value).

2. The asymmetry is real. The per-profile path (profile.py:235,245) explicitly imports and applies `canonicalize_units(str(v))` to channel units "because RSI's INI emits unit strings like umol_L-1 and deg that UDUNITS won't parse". The mapped L1 variable specs (_l1_variable_specs) use hardcoded UDUNITS-clean strings (e.g. degree_Celsius, mS cm-1, umol L-1, degrees). Only the supplementary L1 path at convert.py:535-543 bypasses canonicalization. The proposed fix (wrap in canonicalize_units, imported from perturb.netcdf_schema) is exactly the pattern profile.py already uses.

3. canonicalize_units/UNITS_FIXUP (netcdf_schema.py:24-38) exist precisely to repair umol_L-1->'umol L-1' and deg->'degree'. I confirmed with cf_units that 'deg' and 'umol_L-1' are genuinely NOT parseable (cf_units.Unit raises), while 'counts','deg_C','V','unknown' parse fine — so the SN479 sample's supplementary bucket (Gnd=counts, Incl_T=deg_C, PV=unknown, V_Bat=V) does not surface the bug, matching the finding's evidence.

4. In-practice reachability confirmed (not contrived): convert_poly (type 'poly', channels.py:148-161) returns the INI 'units' field verbatim (`units or "unknown"`); 'poly' is a generic catch-all channel type that lands in the supplementary bucket (_classify_channels only maps specific types). The SN479 'PV' channel is itself a poly channel. Across the actual VMP/*.p files the raw unit strings emitted include 'deg', 'umol_L-1', 'mS_cm-1', 'FTU', 'dBar' — all non-UDUNITS. A poly supplementary channel configured with units='deg' (or an inclxy channel not named X/Y, which emits 'deg' at channels.py:220) would write a CF-non-compliant attribute.

5. No test catches it: test_convert.py::test_cf_compliance only asserts specific named attrs (Conventions, time, PRES) and never runs a real compliance-checker/UDUNITS validator on supplementary units; test_perturb_cf_compliance.py runs the real compliance-checker only on a synthetic perturb combo, not on the L1 convert output.

Impact is low: it does not corrupt data values, only renders auxiliary-channel unit attributes CF/UDUNITS-non-compliant in the L1 file, and is not triggered by the shipped SN479 sample. Minor severity is correct.

title: test_cf_compliance does not validate units against UDUNITS, hiding the MAG/TURB unit defects
evidence: test_cf_compliance asserts only Conventions, title/history presence, time standard_name/axis/units substring, and PRES standard_name (test_convert.py:160-176). It never parses any variable's units with cf_units/UDUNITS and never touches TURB, MAG, or supplementary channels. Because the SN479 sample carries no magnetometer, the micro_Tesla MAG unit is wholly untested, and FTU on TURB is asserted nowhere. A loop validating every emitted units attr with cf_units.Unit() would have caught both findings above.
suggested_fix: Add a test that opens the L1 group and calls cf_units.Unit(v.units) for every variable with a units attr, asserting no parse error; and/or synthesize a config with a magn channel to exercise the MAG spec.
verification: Verified by reading the cited code and running cf_units directly. (1) test_cf_compliance (tests/test_convert.py:149-177) asserts only Conventions=="CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3", presence of title/history, time standard_name/axis/units-substring, and PRES.standard_name. It never calls cf_units/UDUNITS on any units attr and never references MAG or TURB. (2) No UDUNITS validation of the L1 converter exists anywhere in tests/: the only cf_units use (test_perturb_pipeline.py:1839 test_cf_units_converted) is a time-epoch-seconds conversion test; test_perturb_cf_compliance.py runs IOOS/cf compliance on the perturb COMBO output (not p_to_L1) and only checks T/lat/lon units. Other test_convert.py tests hard-compare a couple unit strings ("s-1", "degrees_Celsius m-1") but never validate against UDUNITS. (3) The underlying defects are real: running cf_units.Unit() on convert.py's emitted strings, 'micro_Tesla' (convert.py:360) and 'FTU' (convert.py:383) both FAIL parsing (ValueError UT_UNKNOWN), while every other emitted unit parses; there is also an internal inconsistency since convert_magn (channels.py:212) returns 'uT' (which parses) yet the MAG variable hard-codes 'micro_Tesla'. (4) MAG/TURB are wholly untested: the SN479_0006.p sample has no magn or turbidity channel (confirmed via PFile: types include shear/therm/voltage/etc. but no magn), so those variables are never even emitted under the suite. The proposed fix (loop cf_units.Unit(v.units) over emitted vars + synthesize a magn config) would catch both. Minor caveat: cf_units is installed in this env (3.3.1) but is not a declared dependency in pyproject.toml, so a fix should add it as a dev dep or use importorskip — an implementation detail that does not refute the finding. Severity minor is correct: it is a test/coverage gap, not itself a runtime defect.

title: make_combo schema overwrite downgrades correct units='1' to empty-string units on epsilonLnSigma/FM/fom diagnostics
evidence: epsilon_combine.mk_epsilon_mean writes epsilonLnSigma with units="1" (epsilon_combine.py:174-186), the CF-correct spelling for a dimensionless quantity. But COMBO_SCHEMA gives epsilonLnSigma units="" (netcdf_schema.py:235), and apply_schema unconditionally overwrites variable attrs from the schema (netcdf_schema.py:470-472), so the combined product silently changes the unit from "1" to "". The same empty-string units appear on FM_1/FM_2 and fom_T_1/fom_T_2 (netcdf_schema.py:240,245,319,324), inconsistent with every other dimensionless var in the package (mad, fom, K_max_ratio, Gamma all use "1"). Empty-string units are tolerated by CF as dimensionless but trigger compliance-checker warnings and are internally inconsistent.
suggested_fix: Replace the five units="" entries in COMBO_SCHEMA/CHI_SCHEMA with units="1" for consistency with the rest of the schema and the per-profile combine output.
verification:
Verified against the code. The outcome the finding describes is real and the proposed fix is valid; one part of the stated mechanism is inaccurate but does not change the conclusion.

CONFIRMED FACTS:
- COMBO_SCHEMA/CHI_SCHEMA assign units="" to five dimensionless diagnostics: epsilonLnSigma (netcdf_schema.py:235), FM_1 (:240), FM_2 (:245), fom_T_1 (:319), fom_T_2 (:324).
- apply_schema unconditionally writes every schema attr (except nc_name) onto the variable: netcdf_schema.py:470-472. It is invoked in make_combo at combo.py:139, so the combined product carries units="" on these vars.
- The rest of the package uses the CF-canonical "1" for dimensionless quantities: upstream epsilonLnSigma (processing/epsilon_combine.py:179), chi sigma (processing/chi_combine.py:198), FM (rsi/dissipation.py:472), K_max_ratio (rsi/dissipation.py:487), Turbidity (netcdf_schema.py:122), Gamma (netcdf_schema.py:359). The only other units="" in src is on a QC flag bitfield (perturb/qc_rules.py:368), which is a legitimately different case (CF flag variables carry flag_masks/flag_meanings, not a dimensionless "1"), so it does not undermine the internal-inconsistency argument.

INACCURATE PART OF THE FINDING (does not change verdict): The finding frames this as apply_schema "downgrading correct units='1' to empty-string," implying the binned input already has units="1" that gets overwritten. In fact the binning steps strip ALL per-variable attributes: the depth path's _load_one_profile reads only raw numeric arrays into `vars` (binning.py:272) and carries scalar_attrs only for lat/lon/stime/etime with units explicitly stripped (binning.py:242-246); the time path bin_by_time builds the output Dataset with no per-variable attrs (binning.py:583, 610-613). So on the binned intermediate these data vars have NO units, and apply_schema is the SOLE source of units on the combo, not a downgrader. The end state the finding cares about (combo has units="" on these five vars, inconsistent with the package-wide "1") is nonetheless exactly correct.

SEVERITY: Cosmetic CF-metadata inconsistency. Empty-string units are tolerated by CF/UDUNITS as dimensionless; this is a compliance-checker warning and an internal-consistency wart, not a data-correctness defect. nit is the right severity. The proposed one-line-per-entry fix (set units="1") is appropriate and low-risk.
